item.title: "An MD on the trading floor has been making our lives a nightmare, but his sponsor loves him"
item.description:
I read an article here a few weeks ago about an idiot </news/there-is-an-idiot-on-my-macro-trading-floor>making someone's life a misery on a trading floor. Regrettably I am in a similar situation, except my situation is worse because the idiot I am dealing with is a managing director (MD).
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
He's a relatively recent hire and has made our lives a nightmare ever since he arrived. A lot of the team have left because of him, but the bank seems blind to his failings. This is partly because he has been making money and has an edge but also because he's got a senior sponsor who blindly backs him. Whenever we've complained, this sponsor intervenes and nothing goes any further. Everything is dismissed.
It's frustrating. He's a very divisive character and the bank has lost a lot of good people by keeping him. He's a specialist in managing up, but treats the rest of us terribly.
This is noticeable in pay. Plenty of people who generated good profits were given zero bonuses last year, while he seemed to keep the money for himself. It's not the sort of thing you expect to happen in a major bank, but it's happened in my team. He's created a clique of favourites and if you're not part of that you won't get paid, even if you've generated good pnl.
It's depressing. Most people have left for funds. People have choices. The bank needs to recognise that.
Leo Danzi is a pseudonym

item.title: Optiver's hiring spree includes a head of AI from Apple and a JPMorgan trader
item.description:
Electronic trading firms </news/high-frequency-trading-hiring-and-pay> are known for their ability to develop homegrown talent </news/this-is-what-it-takes-to-get-a-64k-jane-street-internship>, but Amsterdam-based trading giant Optiver </news/optiver-interns-need-to-pass-an-88-question-theory-exam-claude-outperforms-most-of-them>has been hiring a lot at senior level as of late. Arrivals at Optiver this month include traders from banking and AI specialists from Big Tech.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
In New York, where Optiver has been expanding over the past year, Noyan Tokgozoglu joined as the firm's global head of AI. He spent the last seven and a half years at Apple, where he was the head of its foundation modelling and research platform within services engineering. His work included work on AI agents and burgeoning subsets of machine learning like reinforcement learning.
It's not clear whether Tokgozoglu's role will also encompass Optiver's AI lab, </news/optiver-ai-lab> which is an independent business building out across China </news/optiver-s-top-options-trader-in-china-left-for-rival-electronic-trading-firm> and New York. Job descriptions in the lab said that Optiver is looking for people with "Big Tech rigor," so he would presumably fit right in.
In London, meanwhile, Bertrand Marchal joined Optiver as its head of exotics. He was most recently JPMorgan's EMEA head of index exotics, but he also worked as HSBC's Americas head of equity derivatives trading.
Other senior hires at Optiver already have experience in the electronic trading space. Nate Clark joined in New York in an undisclosed technology role; he was most recently an equity trader at Old Mission Capital, but also previously worked as head of client technology at publicly traded HFT firm Virtu Financial.
Optiver is also building out its ETF sales trading team with experienced electronic trading staff. Brett Sussman of Flow Traders and Caitlin Krantz, another Old Mission alum, were both previously reported <https://www.thetradenews.com/optiver-enhances-us-institutional-etf-trading-desk-with-two-new-hires/>to be joining the firm in New York.
The people and firms associated with this article all did not respond to a request for comment.

item.title: MDs are being let go by banks. They just want to earn $1.5m again
item.description:
2026 is turning to out to be a funny year for jobs in banks. On one hand, revenues are rising by double digits in percentage terms. On the other hand, banks are cutting costs. Middle and back office </news/front-office-middle-office-back-office-banking> staff are at the sharp end of this cost-cutting, but managing directors (MDs) in client-facing roles are not immune.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Matthias Schwarz, the former head of EMEA credit trading at Bank of America in London, says banks are increasingly jettisoning markets MDs in their 40s. They are particularly jettisoning markets MDs in sales roles, he says.
This is because sales revenue accountability is less transparent than in trading. “If a trader makes $20m, banks can’t simply replace him or her with a VP, but if you have an MD that covers a major client there will typically be hundreds of other touchpoints so that in the short to medium term at least you can make do with a more junior VP," Schwarz says. "Sales relationships are much more institutionalised.”
Sales MDs are therefore spat out more often than others. But when any MD is let go, Schwarz says they must confront a harsh financial reality. Their removal has often been preceded by years of dwindling compensation, and they are unlikely to recover their former earning potential again.
"There are MDs whose total compensation has gone from $1.5m to $1.2m to $950k," says Schwarz. "They always want to get back to $1.5m but unfortunately they are not worth that any more, and they won't get that in a third or fourth tier bank."
No one wants to hear this, but Schwarz isn't alone in delivering bad news. Alan Johnson, founder of Wall Street pay consultancy Johnson Associates, says banks are giving up on their tactic of 'starving out' poor performers with years of low bonuses in the hope that they'll simply leave voluntarily. Starving people out takes too long, says Johnson, who's forecasting 10-15% staff cuts in front office jobs over the next two to three years. People are being forcibly removed.
Schwarz says MDs are the natural choice for the cuts, and not just because of their high salaries. </news/md-pay-investment-banks>Banks have overpromoted and now have too many people at the top of their pyramids, says Schwarz. “Ten year ago, there were fewer restrictions on MD promotions and a lot of people made MD. This was followed by the diversity push, when they promoted people for diversity reasons but didn’t reduce the overall number of promotions. Now, they are cutting costs and these MDs are the best place to start.”
None of this is good news if you lose your MD job. But it's best to know the truth than to fantasise about the $1.5m. "People need to be realistic about their options," Schwarz warns.

item.title: UBS private banker in Singapore performed an athletic manoeuvre to celebrate leaving
item.description:
What’s the happiest you’ve been to leave a job? Have you ever leapt, for joy or misery or otherwise?
Click here to join the bubble by eFinancialCareers, our new anonymous community. <https://bubble.efinancialcareers.com/login?_gl=1*ut8n6u*_gcl_au*MTg4MDY2NTExNi4xNzYxNTU3NDIz*_ga*ODczNzI2NTA0LjE3NTM2OTk2ODI.*_ga_NZFQV4MXPM*czE3NjM2NDM0MDYkbzI1NiRnMSR0MTc2MzY0MzQzOSRqMjckbDAkaDMyMjc4OTUzMw..>✍️
Michael Sutter left UBS after a long, long time at the firm. The Swiss private banker joined the Swiss bank all the way back in 2000, and spent his entire time there with only a brief 10-month interlude at BNP Paribas in 2018.
In a LinkedIn post <https://www.linkedin.com/feed/update/urn:li:activity:7472029363627196416/> celebrating his departure, Sutter posted a strange video jumping over the entrance turnstiles to a UBS office, presumably in Singapore, where he last worked for the firm, before the camera zooms in on his face to turn into a generic corporate headshot.
“In an age shaped by AI, no barrier (and no turnstile) is too high if you are willing to challenge yourself and follow what feels right,” Sutter said. “I recently made the decision to take a bold leap of faith.”
It isn’t clear where Sutter is going next – he did not respond to a request for comment on this article. His professional circle, however, is very supportive: “What a glorious jump,” one person said. “Impressive first jump and step,” said fellow UBS executive director and 28-year veteran of the firm Pietro Zarrilli. “The video was seriously well done,” said another UBS colleague.
The cause for Sutter’s departure from UBS isn’t very clear either. The bank recently reshuffled its senior leadership in Southeast Asia, Hubbis reported <https://www.hubbis.com/news/ubs-reshuffles-southeast-asia-wealth-team-with-senior-singapore-appointments>.
Michael Sutter did not respond to a request for comment.

item.title: BNP Paribas's top trading professionals are moving on
item.description:
There have been some changes at BNP Paribas. Some people have left. Some people have moved around.
The leavers include Jaafer Amrani, BNP's head of liquidity strategy for EMEA equities. They are also understood to include Adrian Hudd, BNP's global head of G10 FX swaps.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Amrani didn't respond to a request to comment for this article, but is thought to be moving to a new role elsewhere. Hudd also didn't respond to a request to comment; the FCA Register shows him leaving the French bank at the end of last month.
BNP Paribas didn't respond to a request to comment either. Amrani joined BNP via Exane in 2023, and is the latest ex-Exane </news/bnp-paribas-cuts> figure to exit. As head of liquidity strategy for EMEA equities, he managed market structure, internal and external liquidity and represented the firm with regulators. Hudd joined from Credit Agricole in 2007; he spent the first 12 years of his career at BNP in New York and returned to London as global head of G10 FX swaps in 2020.
Amrani and Hudd are not BNP's only moves. We understand that senior people are also moving internally. Joe Bonnaud, BNP's deputy head of global markets Americas is thought to be returning to London after nearly four years. Arno De Tregomain, the current COO for global markets at BNP in London, is thought to be going to New York in his place.

item.title: "Quant researchers spend all their time coding while junior traders do real quant work"
item.description:
I started my career as a hedge fund </news/how-to-get-a-hedge-fund-job> quant </news/quant-jobs-finance> in London. </news/best-paying-hedge-funds> I moved into discretionary trading so that I could do more than just write code. </news/programming-languages-for-a-career-in-finance>
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Your experience will vary from firm to firm, but in my last fund, quant researchers </news/what-is-a-quant-researcher-hedge-fund> spent nearly all of their time coding and almost none managing positions. As a quant, you feel like your mandate is not to make money yourself, but to aid in the generation of PnL for your portfolio manager. You have no large exposure to a positive PnL and, while you have no direct exposure to a drawdown either, you may be in danger if your pod gets shut down. You also have to try and explain complex mathematical problems to people who don't even understand first level undergraduate mathematics.
At the same time, the junior traders being hired are becoming increasingly quant-like; they now need to understand a lot of the mathematics and statistics, plus they need to code at a much better standard than junior traders in the past, who only needed to pass Python 101.
The day to day of a junior trader </news/2016/09/what-do-you-really-do-all-day-as-a-junior-in-a-hedge-fund> is to use code to understand the markets and predict some portion of the future in order to generate PnL... sound familiar?
This is why I have become a trader too. The lines are blurred but traders get many benefits. We're first order observers of the market while quants are second order. We have skin in the game and actively shape the trading sessions at our fund while, as a quant, your work can feel particularly futile if your research isn't going well.
The only real difference between a quant and a junior trader today is how much time is spent coding versus managing positions. Moving from one role to the other, therefore, isn't the jump you might expect.
Phil Hennessey is a pseudonym

item.title: Morning Coffee: Goldman Sachs & Morgan Stanley bankers' strange new shoes. London analyst jobs collapse
item.description:
If you look at Elon Musk's personal X account <https://x.com/elonmusk>, you will see some curious sights. Among the retweets <https://x.com/elonmusk/status/2065702920427896916?s=20>of claims that SpaceX is a company whose mission is 'axiomatically the love of humanity', there are Goldman Sachs <https://x.com/GoldmanSachs/status/2065457326039933168?s=20>and Morgan Stanley <https://x.com/elonmusk/status/2065456782550401072?s=20> bankers wearing green shoes.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
The green shoes are not the same; Goldman bankers' are less garish. Both sets are reportedly inspired by a 'green shoe' clause which the bankers themselves wanted to add to the SpacexIPO. The clause allows for up to 15% more SpaceX shares to be sold over a 30 day period if demand is strong. Musk agreed to add it, but only if the bankers wore actual green shoes.
Immersive outfits are standard for bankers competing for IPOs (David Solomon once wore Lululemon </news/2018/10/outrageous-sales-pitch-since-ubs-yoga-pants-flash-mob>). The groups of Goldman Sachs and Morgan Stanley were each paid $100m <https://x.com/LaurenSHirsch/status/2065388596643700820?s=20>for their lead roles; BofA, Citi and JPMorgan got $75m. Lurid shoes are a small price to pay.
But there is more to it than that. Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-06-12/spacex-strikes-rare-deal-to-pay-0-to-bankers-for-11-billion-greenshoe-option?utm_medium=email&utm_source=author_alert&utm_term=260612&utm_campaign=author_21835441> reports that Musk negotiated that if the green shoe clause is activated and if SpaceX sells an extra $11.25bn of shares, the Goldman Sachs and Morgan Stanley bankers wearing the footwear won't receive any extra fees for their extra work. The additional fee should have been $75m. It comes after Musk already negotiated bankers' main fee on the IPO down to 0.67% versus a standard 4-7% of money raised. The green shoes might therefore be interpreted as a public display of subjugation.
Goldman and Morgan Stanley's bankers probably wouldn't see it like this. The $75bn SpaceX IPO was oversubscribed </news/spacex-ipo-oversubscribed>with more than $100bn <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-06-11/spacex-ipo-said-to-draw-more-than-70-billion-in-retail-orders> of retail orders alone. Bankers with SpaceX stock to allocate are therefore kingmakers and able to ingratiate themselves with clients. The Financial Times <https://www.ft.com/content/a7f4246d-9ae2-4f7b-90af-e5a53c52203b?syn-25a6b1a6=1> reports that investors "cycled through" Goldman's offices on Thursday petitioning for a slice of the SpaceX deal. 70% of the offering reportedly went to long only managers; 20% to retail; hedge funds only got 10% of the book. The extra $11.25bn will therefore banks' opportunity to keep lucrative hedge fund clients happy. Zero fees and garish shoes are probably ok in the circumstances.
Separately, Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-06-14/ai-led-job-losses-bite-for-london-s-coders-lawyers-and-analysts?taid=6a2e8e2b538c43000133ddeb&utm_campaign=trueanthem&utm_content=business&utm_medium=social&utm_source=twitter>reports that analyst jobs in London seem to be vanishing and that where four years ago there were 350 jobs for 'finance analysts' available at any one time, there are now only 80.
AI appears to be the culprit. AI can do analysis. “The demand for great portfolio managers has never been higher,” Sean Sweeney, one recruiter declares. “But the layer of people underneath them is disappearing fast. Firms aren’t building teams around PMs anymore, they’re handing one person an AI tool and calling it a day.”
Meanwhile...
Elon Musk has described Mars as a “fixer-upper of a planet” with “a lot of potential.” Musk wanted the SpaceX IPO to happen last week because Mercury, Venus and Jupiter were in rare alignment in the night sky. (Financial Times) <https://www.ft.com/content/a7f4246d-9ae2-4f7b-90af-e5a53c52203b?syn-25a6b1a6=1>
JPMorgan had a SpaceX party on the 57th floor of its New York headquarters. There were with tomahawk steak bones branded with JPMorgan and SpaceX logos. (WSJ) <https://www.wsj.com/finance/banking/jpmorgan-chase-has-a-key-role-for-spacexs-ipo-party-host-88534f80>
There’s a sharp drop in commercial bank employment that starts in 2026. This is the beginning of back-office downsizing and still has a long way to go. Banks are only just getting started. (Robin Brooks) <https://robinjbrooks.substack.com/p/four-charts-for-sunday-4d9?utm_source=substack&publication_id=4981217&post_id=201918154&utm_medium=email&utm_content=share&utm_campaign=email-share&triggerShare=true&isFreemail=true&r=qrn8&triedRedirect=true>
DRW lost $176m in the first three months of the year and parted company with the head of its US desk for power and natural gas trading. (Financial Times) <https://www.ft.com/content/58558368-a938-4194-ba0d-cb10f3a0915d?syn-25a6b1a6=1>
Susquehanna International Group's sports trading unit lost over $10m after offering to bet on the Knicks losing. They won, despite their chances being only 5%. (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-06-12/knicks-win-triggered-record-loss-for-susquehanna-sports-traders?utm_medium=email&utm_source=author_alert&utm_term=260612&utm_campaign=author_19908303>
Rates trader Luke Ryan left DRW in December 2025. Now he's at BlueCrest. (Financial News) < https://www.fnlondon.com/articles/bluecrest-boosts-rates-trading-team-with-ex-drw-trader-be797b7f?mod=FNMOSTPOPULAR>
Centerview was negotiating a $150m fee to restructure Venezuela's debt. Then Lazard came along offering to do it for $25m. (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-06-14/lazard-offers-to-undercut-rival-investment-bank-for-historic-venezuela-deal?utm_medium=email&utm_source=author_alert&utm_term=260614&utm_campaign=author_21835441>.
Russell Horwitz </news/goldman-sachs-chief-of-staff>, Goldman Sachs' outgoing chief of staff, didn't agree with David Solomon's backing of Kathy Ruemmler. </news/kathy-ruemmler-goldman-sachs> Horwitz says this isn't why he's leaving. (FT) <https://www.ft.com/content/468f48df-c802-4f16-972c-8b2caa1d2802?syn-25a6b1a6=1>
Chirayu Rana has got a new lawyer. He is saying: "We intend to vindicate every one of Mr. Rana's rights and hold JPMorgan and Ms. Hajdini fully accountable for the catastrophic harm they caused. The story the public thinks it knows is about to change dramatically. Stay tuned." (MorningStar) <https://www.morningstar.com/news/pr-newswire/20260608ny78108/joseph-norinsberg-llc-to-represent-former-jpmorgan-executive-chirayu-rana-in-racial-discrimination-harassment-and-retaliation-case>
Now HSBC has $400m of exposure to IFFCO Group, a consumer credit company in the Middle East that might be going insolvent after trying to restructure $2bn of debt. (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-06-12/hsbc-said-to-have-400-million-exposure-to-ailing-uae-firm-iffco?utm_medium=email&utm_source=author_alert&utm_term=260612&utm_campaign=author_20887312>
KPMG issued a report saying that UBS “integrates AI agents across investment advisory, risk management and compliance monitoring”. UBS said this was incorrect. (Financial Times) <https://www.ft.com/content/b3828e92-4961-4b39-84f0-c42f33be3c3f?syn-25a6b1a6=1>
Simon Dove was MD and head of liquidity at Instinet. Now he's left. (The Trade News) <https://www.thetradenews.com/instinets-dove-departs-firm-for-new-chapter/>
SocGen expanded into cash prime brokerage. Good luck with that. (Financial News) <https://www.fnlondon.com/articles/socgen-enters-cash-prime-brokerage-in-hedge-fund-push-4e21974e?mod=topic_news>
Goldman Sachs and Morgan Stanley bankers can request to work from home on match days during the world cup. (FT) <https://www.ft.com/content/d2afe6bb-9c02-4893-a246-b63d6ae724f6?syn-25a6b1a6=1>
Apollo Global's second biggest US office might be in Austin. (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-06-12/apollo-leaning-toward-austin-as-city-for-second-us-headquarters?srnd=phx-financ>
Solo participants in the International Quant Championship, hosted by hedge fund WorldQuant, were 150,600 this year, an increase of nearly 100%. Blame AI. (Financial News) <https://www.fnlondon.com/articles/ai-drives-doubling-of-entrants-to-global-quant-contest-5a27a6c5?mod=topic_news>
Citadel put $500m into New York-based Toms Capital run by Benjamin Pass. (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-06-12/citadel-backs-hedge-fund-toms-with-500-million-in-rare-move?utm_medium=email&utm_source=author_alert&utm_term=260612&utm_campaign=author_19458291>
What developers do now: "LLMs are superb at spitting out small chunks of reasonably good code if asked to do a reasonably specific thing. This was what junior developers used to do. My day to day focus is now on “architectural” decisions (I.e. how do we best assemble all of those small chunks of code to do something complicated?)" (Financial Times) <https://www.ft.com/content/e9dfe09e-d0e2-4baf-bd33-37f02a8c1f4a?syn-25a6b1a6=1>
Beta mothers let their kids explore on bikes and achieve only moderately good grades. Anything to get them away from screens. (WSJ) <https://www.wsj.com/lifestyle/relationships/beta-moms-influencers-tiktok-6cf99674>

item.title: The Coffee Run: Is Goldman Sachs really drinking the SpaceX Kool-Aid?
item.description:
We've launched a new weekly video series <https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PLqJc_c5JT66B3Cp_26xsN8Xsv2EtvtEQQ> to accompany Morning Coffee, in which we look at one story in more depth to give you the full picture.
The SpaceX IPO is finally here and it went... pretty well. The current price (as of ~1:30 New York time) is $174, up 28.8%.
It has been a long road to reach this point for the bankers involved in orchestrating the initial public offering, and there's a long way to go until Goldman Sachs, which had lead left position, can call the IPO an unequivocal success. Goldman and the other banks had pretty wild predictions for SpaceX's future revenue potential, and if the market starts to doubt those claims, SpaceX investors could be in for a rude awakening.
Watch the video below to see how Goldman "won" top position on the IPO, and the implications if SpaceX's stock price either continues its solid momentum or falters before lockup periods expire.

item.title: Famous 23-year-old finance professional seeks wife
item.description:
It's been three months and Demarre Johnson is still famous. In early March, he appeared in the infamous photoshoot, </news/goldman-sachs-junior-bankers-magazine-shoot> 'Meet the finest boys in finance', where he declared that he owned six vests and spent 55 hours a week working for PWC helping clients implement AI. One week later, it became apparent that Johnson wasn't actually working for PWC anymore </news/demarre-johnson-finest-boys-in-finance>, but he has not let that stop him.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
"I left PWC prior to the photoshoot," he tells us. "I left in February. I am working full time for a hedge fund." He does not say which hedge fund.
Johnson says PWC was cool with him appearing in the photoshoot and that his departure was unrelated. "I think corporate is going out of style," he says. "It's good for learning things but you don't want to get pigeon holed."
The photoshoot went viral and Johnson became sort of famous. "I blame social media," he declares. "I became a name," he adds. "Random finance bros would stop me in the street, guys with Patagonia vests and duffel bags. I couldn't go anywhere without being stopped."
This sounds annoying, but Johnson is not perturbed. Johnson grew up in Dallas and he wants to convert his newfound fame into a career as a finance influencer for people with his background. He already features in adverts <https://www.instagram.com/reel/DZVtUnZPCeZ/>for a wealth management app. He has an agent. "I was on the winning end of the stick."
However, he's had to close his DMs. "I've been letting the dust settle," he says. "There was a girl in my DMs who was like 'Are you looking for a finance girl?'" That particular girl only wanted someone with the "right assets," but Johnson remains hopeful. "Hopefully I will find my wife from this," he tells us.

item.title: Standard Chartered has had a good time hiring six electronic FX people from UBS
item.description:
People who have worked together in the past like to work together in the future. Look at RBC, where Citi's ex-credit traders are congregating. </news/citi-credit-rbc>
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Citi's credit defectors are not the only cluster of former pals in the London market. It's come to our attention that Standard Chartered has got a cluster too. Standard Chartered has hired at least six electronic FX people from UBS.
The Standard Chartered recruits may have something to do with John Newman who joined from UBS last August as the London based global head of FX and rates trading. Two months later, Chris Aspinall joined from UBS in New York as head of eFX trading for the Americas. Then Robin Hutchings from UBS joined in January as Standard Chartered's global head of quantitative development (having previously been head of eFX quant dev at UBS). Two members of Hutching's team (Nikolaos Katsanos, Charles Exley) appeared at the same time.
That might have been the end of it, except that John Luke, an eFX quant developer from UBS also joined Standard Chartered in May.
There are unconfirmed suggestions that UBS is a bit peeved by all the hiring. UBS declined to comment. So too did Standard Chartered.

item.title: Top US MBA graduates are still earning $175k in finance. Sometimes less, rarely more
item.description:
If you want to start a career in finance </news/how-to-get-a-job-in-banking>, one of the most tried and tested ways is to get a top MBA </news/best-mba-for-finance-banking>. They can be expensive – up to $270k at some schools <https://poetsandquants.com/2025/08/27/the-mba-price-tag-21-of-the-top-25-u-s-b-schools-now-charge-100k-a-year-or-more/> – but the upside is that you get to enter start a career in a high-paying industry with a high-paying role. Right. Right?
Click here to join the bubble by eFinancialCareers, our new anonymous community. <https://bubble.efinancialcareers.com/login?_gl=1*ut8n6u*_gcl_au*MTg4MDY2NTExNi4xNzYxNTU3NDIz*_ga*ODczNzI2NTA0LjE3NTM2OTk2ODI.*_ga_NZFQV4MXPM*czE3NjM2NDM0MDYkbzI1NiRnMSR0MTc2MzY0MzQzOSRqMjckbDAkaDMyMjc4OTUzMw..>✍️
Data compiled from the MBA employment reports of the top thirty schools in the USA – according to poetsandquants.com <https://poetsandquants.com/2026/05/31/high-low-mba-salaries-bonuses-at-the-top-100-u-s-b-schools/> – shows that the average class of 2025 MBA graduate that works in finance earns a median salary of $175k.
That not bad, but it’s not exactly jaw-dropping. Salaries for MBA graduates in finance have been static for years now – three years ago, when we analysed starting salaries for Harvard’s 2022 MBA graduates </news/finance/heres-much-mbas-earn-banking-private-equity-consulting-hedge-funds>, the starting salary for graduates working in financial services was… still $175k. At one point, one Harvard MBA graduate from 2021 </news/2022/01/mba-pay-private-equity> who went into private equity earned $650k. The closest from 2025's class was someone who earned $470k.
US inflation since then (assuming a June graduation date) means that salaries should be around $190k now. The fact that they are still $175k indicates, effectively, an 8% pay-cut in purchasing power terms. US house prices alone have increased by around <https://www.fhfa.gov/reports/house-price-index/2025/2?utm_source=chatgpt.com> 20% to 25%.
There were a few exceptions to the $175k regularity. On the higher end, Stanford Business School MBA class of 2025 graduates earned $200k on average. On the lower end, nine schools’ MBA graduates earned below $175k – all the way down to the Foster School of Business at the University of Washington, whose class of 2025 working in finance earned just $119k on average.
Additional bonuses varied, and data was patchy. The highest signing bonuses were for graduates from the Stern School of Business NYU, who received median bonuses of $56k. Only Harvard disclosed first year bonuses for finance professionals – a very tidy $119k.

item.title: This macro trader also left Nomura but it's ok
item.description:
As we noted </news/nomura-paid-its-bonuses-and-now-some-traders-are-leaving> earlier this week, some macro traders </news/it-s-been-a-dark-year-for-macro-traders-but-not-for-macro-trading-jobs>have been leaving Nomura after the Japanese bank paid bonuses </news/nomura-bonuses>at the end of last month.
It transpires that we left one senior trader off the list of departures. We understand that Bevan Harris, an executive director in interest rate derivative trading who had been at Nomura for 13 years has also gone. Harris is understood to have resigned two weeks ago. His destination is unknown.
Nomura declined to comment on Harris's exit. He's understood to have been an AUD rates trader at the bank. Nomura also recently lost Christopher Killoran, another AUD rates trader in Sydney.
These exits are manageable, though, because Nomura has also made many hires. Most notably, it hired Raghav Joshi, </news/nomura-made-another-big-hire-for-its-singapore-macro-team> a senior G10 FX trader joined in Singapore from Santander in April as head of AUD and NZD rates trading. Joshi previously worked at ANZ with Anshu Sidher and Herr-Ling Yeo </news/nomura-is-thought-to-be-hiring-anz-s-senior-escapees-in-singapore> who both joined in recent months as Nomura's co-head of global FX and emerging markets and head of FX flow trading respectively.
Harris did not formerly work for ANZ.

item.title: Hedge fund Balyasny’s intern class is very good, and not very interesting
item.description:
When hedge funds hire students </news/hedge-funds-with-graduate-training-programmes>, they tend to have their pick of the litter, and the pick of the litter tends to pick them in return. At Chicago-based hedge fund </news/how-to-get-a-hedge-fund-job> Balyasny Asset Management, for example, where the 2026 intern class has just started <https://www.instagram.com/p/DZaiiHhH7At/?img_index=1> – the firm has a type.
Click here to join the bubble by eFinancialCareers, our new anonymous community. <https://bubble.efinancialcareers.com/login?_gl=1*ut8n6u*_gcl_au*MTg4MDY2NTExNi4xNzYxNTU3NDIz*_ga*ODczNzI2NTA0LjE3NTM2OTk2ODI.*_ga_NZFQV4MXPM*czE3NjM2NDM0MDYkbzI1NiRnMSR0MTc2MzY0MzQzOSRqMjckbDAkaDMyMjc4OTUzMw..>✍️
Public LinkedIn profiles of approximately 30 Balyasny 2026 summer interns, suggest that BAM likes to hire from Ivy League schools. 40% of interns we spotted went to an Ivy League school. Pennsylvania (including Wharton) is the most popular single source of Balyasny interns, followed closely by Brown. Brown especially is a surprise, providing a number of quant-y students despite its lack of association with hard finance.
In the UK, BAM interns seem to be more represented by Cambridge University than Oxford, despite Oxford’s Alpha Fund (its student investment society) being sponsored by the firm.
Only around 15% of the interns that we saw on LinkedIn are based in the UK, compared to 85% in the USA. That roughly matches what Balyasny disclosed last year </news/where-do-portfolio-managers-work-balyasny> – of its 176 portfolio management teams, 17% are based in London, and 63% are based in the USA. The only major region which we didn’t note interns in was APAC, where the firm has 14% of its portfolio management teams.
More than half of the interns we saw are STEM students - the closest among Balyasny interns is a political science student from Duke with an economics “concentration”, but most non-STEM students are studying economics, anyway.
We didn’t see every single Balyasny intern, of course. There were around 80 in total last summer </news/balyasny-internship>, of which 40 converted their internship into a job at the firm, out of 16,000 applicants in total. That conversion rate – of 0.5/0.25% – was even more brutal than Goldman Sachs </news/goldman-sachs-internship-acceptance-rate> or JPMorgan’s </news/bank-acceptance-rate> 0.7%.
Balyasny Asset Management declined a request for comment on this article.

item.title: "My friend applied for a banking job she was perfect for and was rejected 7 minutes later"
item.description:
My friend applied for an executive assistant role at an Asset Management firm this morning. She submitted her CV and supporting documents at 09.22.
At 09.29, the reply arrived.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
“After careful consideration, we regret to inform you…
Seven minutes. That was the careful consideration.
She ticked every box. Relevant experience, immediately available, happy with the location. Not a borderline application, exactly what they asked for.
Somewhere between her hitting send and making a cup of tea, an algorithm had already decided. But the company still chose to dress it up in the language of human judgement. Careful consideration. The phrase does real damage when it’s a lie — it doesn’t just reject a candidate, it mocks the effort she put into applying.
This isn’t a one-off. It’s standard practice. Companies automate the door but keep the welcome mat out. And job seekers, already in a vulnerable position, are left wondering what they did wrong. The answer is that they did nothing wrong. It's just that nobody looked.
Seven minutes of careful consideration. Remember that next time you read the small print on your own rejection.

item.title: Morgan Stanley's top Singapore rates trader appears to have left after making MD
item.description:
Where is Sahil Aggarwal, a recently promoted managing director (MD) in rates trading at Morgan Stanley in Singapore? Seemingly not on the desk any more.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Morgan Stanley isn't commenting and Aggarwal didn't respond to a request to comment, but we understand that he is leaving the bank, possibly for a hedge fund.
Aggarwal's apparent exit is thought to have come as a surprise given that he was only promoted to managing director (MD) </news/morgan-stanley-s-2026-md-list-ai-experts-ex-hedge-fund-traders>in January 2026. He is thought to be one of Morgan Stanley's top rates traders in Asia.
Singapore's market for macro trading talent is hot. Last month, one local headhunter informed us </news/singapore-macro-hiring> that hiring there is going "full throttle," that teams are lean and that people are getting bid back by banks when they attempt to leave.
Funds hiring macro portfolio managers in Singapore include Balyasny, Dymon Capital Asia and Jain Global.
Aggarwal joined Morgan Stanley in 2020 and previously worked for Citi and Goldman Sachs.

item.title: Morning Coffee: Goldman Sachs is sensitive about this interview process. How to make huge money from hedge funds redux
item.description:
If you've spent six years working for Goldman Sachs, have been a member of the Goldman management committee </news/kathy-ruemmler-goldman-sachs> and are a favourite </news/kathryn-ruemmler-goldman-sachs-epstein> of CEO David Solomon </news/david-solomon-goldman-sachs>, then when you leave maybe it's inevitable that you will be drafted in to interview your replacement. But what if you left following a furore over your appearance in the Epstein report, wherein you were seen both to have called Jeffrey Epstein "a sweetie" and to have received various Jeffrey gifts including but not limited to a $9.4k Hermès bag (before you worked for Goldman)? In this situation, would interviewing your successor still be a thing?
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Goldman Sachs seems to think that, yes, it absolutely would. But Goldman thinks Ruemmler has done nothing wrong. Both the firm and Ruemmler herself have always denied that she committed any transgressions in relation to Epstein, and Ruemmler - who was Goldman's top lawyer - has said she regretted ever knowing him. Epstein was a "monster," says Ruemmler. Solomon has long held the line that Ruemmler was "an excellent general counsel and we benefit from her advice every day.” The historic relation with Epstein was a oversight in an exemplary career.
All of which means that if you feel like being the next general counsel at Goldman Sachs when Ruemmler leaves on June 30, you may encounter Ruemmler during your interview process. The Financial Times <https://www.ft.com/content/25a6b16b-5f3e-45e8-aeda-33f9f21845ed?syn-25a6b1a6=1> reports that Ruemmler has been involved in hiring her replacement and is interviewing candidates. Goldman Sachs confirms this and told the FT: “Of course the current general counsel is involved in interviewing the candidates for our next general counsel...No one knows the requirements of this job better than Kathy. But it’s absurd to imply that Kathy is choosing her successor.”
When Ruemmler's successor is chosen, he or she will also have Ruemmler in the background as an advisor. Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-06-05/goldman-ceo-asks-top-lawyer-to-stay-at-firm-after-epstein-furor> reported last week that Solomon had pressed Ruemmler to remain with the firm. Maybe this too is perfectly normal. At the very least, it might be an indication that when you interview at Goldman Sachs, you need to know everything about the person who occupied the role before you. Goldman Sachs declined to comment.
Separately, you don't have to work for a hedge fund </news/how-to-get-a-hedge-fund-job> to make money from hedge funds. Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-06-11/abu-dhabi-s-adic-plans-15-billion-leveraged-hedge-fund-bet?utm_medium=email&utm_source=author_alert&utm_term=260611&utm_campaign=author_19458291> reports that Abu Dhabi Investment Council, which is a unit of Mubadala Investment Company, which is another sovereign wealth fund, is contemplating $15bn of bets on multistrategy hedge funds </news/multistrategy-hedge-funds-hiring> using total return swaps.
Total return swaps are highly leveraged derivatives which enable investors to get all the returns from an asset without actually owning it. This sounds great. Except that they also expose investors to all the losses from the asset without actually owning it. And leverage means the losses can be large.
Total return swaps have caused problems in the past. Archegos Capita <https://www.ft.com/content/fb364689-9b04-47cb-aba9-5eb15d1cea85?syn-25a6b1a6=1>l had tens of billions of total return swaps and when its investments unexpectedly tanked <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/features/2021-04-08/how-bill-hwang-of-archegos-capital-lost-20-billion-in-two-days>in March 2021, it did not end well, either for the fund or for Credit Suisse. The chief investment officer for hedge funds at ADIC is saying: "We continue to see value in strategies that can provide diversification, downside protection, and attractive risk-adjusted returns across market cycles.”
Meanwhile...
“The core lesson that everyone has learned from this experience is never bet against Elon – it’s just a bad idea." (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-06-11/founders-fund-andreessen-set-for-record-returns-from-spacex-ipo?cmpid=061226_morningemea&utm_campaign=morningemea&utm_medium=email&utm_source=newsletter&utm_term=260612&utm_content=5464>
SpaceX cafeteria workers are about to become millionaires. It is too late for you but here is a job there anyway. (Spacex) <https://job-boards.greenhouse.io/spacex/jobs/8529777002>
Now that McKinsey & Co. is using AI for its presentations, it's realising how annoying PowerPoint was. In the old days, multiple versions of a presentation would circulate and people would edit the wrong one. (Business Insider) <https://www.businessinsider.com/mckinsey-consultant-ai-powerpoint-reliance-2026-6>
Jinger Zhao, a former Two Sigma Investments managing director, once authored a paper on gender differences in marathon running, and found that men overestimated their abilities compared to women. Now she's possibly being given $300m from Blackstone to set up an absolute return platform called z Star Research. (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-06-11/blackstone-to-back-two-sigma-veteran-s-quant-hedge-fund-startup?utm_medium=email&utm_source=author_alert&utm_term=260611&utm_campaign=author_19458291>
JPMorgan hired 350 people in the EU last year. (Financial News) <https://www.fnlondon.com/articles/jpmorgan-bolsters-eu-headcount-by-350-people-f3d2e6ae>
Healthcare jobs are regulated and so not susceptible to AI, says Apollo. (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-06-11/apollo-is-screening-all-software-investments-for-ai-threat-risk>
Work for Citadel, live in Ken Griffin's housing. He is building 300 apartments and a 1,400 parking space garage in Miami. (WSJ) <https://www.wsj.com/real-estate/ken-griffin-ramps-up-his-miami-real-estate-play-76a126f9?mod=hp_featst_pos3>

item.title: Citadel's risk data engineering head in London joined OpenAI to work on agents
item.description:
The flow of talent from hedge funds </news/how-to-get-a-hedge-fund-job> and trading firms into AI labs </news/the-difference-between-working-as-a-quant-in-finance-and-as-a-researcher-in-an-ai-lab> is not exclusively reserved for interns and new grads </news/2025-quant-interns-are-earning-5k-per-week-at-citadel-d-e-shaw-and-point72>. Senior technologists are also in demand. In London, OpenAI has hired a senior data engineer from Citadel as one of its coveted members of technical staff (MTCs). </news/top-paying-ai-jobs>
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Tejas Sharma joined OpenAI </news/openai-culture> as an MTC working on agentic infrastructure. He spent just under three years at Citadel, where he was its London-based head of data risk engineering. Before Citadel, Sharma worked across multiple tech firms; he spent five years as a staff engineer at Meta working on Instagram's machine learning architecture, and six years at financial data firm FactSet, latterly as a manager in its core services infrastructure team.
Sharma's not the first Citadel engineer to show up at a major AI lab. Gautham Thambidorai, a former MD in Citadel's core engineering team, is currently a VP at Google Deepmind. Yaqi Ji, formerly a quantitative research engineer in Citadel's global quantiative strategies group, joined OpenAI's post training inference team while serving a two-year non compete clause at Citadel. AI labs are a viable opportunity for hedge fund staff on gardening leave, but it's understood that the option to join an AI lab is not guaranteed and that it is treated as a competitor for certain roles.
OpenAI is growing fast in London. While Companies House accounts for its UK entity show that it had 55 employees at the end of 2024, LinkedIn data suggests it has more than 200 today including more than 40 members of technical staff. There are 38 more roles currently advertised in London, so hedge fund technologists looking to follow Sharma might have plenty of opportunity to do so.

item.title: Bank of America's DiCaprio left for Morgan Stanley
item.description:
In a time where mega-IPOs such as that of SpaceX </news/spacex-ipo-bankers>, OpenAI, and Anthropic </news/jpmorgan-ai-token-spending> are dominating the airwaves, the private markets that produced them might be overlooked by the public. Banks, however, are quietly doubling down on their hiring
Click here to join the bubble by eFinancialCareers, our new anonymous community. <https://bubble.efinancialcareers.com/login?_gl=1*ut8n6u*_gcl_au*MTg4MDY2NTExNi4xNzYxNTU3NDIz*_ga*ODczNzI2NTA0LjE3NTM2OTk2ODI.*_ga_NZFQV4MXPM*czE3NjM2NDM0MDYkbzI1NiRnMSR0MTc2MzY0MzQzOSRqMjckbDAkaDMyMjc4OTUzMw..>✍️
Brian DiCaprio joined Morgan Stanley in New York this month as a managing director in its alternative capital solutions team, specialising in private placements. He joined Morgan Stanley from Bank of America, where he spent 5 years, and was made an MD in its 2024 round of promotions. </news/bank-of-america-mds>
DiCaprio’s team in private placements works with venture capital and growth equity firms, among others. An older job placement within the team described it as providing “innovative structuring, market insight, and execution expertise” for private fundraising, such as valuations during a funding round.
Private placement bankers are in high demand. Back in April, Lazard announced <https://www.lazard.com/news-announcements/lazard-to-acquire-campbell-lutyens-creating-the-global-leader-in-private-capital-advisory/> its $575m acquisition of leading private markets advisor Campbell Lyutens. Lazard CEO Peter Orszag noted that private capital advisory “has been growing rapidly”.
Moving from Bank of America to Morgan Stanley is a practical choice. LSEG, formerly Refinitiv, noted that the firms swapped rankings in the investment banking fees league table between Q1 of 2025 and Q1 of 2026, due to Morgan Stanley’s banking revenue growing by 46%, versus 17% for BofA.
Morgan Stanley, and Brian DiCaprio did not respond to a request for comment on this article. Bank of America declined to comment.

item.title: SpaceX's IPO is only 4x oversubscribed. Spare a thought for the bankers selling it
item.description:
Today is the day of the SpaceX IPO. Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-06-10/spacex-ipo-is-said-to-be-more-than-four-times-oversubscribed> reported yesterday that its 555.6m shares are "more than 4x" oversubscribed. That sounds impressive. Quietly, though, some equity capital markets bankers are suggesting that maybe it's not.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
The SpaceX IPO will be the biggest IPO of all time and it is expected to value the company at $1.8 trillion. The previous biggest IPO of all time was that of Saudi Aramco in 2019, valued at only $29.4bn.
Saudi Aramco's offering was 465% oversubscribed <https://www.argaam.com/en/article/articledetail/id/1333537>. However, some bankers are suggesting that the Saudi Aramco IPO and SpaceX are not comparable. Although Saudi Aramco was also marketed to both retail and institutional investors, the demand for its stock came primarily from investors in Saudi and the Middle East. The WSJ <https://www.wsj.com/articles/saudi-aramco-sounds-out-investors-on-fresh-international-ipo-11576004672>noted at the time that non-Saudi institutional investors only received 23% of the allocation. By comparison, SpaceX is being marketed internationally, including a retail allocation of 30% versus the standard 5% <https://www.cnbc.com/2026/06/09/spacex-ipo-explained-stock-price-date.html>. "We've all had the emails from Hargreaves Lansdowne <https://www.hl.co.uk/news/inside-spacexs-ipo-filing-revenue-starlink-ai-and-key-financials>," observes one banker. "I can't remember the last IPO that had this much hype around it. The FOMO is real."
In the circumstances, then, a 4x over-subscription might be conceived as modest. The institutional tranche, which accounts for 70% of the total, should be above 4x covered in the opinion of some bankers. It's worth remembering that Figma's (much smaller) $1.2bn IPO <https://www.ft.com/content/d191bcea-15ae-40c8-9f9a-a850762cfa25?syn-25a6b1a6=1> last summer was 40 times oversubscribed.
It's possible, then, that the bankers who've competed hard to get a role selling the SpaceX IPO </news/spacex-ipo-2nd-tier-bank> are not having an entirely easy ride. It may not help that SpaceX has set a specific $135 price for its shares, instead of using the standard approach of offering a price range and seeing what investors are willing to pay. The WSJ <https://www.wsj.com/business/spacex-ipo-would-raise-75-billion-281b0886> reported earlier this month that SpaceX did this because it felt it had sufficient demand for its shares to fix a price.
Given that the IPO is 4x oversubscribed, maybe this was the right approach? However, 4x coverage isn't necessarily the achievement it seems. Bankers also observe that much depends upon whether the institutional demand is coming from long only investors or from hedge funds. Long only investors are far preferable. "4x coverage from hedge funds would be a bad thing," says one senior banker. Being on an ECM team at a hedge fund is often hard simply because syndicate desks in banks won't allocate to you.
All will become clearer when the stock floats tomorrow morning. Goldman Sachs and Morgan Stanley, which are leading the SpaceX deal declined and didn't respond to a request to comment respectively.

item.title: Citi's ex-IG credit traders have got something going at RBC Capital Markets
item.description:
As we noted just the other day </news/robert-thorne-rbc>, RBC Capital Markets has hired Robert Thorne, Citi's head of UK investment grade credit sales. It has since come to our attention that Thorne is not the only investment grade (IG) credit person RBC has hired from Citi. There are at least four other senior Citi people there too.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Not all were hired recently. Dan Spitzberg, RBC's head of high grade credit sales in New York, joined from Citi in the dark days of 2019. Justin Snyder, RBC's head of US investment grade credit trading, came from Citi in 2023. Nirva Shah, RBC's head of spread products technology, arrived from Citi in 2023 too. Mike Fisher, RBC's head of futures product development appeared from Citi in 2024.
Thorne will therefore be in good company, and may find himself joined by other ex-Citi people soon. Vincent Boccio, for example, has also just joined in New York as US head of electronic credit strategy. Boccio spent the last six years bouncing <https://brokercheck.finra.org/individual/summary/5959443>between four employers, but spent seven years at Citi until 2020.
RBC didn't respond to a request to comment on its partiality to people from Citi's IG team, but some RBC people are looking askance at their new colleagues. "RBC's hiring seems to have been extremely Citi centric in the past few years," says one. "Hiring externally is supposed to bring new ideas, but this won't be the case if everyone is coming from the same shop."
RBC's credit trading team has undergone changes </news/now-rbc-s-global-head-of-investment-grade-credit-trading-is-leaving> in the past year following the departure of James Chapman, the global head of credit trading who did not come from Citi but who once worked for Deutsche Bank. Chapman appears to have been popular.
Not all RBC's senior hires have come from Citi. Last year, the Canadian bank also hired Ralph Boumalham from UBS for leveraged loan trading, but Boumalham ironically left for Citi </news/rbc-s-new-head-of-european-loan-trading-left-for-citi-after-two-months>after only two months. It also hired Mousemi Dey from Unicredit, Justin Stratton-Christensen from KNG Securities and Stuart Marenghi from Wells Fargo.

item.title: How to work at an electronic trading firm as an 18-year-old sports fanatic
item.description:
It's no secret that electronic trading firms </news/high-frequency-trading-hiring-and-pay> like to hire and develop young talent </news/networking-for-1m-quant-and-ai-jobs-really-begins-at-14-years-old>, but they generally set their sights on students who have already finished their first year of university </news/high-frequency-trading-internship>. At Jump Trading, however, a new global internship opportunity is available to people as young as 18 with no scholastic requirements. It helps if you've got a sophisticated dataset of the Curaçao national football team.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Jump has launched the Probability Cup on free-to-play prediction platform SportsPredict. The tournament involves making a number of probability based bets for matches in the upcoming World Cup; for example, participants are asked to provide a likelihood from 1 to 100% that South Africa will commit more fouls than Mexico in today's tournament opener.
The competition, is open to anyone 16 or older but offers an interesting prize to the top performer aged 18 or over: a 10-week paid "fellowship" in Jump's Chicago office, where the winner will assist the running of a $1m sports-related portfolio.
Of course, there's no upper age limit for Jump's competition either. Since the prize is a 'fellowship' rather than an internship, the prize could just as easily go to someone in their 30s. Jump says the tournament was birthed from the view that exceptional talent can come from anywhere.
It's not clear how much the fellowship will pay, but the competition's FAQs page says it will be "commensurate with Jump Trading Group standard practices for similar short-term internship roles based in its Chicago headquarters." Jump doesn't disclose pay for its Chicago interns but, in New York, where salary disclosures are mandatory, we've seen salaries ranging from $4.8k to $5.8k per week. </news/electronic-trading-summer-internship-pay> A ten week fellowship may net the competition's winner upwards of $40k.

item.title: Morning Coffee: When you work for a 2nd tier bank and it's the SpaceX IPO. Private equity guy calls AI late at night instead of juniors
item.description:
So far in the coverage of the SpaceX deal, everyone has been focused on the controversy over “top left” position. Officially, Goldman Sachs and Morgan Stanley </news/space-x-ipo-goldman-sachs-morgan-stanley>are jointly leading the deal, and we are constantly told (although not quite so emphatically by Goldman) that their relative position on the paperwork is a simple matter of alphabetical order. But what about the banks further down in the syndicate <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-06-10/spacex-leaves-some-banks-peeved-at-junior-roles-in-ipo-lineup?utm_medium=email&utm_source=author_alert&utm_term=260610&utm_campaign=author_17510379&embedded-checkout=true>?
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
This part of the prospectus is colloquially known as the “eye test”, because the names and logos get smaller the further you go down. And arguments over positioning in the optician’s chart can be every bit as passionate as those over lead-left, because there is almost as much to play for.
There is a lot of jargon here. The “lead managers” are the top banks in the deal - they get a much bigger share of the fees than anyone else and are responsible for making decisions about timing and execution. They are also responsible for keeping records of investor interest and, very importantly deciding on the allocation of shares.
When an IPO is heavily oversubscribed (SpaceX apparently has bids for about four times as many shares as it is selling), this role as “bookrunner” can be very important and lucrative. As IPO expert Jay Ritter points out <https://finance.yahoo.com/markets/stocks/articles/7-billion-horse-race-goldman-070000527.html?soc_src=social-sh&soc_trk=tw&tsrc=twtr>, there is a lot of discretion about which investors get the shares they bid for and which investors gets their orders scaled back. This means that the investors have to be nice to you; being bookrunner on a hot deal is one of the few situations where the sell side has the power.
At the other end of the scale, “co-managers”, despite the title, have little or no responsibility when it comes to managing the issue. They often don’t have very much to do all except phone up the small clients that bigger banks don’t want to bother with. Co-managers are often responsible for particular geographical areas where they have strength, and if they are able to deliver really big chunks of capital they might have the clout to insist on being called “passive bookrunners”. That’s very much an honorary title when it comes to allocations in an oversubscribed issue – the co-managers and passive bookrunners can ask nicely on behalf of a really important client, but they don’t make final decisions. But it does mean that you get league table credit, which matters a lot to bankers.
It appears that banks like SocGen and Mizuho might have been expecting to be “passive bookrunners” on the SpaceX IPO, but they are not. They are going to be listed as mere co-managers. This sucks pretty badly for them, but there isn’t much that can be done; in a deal like this, the smaller players don’t have much leverage and nobody wants to make a fuss because it might affect their relationship with SpaceX. Everyone involved is declining to comment in public, but it’s likely that harsh words in many languages will have been exchanged in private.
Elsewhere, when the co-founder of private equity house Thoma Bravo used to wake up in the middle of the night with a bright idea, he apparently didn’t scribble it on a pad by the bed and go back to sleep – he called or emailed an associate <https://www.businessinsider.com/orlando-bravo-thoma-private-equity-ai-junior-employees-2026-6>. Now, however, Orlando Bravo is finding that it’s easier to ask an AI chatbot, get a quick answer and then presumably go back to sleep.
Obviously, this is a bit of a double edged sword for the associates. On the one hand, as Bravo says, not getting the midnight calls “improves their life, which is what they want”. On the other hand, if the boss is using AI to answer his questions in the middle of the night, that raises the worrying possibility that he might be able to use it during the day too.
The best scenario for PE associates would be that they now get to work on a curated selection of best ideas, rather than any random shower thoughts or night terrors. Bravo himself says that he thinks AI will allow young employees to “mature faster” and focus on higher-order thinking and investing. But although their lives might get easier, they may feel a bit lonely in the middle of the night.
Meanwhile …
It seems that President Trump may have mistaken a comment about Citi’s number one position in the league table for power and energy M&A for them being in the lead overall. (Actually, Goldman Sachs is, by quite a margin). Slightly embarrassing, but his public message of congratulations might be welcome in any case, as everything contributes to the sense of improving momentum and Citi's share price received a brief boost. (Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-06-10/trump-lavishes-praise-on-citi-for-m-a-ranking-it-doesn-t-have?utm_medium=email&utm_source=author_alert&utm_term=260610&utm_campaign=author_17510379>)
“Microshifting” is the new term for employees splitting their work into chunks and taking time for family life, personal projects and relaxation in between. It’s not a new phenomenon, though – as well as being what everyone did while working from home in the pandemic, junior bankers have been finishing the day’s work, getting dinner and then starting the evening shift at 11pm for ages. (WSJ <https://www.wsj.com/lifestyle/workplace/what-is-microshifting-workday-productivity-be5d150f?st=2p1FRq&reflink=desktopwebshare_permalink>)
Former Morgan Stanley equities analyst Huw van Steenis is moving from his position as vice-chair at Oliver Wyman to an “economic and policy strategist” role at Apollo. (Financial News <https://www.fnlondon.com/articles/apollo-poaches-oliver-wymans-huw-van-steenis-d28f71be?mod=topic_news>)
Magnetar Capital is launching a new equities hedge fund which will be managed by human portfolio managers, but which will have hundreds of AI bots “scouring the investment universe for ideas” and making recommendations to them. Head of AI Quant Trevor Mottl has bought multiple Nvidia servers and developed an advanced inference layer, but it’s not clear which lucky portfolio manager is going to enjoy the experience of an electronic sell-side. (Bloomberg <https://www.fnlondon.com/articles/apollo-poaches-oliver-wymans-huw-van-steenis-d28f71be?mod=topic_news>)
Revolut founder Nik Storonsky described his top employees as “self-guided missiles”, but although he has given them the freedom to launch new products on their own initiative, the ECB has reminded Revolut that there does actually need to be a bit of central control and approval. (FT <https://www.ft.com/content/7cc86162-f68c-4772-b7ce-9c72dfb15f05?syn-25a6b1a6=1>)
Travelling bankers will be pleased to hear about a new chain of airport lounges centred on video games, which might mean that there is less of a queue for the small sandwiches and warm white wine on your next roadshow. (WSJ <https://www.wsj.com/lifestyle/travel/the-airport-lounges-where-videogames-are-more-important-than-drinks-a0d10ddf>)

item.title: Hedge fund Millennium's big US portfolio manager and the employment contract dispute
item.description:
Adam Grunfeld seems to be a big deal at hedge fund </news/how-to-get-a-hedge-fund-job> Millennium </news/how-to-get-a-job-at-hedge-fund-millennium> in New York City. Since April, Grunfeld has been Millennium's head of portfolio manager (PM) development and strategic initiatives for the fixed income unit. Prior to that, he was a senior portfolio manager at Millennium for four and a half years. He's also done stints at Element, Tudor and Balyasny Asset Management. </news/balyasny-hedge-fund>
Earlier this year, though, Grunfeld thought about leaving Millennium. He had these thoughts in January, after a headhunter </news/ideal-hedge-fund-recruiter> introduced him to rival hedge fund, Schonfeld Strategic Advisors. For a few brief months, Grunfeld had his head turned. He even engaged an attorney and signed an employment agreement committing to join Schonfeld in July 2027 after exiting Millennium as expeditiously as possible. But then Grunfeld changed his mind. And Schonfeld has not taken kindly to that.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
As we reported last month </news/adam-grunfeld-schonfeld-millennium>, Schonfeld has filed a lawsuit <https://iapps.courts.state.ny.us/webcivil/FCASeFiledDocsDetail?county_code=LG7MrY4CWhxnqGoeBSXKeg%3D%3D&txtIndexNo=Qp76dpIEp0xf4U5keEpyDQ%3D%3D&showMenu=no&isPreRji=N&civilCase=gWa9wSbdVOEGZtC1%2FyaVGA%3D%3D> requesting that Grunfeld pay $11m in damages. In its court filing, Schonfeld says it's not only lost the profits that Grunfeld would have generated had he joined, but that it's lost opportunities to hire other candidates. Schonfeld says it spent a lot of money trying to hire Grunfeld, including "internal personnel time, third-party costs, recruiting fees, investment and risk due-diligence expenses, legal fees, and background investigation costs." It wants all of this to be repaid in the $11m, plus extra for the fact that it says Grunfeld disclosed details of his employment negotiations to Millennium, in violation of what Schonfeld claims was its confidentiality agreement.
Grunfeld is having none of this. Still happily at Millennium, Grunfeld made his own filing last week asking for the case to be thrown out by the New York Court. He says he reconsidered his decision to leave Millennium just 15 days after signing Schonfeld's employment agreement and that the $11m is "grossly disproportionate" to Schonfeld's costs. Instead, Grunfeld says the $11m is equivalent to a signing bonus </news/de-shaw-employee-fund> (which he hasn't been paid) </news/hedge-fund-pass-throughs> that would have locked him in at Schonfeld for three years. If he'd joined Schonfeld, Grunfeld's filing says he would have received the $11m in his first month and would have been obliged to repay it if he'd left again within three years. But because he didn't join Schonfeld, Grunfeld didn't get the $11m and therefore he says he can't be expected to pay it back. Effectively, Grunfeld says Schonfeld wants to charge him the same amount for changing his mind two weeks after signing when no money was exchanged as it would if Schonfeld had actually paid him the $11m and then he'd left after that.
Part of the problem is that Schonfeld really likes Grunfeld. In its own filing, Schonfeld says Grunfeld is a "highly specialized portfolio manager" and that he has "unique investment judgment, strategy design, and capital deployment expertise developed over many years." Few other portfolio managers can replicate Grunfeld's ability, enthuses Schonfeld. If only he'd joined, Schonfeld says Grunfeld wouldn't have been any standard portfolio manager there. It says the two were contemplating a "multi-year relationship" in which Grunfeld would deploy "increasing amounts of capital" and "play a meaningful role in expanding Schonfeld’s investment capabilities and management bench."
Being jilted is presumably therefore painful, particularly as Grunfeld now seems to be developing the PM bench back at Millennium instead. But Grunfeld in turn says that Schonfeld's claims don't make any sense. - If he's really such a rare talent, Grunfeld says Schonfeld can't claim that it's also suffered from not using its time to hire someone else instead. He says that Schonfeld didn't have anyone else lined up and that it's simply claiming damages for losing "phantom candidates" and is trying to "coerce" him into going there. Schonfeld would clearly disagree.
Lastly, Grunfeld says Schonfeld can't claim liquidated damages for the lost profits he might have generated if only he'd arrived. Grunfeld says he would have had to generate more than $22m to create $11m in lost profits for Schonfeld, which also seems to suggest he'd negotiated an unusually generous 50% cut of his profits for himself...
Both Schonfeld and Grunfeld declined to comment for this article. Maybe Schonfeld would still like Grunfeld to change his mind? Maybe not. Schonfeld thinks Grunfeld used its job offer to negotiate a new and superior position at Millennium. If he'd actually joined Schonfeld as planned, then Schonfeld says $11m would have been "only a fraction" of his anticipated compensation there. However this case ends, there's clearly an interesting opening available for someone else.

item.title: JPMorgan hired a Hudson River Trading quant as a credit trading MD
item.description:
It's a rare sight for someone to leave an electronic trading firm </news/high-frequency-trading-hiring-and-pay> like Jane Street </news/how-to-get-a-job-at-jane-street> or Citadel Securities to join a bank. It does happen though, and Steve Cho is the most recent example. After spending five years at Hudson River Trading </news/hudson-river-trading-pay-uk-high-frequency-trading> (HRT), he left to join JPMorgan last month.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Cho joined the bank as an MD in credit trading. At HRT he was an algo developer (their term for a quant trader), and an algorithmic credit trading </news/credit-trading> lead. His LinkedIn states that he worked on long/short market neutral strategies for bonds and derivatives, while also employing some fundamental analysis.
Most people that leave a prop firm for a bank are banking alums and Cho is no exception. He joined Barclays' electronic credit market making team as a PhD </news/do-you-need-a-phd-for-a-quant-job-in-finance> graduate before being swiftly poached by Citadel to become a portfolio manager. He spent one year at the former and six at the latter before joining HRT.
Other HRT alums working in banks today spent far longer in banking before they left in the first place. Douglas Borden, who joined JPMorgan's execution team in 2024, had already made MD at Goldman Sachs before joining the HFT industry with now-defunct KCG Holdings. Antoine Savine </news/hudson-river-trading-s-head-of-macro-analytics-makes-shock-switch-from-a-hft-to-a-bank>, HRT's ex-head of macro analytics that joined Barclays last year, was previously head of quant research at BNP Paribas. By that standard, Cho is a relative newcomer to banking.
It's not clear what stood out about a return to banking, but Cho may have felt kinship with JPMorgan over its affinity for letting their staff burn through AI tokens. At the bank, middle-office staff have reportedly used AI tokens equivalent to more than their annual salary </news/jpmorgan-ai-token-spending> while, at HRT, employees spend upwards of $1k a day on AI. </news/ai-token-use-traders>
JPMorgan declined to comment, while HRT did not respond to a comment request.

item.title: Are you "building a boat out of paperclips" if you don't start coding in Rust?
item.description:
Rust, </news/rust-vs-c-plus-plus-financial-services-low-latency> a language on the fringe of industries like Big Tech and finance, has a cult following for its ability to combine performance and safety. Fans have called for Rust to replace the likes of C and C++ </news/2022/02/is-it-worth-learning-c> for years. When it comes to the former, veteran language designer Simon Peyton Jones agrees; he recently said "things would be way better" if we "rewrote all of our software infrastructure in Rust."
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Jones, co-creator of functional language Haskell, recently spoke to ex-Meta engineer Ryan Peterman on a podcast. <https://www.developing.dev/p/co-creator-of-haskell-functional> He said that Haskell and C are polar opposites. C has a lot of memory safety issues, but it's very useful, while Haskell was extremely safe but had very few use cases. Rust, however, keeps Haskell's safety while also maintaining C's performance. It'd be a gargantuan task to convert all the world's C into Rust, but Jones said "it's tragic how much effort and ingenuity and money has been lost" by writing computational infrastructure "in an insecure language." He said that continuing to develop C infrastructure is like "build[ing] a boat out of paperclips and [being] surprised that it's leaky."
C++ has a similar reputation for being unsafe </news/2022/09/c-programming-language-safety>, but it's a very different language at heart. It's difficult to use, and involves a lot debugging, but in return you get supposedly unparalleled speed. For some of the most lucrative engineering roles, such as those in low-latency trading teams at hedge funds and prop firms, the safety of Rust is somewhat irrelevant. Engineers are paid upwards of $500k to make their code extremely fast but also extremely reliable. </news/low-latency-c> Engineers that have tried to port their C++ systems into Rust have previously complained that the language is "a nightmare to implement" </news/C-vs-Rust-High-Frequency-Trading-Hell> for the most latency sensitive aspects. This doesn't mean hedge funds don't appreciate Rust. We noted last year </news/rust-replacing-c-programming-language-hedge-fund> that hedge funds like Capula and Millennium are replacing their old C infrastructure with Rust. It won't be replacing C++ at these firms any time soon.
There are other options; Jones said that OCaml </news/2023/11/ocaml-vs-c-high-frequency-trading>is "an absolute hotbed of innovation." It's another functional language, but it's more "strict" than Haskell. Jones said that, similarly to C++ it doesn't get in your way when writing code, and that "good OCaml programmers won't [create] many side effects." It's the language of choice at Jane Street, </news/how-to-get-a-job-at-jane-street>the electronic trading firm which generated $39.6bn in revenue </news/jane-street-employees> last year, so its presumably at least competitive with C++ when it comes to latency.
Functional languages like OCaml and Rust may become more influential in the age of AI. Jones said that, due to their stricter focus on safety, they're less likely to accept unforeseen bugs written by AI which "dramatically tightens" the code editing process. C++ creator Bjarne Soustrup said last month </news/using-english-as-a-coding-language-won-t-get-you-a-600k-programming-job> that the process of editing out AI-written bugs is causing top C++ engineers to retire; perhaps they'll come back if they're offered a Rust job instead.

item.title: Bank of America's nicest guy says firms have been surprised into hiring by all the deal activity
item.description:
It wasn't so long ago that Brian Moynihan, the deeply enduring CEO of Bank of America, said </news/hedge-fund-analyst-sue-boss> he expected headcount at the bank to keep falling amidst the industry-wide enthusiasm for automation. </news/banking-recruitment-s-sorry-story-more-candidates-fewer-jobs>2,000 coding jobs have been 'saved' by AI, said Brian </news/banking-recruitment-s-sorry-story-more-candidates-fewer-jobs>in January. Relationships cannot be automated, though. And so senior bankers are still needed.
Speaking yesterday at the Morgan Stanley Financials Conference, Jim De Mare, the known pleasant guy </news/2023/08/jim-demare-bank-of-america>who is president of global markets at Bank of America, suggested that BofA might be hiring more bankers than it expected this year.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
"Candidly, I think the economic growth that we're seeing in the deal activity has caught many firms by surprise, and that's why it's been such an active environment for recruiting," said De Mare. He also said: ."..we build in the flexibility in our planning such that, yes, we're going to staff up accordingly where we need to be."
Bank of America is one of the lead bookrunners on the SpaceX IPO </news/spacex-ipo-bankers> and it has therefore decorated its lobby with rockets. <https://www.reuters.com/legal/government/wall-street-banks-ceos-promote-spacex-in-flashy-events-with-bofa-morgan-stanley-2026-06-04/>With a possible view to winning future technology deals, BofA has also been hiring supplementary technology bankers. Semiconductor specialist Richard Hardegree <https://www.reuters.com/legal/transactional/bank-america-taps-tech-dealmaker-richard-hardegree-vice-chair-ma-memo-shows-2026-05-08/>is joining from UBS as vice chair of M&A. Former BofA head of TMT banking, Gary Kirkham, is returning from Centerview. Goldman Sachs MD Jason Rowe is joining as co-head of global technology investment banking. Mahir Zaimoglu has returned to banking after co-founding an AI recruiting business and is now head of TMT M&A at BofA in London. And Patrick Czornick joined the bank two weeks ago from JPMorgan in London as co-head of EMEA TMT banking, which was a step up from his previous role as a telecoms banker.
It's not clear whether this is what De Mare was referring to, but BofA has presumably invested over $10m in its telecoms banking business in the space of only a few months.
Away from investment banking deals, De Mare was especially enthused about Asian equities. "There's a lot of desire to access Asia, the Asian markets. That's been a high area of growth," he said. Japan is experiencing "newfound enthusiasm" now that Japanese rates are higher.
In the past few years, De Mare said the potential for the markets business has grown dramatically. "I think we were doing like seven billion shares a day in trading volume in equities pre-COVID. I think we got up to 11 billion or 12 billion. We're between 15 billion and 17 billion a day today," he said of equities trading. In fixed income, De Mare said rates hedging is a big part of the business. But trading has also changed, he said. It's increasingly about "liquid observable assets" with low capital requirements. Banks are now focused on "facilitating" trades and electronic market making, not taking risk. "More of the activity of the banks today is in -- and brokerage houses are in Level 1 and Level 2 assets," he said, referring to assets that are liquid and reliably priced. "- I don't even hear anybody talk about Level 3 assets anymore as a part of their balance sheet."
While this sounds good for systematic traders and senior investment bankers, De Mare reiterated BofA's overall focus on costs. "We're always trying to optimize capital and financial resources," he declared yesterday. De Mare said too that: "I think we're pretty religious about reviews and performance and how we're doing." This might suggest that BofA people who are not sufficiently venerated will find themselves excommunicated.

item.title: Nomura paid its bonuses and now some traders are leaving
item.description:
As you will recall if you read this article, </news/nomura-bonuses>Nomura announced its bonuses last month. Some people there were paid "beyond expectations." Some were not. Some have been leaving.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
We understand that around five people have left Nomura's trading business in recent weeks. But we also understand that at least two people have arrived.
Nomura declined to comment for this article but we understand that the departures include: Sergey Smallkov, an emerging markets STIR trader in London; Kentaro Seki, a senior rates trader in Tokyo and Christopher Killoran, a junior rates trader in Sydney. An executive director in equities sales is thought to have left too, along with an associate in FX options.
It's not clear where they are going. Nomura traders can be popular with hedge funds </news/escaped-nomura-rates-trader-joins-hedge-fund-amidst-bonus-rumblings>because they take more proprietary risk than traders elsewhere.
Amidst the post-bonus departures, Nomura is also understood to have hired. Suhail Patel, an FX options trader has just joined from UBS in Singapore. So too has Lattakit Lapudomkarn, an economist in Thailand.
At its recent 2026 investor day <https://www.nomuraholdings.com/en/investor/presentation/main/017/teaserItems1/00/tableContents/00/multiFileUpload30/link/2026_0529_01_prem.pdf>, Nomura noted that macro products now account for only 25% of revenues in its wholesale (investment) bank, down from 35% ten years ago. Credit trading now accounts for 22% of revenues, up from 16%. Securitised products trading revenues in particular have grown 90% in recent years. By diversifying, Nomura said it has cut revenue volatility in its wholesale business by 37%.
