item.title: How to get a banking job: 14 routes in
item.description:
Getting a job in an investment bank </news/what-is-an-investment-bank> will very likely be the most difficult thing you do in your entire life.
Click here to join the bubble by eFinancialCareers, our new anonymous community. <https://bubble.efinancialcareers.com/login?_gl=1*ut8n6u*_gcl_au*MTg4MDY2NTExNi4xNzYxNTU3NDIz*_ga*ODczNzI2NTA0LjE3NTM2OTk2ODI.*_ga_NZFQV4MXPM*czE3NjM2NDM0MDYkbzI1NiRnMSR0MTc2MzY0MzQzOSRqMjckbDAkaDMyMjc4OTUzMw..>✍️
No, we’re not joking. Goldman Sachs’ internship has an acceptance rate </news/goldman-sachs-internship-acceptance-rate> of just 0.7%, on par with peers </news/bank-acceptance-rate> such as JPMorgan and Morgan Stanley. Getting a job on the buy-side is even harder: Citadel <https://www.businessinsider.com/citadel-internship-acceptance-rate-schools-majors-ken-griffin-2025-6> has an acceptance rate of just 0.4% and Blackstone <https://www.businessinsider.com/blackstone-analyst-class-entry-level-recruiting-2025-11> an acceptance rate of just 0.2%, according to Business Insider.
Your life before finance is nothing in comparison. Did you get into Oxford? Bah - an acceptance rate of 14% is a doozy. How about Harvard? A 4% acceptance rate; frankly decadent.
If you want to get that job, you will need to work very hard, exhaust every opportunity you have, and really – really, genuinely – be a little bit lucky. You have more options than you realise – and these are what they are.
Get into banking by doing a degree apprenticeship
The absolute earliest you can get a job in an investment bank is when you’re applying for university </news/finance/the-top-100-universities-globally-for-careers-in-banking-and-finance>.
Degree apprenticeships – during which you’re employed by the bank while you’re studying – only exist in the UK, and they’re insanely competitive to get into: one program participant in 2023 told us that </news/2023/09/banking-degree-apprenticeship> there were over 4,000 applicants to 28-degree apprenticeship roles at his bank last year, an acceptance rate of less than 0.7%. That’s on par with Goldman Sachs or JPMorgan’s “normal” internship acceptance rates.
It might be worth doing a degree internship though, even if you have to start preparing for a banking job at age 17. At the end of your four-year course, you have years of part-time experience, much better finances than your average university graduate, and a graduate offer to return to the firm as a full-time employee.
Degree apprenticeships have grown in popularity in recent years. Goldman Sachs, for example, launched its first one in London in 2016 <https://www.goldmansachs.com/pressroom/press-releases/2016/announcement-degree-apprenticeship-jan-2016>, and it expanded the program in October last year, announcing a partnership <https://www.goldmansachs.com/pressroom/press-releases/2025/degree-apprenticeship-programme-in-birmingham> with the University of Warwick in Birmingham. The firm offers degree apprenticeships in FICC and equities, engineering, and operations in London, as well as digital & technology solutions in Birmingham.
A number of banks offer degree apprenticeships. Aside from Goldman, they are also offered by JPMorgan, Deutsche Bank, HSBC, and Santander.
Get a banking internship as an undergraduate and convert it
Summer internships </news/internship-applications-banks> are the most typical way to get a job in an investment bank, be it a front- or back-office </news/front-office-middle-office-back-office-banking> job.
Among summer internships, the most numerous are summer analyst programs, which are meant for university/college students to do in their penultimate year of undergraduate study. Other types exist, and we’ll talk about them later, but it’s the summer analyst programs that are the gold standard of bank recruitment.
If you’re an undergraduate right now, most of your energy should be going into applying for these and getting into these.
Be warned, however – they are very competitive. The 0.7% figure in the introduction to this article is mostly comprised of summer internship applications. Data from UK-based student application Trackr suggested in April </news/how-many-intern-applications> that the average student who received an internship offer from an investment bank made over 100 applications on average.
What can help improve your odds? Going to the right university </news/target-universities-finance-uk>, for one. Playing the right kind of sport </news/best-sport-for-hedge-fund-job>. Having a bomb CV/resume </news/the-absolutely-perfect-investment-banking-cv>. Writing a fire cover letter </news/cover-letter-template-will-get-job-banking>. And – especially if you’re pursuing a career in Europe – getting a spring internship </news/spring-week-bank> or two under your belt.
Spring internships </news/spring-week-bank> are week-long taster sessions in your first year of university that, hopefully, can lead to receiving an offer to join a bank for a second-year summer internship, although not all banks offer this. They’re extremely important </news/internship-applications-banks>. Some banks offer similar “sophomore” internships in the US, but those are going out of fashion, as they are/were mostly used </news/bank-diversity-programs> for diversity recruitment.
Get a banking internship as a postgraduate and convert it
If you don't get into a summer analyst programme while you're at university, you can try when you've graduated. But this is a controversial topic.
If they don't get a job in a bank or financial services firm while at university, most students aspiring for a career in finance either try to get a masters in finance </news/the-top-30-masters-in-finance-for-getting-a-job-in-investment-banking> degree or a masters in financial engineering </news/top-masters-in-financial-engineering-courses> (MFE). It's worth noting, however, that doing a master’s degree (no matter that kind) still only gets you a summer analyst internship. You will be at the bottom of the bank’s hierarchy </news/banks-weird-hierarchies-analysts-associates-vps-mds>. Therefore, what you’re really doing is paying five or six figures and spending an extra one or two years to get an opportunity that you should have gotten well before.
Additionally, not all master’s in finance degrees </news/the-top-30-masters-in-finance-for-getting-a-job-in-investment-banking> are made equal. Former careers coach Mark Ross told us last year </news/masters-in-finance-worth-it> that a lot of degrees are “ripping off candidates” and “need to be shut down”. Many graduates of these programs say that they feel like their employment prospects were misrepresented to them, and that they’re unhappy with the impact their masters degree had on their careers.
Masters in financial engineering degrees are more mathematical and designed for quantitative careers. The gold standard is the one offered by Baruch College in New York, where the MFE can cost as little as $29k (if you're a New York State resident) and has a $217k median compensation for first-year graduates.
If you don't want to do an expensive masters, careers coach and former Morgan Stanley & Goldman Sachs intern Brian Landeros </news/how-to-get-a-goldman-sachs-internship> says that you can “buy time” by delaying your graduation.
That doesn’t mean failing exams or modules or whatever else – you can just delay the graduation date. The reason for that is simple: banks require you to have a graduation date no later or earlier than a certain time period to apply for an opportunity. If you can delay your graduation date, you can apply for next year’s internships. That might be better (and cheaper) than doing a master’s degree.
Get a banking internship as an MBA and convert it
Doing a two-year masters in business administration (MBA) and getting an internship as a summer associate during it was once the gold standard of getting an investment banking job.
Although the route is not as popular as it used to be, there are still opportunities to get on a summer associate internship during your MBA </news/mba-salaries-banking>, and converting that into a full-time job in the same way as if you were an undergraduate student.
And yes, it’s summer associate, not analyst </news/banks-weird-hierarchies-analysts-associates-vps-mds>. One of the big benefits of doing an MBA over other ways into banking is that you get to jump up the ladder </news/banks-weird-hierarchies-analysts-associates-vps-mds> a bit. But the biggest benefit is that you get to skip the abysmal work-life balance that analysts </news/banking-jobs-with-the-best-lifestyle> suffer - because you usually need a few years’ work experience to do an MBA, don’t consider it a way to start climbing the ladder earlier in life.
Like master’s degrees, not all MBAs are made equal. Banks are just as picky with who they hire from MBA programs as they are from undergrad ones, and there’s very much a hierarchy of top business schools </news/best-mba-for-finance-banking> that they recruit from.
Another benefit to starting your banking career with an MBA is that associates are better paid than analysts. A top MBA can lead to an investment banking job that pays up to </news/mba-salaries-banking> $350k in starting salary (although the median is usually around $175k). That's much higher than an analyst, who typically earns around </news/investment-banking-salaries-bonuses> $110k - $120k in salary their first year. Bonuses for associates are generally more generous, too.
Get a banking internship as a PhD and convert it
More studying? Well, if you’re this far down the list, you’ve already missed undergraduate, postgraduate, and MBA opportunities. What’s a few more years of studying to you? Besides, PhD holders are well-appreciated by banks, who itch for them to join their quantitative </news/quant-jobs-finance> research teams.
For example, Goldman Sachs is looking for a number of people to join its quant strats (sales & trading </news/how-do-you-get-a-job-in-sales-and-trading-in-a-bank>) team as summer associates in London.
Citi is also currently looking for a lot of PhDs in various roles. One of the roles, an AVP in risk decision model development <https://jobs.citi.com/job/mumbai/risk-decision-model-development-assistant-vice-president/287/93655063648>, is particularly curious – it notes that it’s either looking for seven years of experience, “or PhD degree in statistics, economics, or equivalent experience.” A PhD can jump a few more rungs up the banking ladder than a plain old MBA could.
Other banks have more interesting uses for PhDs. JPMorgan has a famous quantum computing team </news/jp-morgan-is-hiring-quantum-computing-ph-ds-on-260k-salaries-again>, and it hires a lot of PhDs for it, paying them salaries of up to $260k.
Not all quantitative positions requite PhDs, however. Jane Street, the electronic trading firm par excellence, does not exclusively hire PhDs </news/jane-street-employees-reveal-what-degrees-matter-when-applying-for-quant-jobs> – in fact, it mostly does not. “PhD or other research experience is a plus,” the firm wrote in a job opening for a quantitative researcher. “But ultimately, we’re more interested in how you think and learn, than what you currently know.”
Get a banking internship after you graduate
Okay, you might be starting to get desperate now. You should be: if you’ve done an undergraduate degree, a master’s degree, an MBA, and a PhD, congratulations: you’re the best-qualified person who still can’t get into banking alive today.
But let's roll the clocks back a bit. If you're graduated from your bachelors and masters degrees (and haven't committed to an MBA or PhD), two options exist for you to get that all-important internship: graduate schemes and off-cycle internships. A graduate scheme without prior internship experience is pretty much impossible, but off-cycle internships are still an option.
Off-cycle are longer (3 to 12 months long at UBS <https://www.ubs.com/global/en/careers/early-careers/off-cycle-internship-program.html>, for example) internships working as an analyst. You get some training, and then you're basically a full-time analyst who isn't there full-time. Off-cycle internships have had a bad rap over the years, being seen as cheap and disposable labour that was treated rather poorly. That’s less so the case these days: in fact, one Goldman Sachs off-cycle research intern </news/goldman-sachs-internship> in London received a credit in a research note alongside his senior colleagues. We talk more about off-cycle internships here </news/no-spring-internship-bank>.
Get a job in banking by joining the army
Admittedly a bit of an insane step to take for the sake of any internship, and probably one your mother would disapprove of, but there are plenty of examples of banks having specific outreach schemes for military veterans that you can apply to.
Most opportunities for veterans are in the US. JPMorgan <https://www.jpmorganchase.com/newsroom/stories/corporate-world-help-veterans-navigate-the-reverse-military-transition>, Morgan Stanley <https://www.morganstanley.com/people/experienced-professionals/veterans>, and Goldman Sachs <https://www.goldmansachs.com/careers/programs-for-professionals/veterans-integration-program> are all known to have special programs for military personnel transitioning into the private sector, as does Barclays <https://www.hiringourheroes.org/career-services/fellowships/internships/cfp/> (in the USA). Some banks, including JPMorgan, also have EMEA opportunities for veterans <https://www.jpmorganchase.com/careers/explore-opportunities/programs/military-uk>.
One of the most famous veterans recently in finance was Leo Lukenas </news/leo-lukenas>. A former green beret and Bank of America associate, Lukenas died of an "acute coronary artery thrombus," during a period in which he was allegedly working 120-hour weeks. His twin brother, Les Lukenas </news/goldman-sachs-hired-les-lukenas-twin-of-bof-a-associate-who-died-as-a-summer-intern>, who was also a green beret, was hired by Goldman Sachs after his brother's passing.
Start the CFA program
A bit of a hardcore option given the workload, but if you’re already in an established career, doing at least the CFA Level I will help show recruiters and banks that you’re serious about finance, as well as teach you some of the basic concepts that should apply directly to your future work.
Doing a CFA is particularly useful for international students </news/doing-cfa-level-i-student> or professionals </news/is-cfa-worth-it> seeking to move into a finance career, due to its perceived value to Western institutions. Many students also do the CFA Level I during their university years </news/doing-cfa-level-i-student> as social proof, too.
The downside (or upside, to an employer) of the CFA is that you do actually have to work for it - it is more than possible to fail any of the three stages. CFA Institute indicates <https://www.cfainstitute.org/sites/default/files/docs/programs/cfa-program/cfa-exam-results-pass-rate-infographic.pdf> that the pass rates for Levels I, II, and III were around 43%, 42%, and 50% respectively in the latest testing period available (November 2025).
Get a job in banking by coming in through accounting
Getting into banking via accounting takes a very long time, courtesy of all those years of extra studying you also have to do for the ACA/ACCA/etc.
Accountants also tend to be a bit of a desperate pick for banks. They’re usually brought in when dealmaking is booming to work on what seems like an endless supply of business. Alas, dealmaking is not exactly booming at the moment – not the way it was in 2021, at least.
The most famous example of an accountant who succeeded in banking is probably Julian Salisbury </news/2023/01/goldman-sachs-partner-how-i-moved-out-of-the-middle-office>, former chief investment officer of Goldman Sachs Asset Management (and now co-President and co-CIO of asset management firm Sixth Street), who joined Goldman from KPMG.
Get a job in banking by becoming a lawyer
Law isn’t as popular an entry point as it used to be, but it definitely has at least one significant previous example of success – former Goldman Sachs CEO Lloyds Blankfein, who left law to join a trading firm that was a subsidiary of Goldman’s as a trader. The 80s were a very different time compared to now, though.
Get a job in banking through consulting
Management consulting </news/pay-mckinsey-bain-bcg-big-four> is probably the best-positioned way to transition from a different industry into banking. Bankers and consultants tend to be very similar characters in terms of education, work ethics, and so on. Your field of expertise as a consultant also matters – transitioning from regulatory implementation to compliance </news/2022/07/how-to-get-a-job-in-compliance>, for instance, is quite straightforward.
There’s also a veritable hall of fame for people that reached the highest levels of banking despite their backgrounds in consulting. The former CEOs of Credit Suisse, UBS, Morgan Stanley, Standard Chartered, and Commerzbank were all ex-McKinsey partners at one point: the present CEO of Citi, Jane Fraser, was also a partner at the firm earlier in her career.
Get a job in equity research through something entirely unrelated
Equity research </news/finance/research-job-investment-bank> isn’t strictly investment banking, but our 2026 Compensation & Lifestyle Report </banking-compensation-guide> showed that they’re doing very well for themselves in terms of both pay and work-life balance. Given their more relaxed work schedules, the average bank researcher earned as much money per hour worked as some investment banking teams.
Breaking into equity research is often done by someone already in the industry they wish to cover. Going from a medical career into covering healthcare equities is a tale as old as time, for instance.
Get a job in banking by sending an email
Cold emails </news/finance/cold-email-investment-banking-job> are definitely not what they used to be. A running joke these days is that boomers like to recommend youngsters walk into a potential workplace, ask to speak to the owner, “make eye contact and give a firm handshake,” and boom, you have a job. Well, that doesn’t quite do it these days (not for investment banking, at least). And neither does cold emailing.
That being said, if you’re this far down the list, what do you have to lose but your pride? And besides, a low chance is not no chance.
Get a job in banking because your uncle has one
If you can benefit from nepotism, you don’t need our advice on how to dabble in it. And we don’t encourage such a thing, obviously.
Nepotism has always been prevalent in finance, despite rather consistent efforts to eradicate it over the years. One person in investment management </news/i-am-leaving-my-buy-side-job-and-am-fed-up-with-the-nepotism> told us earlier this year that "every firm" for which he had worked us was nepotistic to some degree, based on both the relatives of key executives and key clients.
Brian Landeros </news/how-to-get-a-goldman-sachs-internship> noted that, when he was doing his internships, he was surrounded by "rich Latin Americans, a member of the British nobility, and the son of Kenyan royalty." That's not some bizarre intern class either, believe it or not. Landeros generally recommends avoiding discussing your socioeconomic background, unless you're from the same privileged background as others.
Have a confidential story, tip, or comment you’d like to share? Contact: +44 7537 182250 (SMS, WhatsApp or voicemail). Telegram: @SarahButcher. Signal: sarahbutcher.22 Click here to fill in our anonymous form </about/editor-tips>, or email editortips@efinancialcareers.com.
Bear with us if you leave a comment at the bottom of this article: comments are moderated intermittently by human beings. Sometimes these humans might be asleep, or away from their desks, so it may take a while for your comment to appear. You must take sole responsibility for comments you post on this site. We will take reasonable steps to weed out anything that we consider to be offensive or inappropriate.
item.author: zeno.toulon@efinancialcareers.com (Zeno Toulon)

item.title: How to get a banking job: 14 routes in
item.description:
Getting a job in an investment bank </news/what-is-an-investment-bank> will very likely be the most difficult thing you do in your entire life.
Click here to join the bubble by eFinancialCareers, our new anonymous community. <https://bubble.efinancialcareers.com/login?_gl=1*ut8n6u*_gcl_au*MTg4MDY2NTExNi4xNzYxNTU3NDIz*_ga*ODczNzI2NTA0LjE3NTM2OTk2ODI.*_ga_NZFQV4MXPM*czE3NjM2NDM0MDYkbzI1NiRnMSR0MTc2MzY0MzQzOSRqMjckbDAkaDMyMjc4OTUzMw..>✍️
No, we’re not joking. Goldman Sachs’ internship has an acceptance rate </news/goldman-sachs-internship-acceptance-rate> of just 0.7%, on par with peers </news/bank-acceptance-rate> such as JPMorgan and Morgan Stanley. Getting a job on the buy-side is even harder: Citadel <https://www.businessinsider.com/citadel-internship-acceptance-rate-schools-majors-ken-griffin-2025-6> has an acceptance rate of just 0.4% and Blackstone <https://www.businessinsider.com/blackstone-analyst-class-entry-level-recruiting-2025-11> an acceptance rate of just 0.2%, according to Business Insider.
Your life before finance is nothing in comparison. Did you get into Oxford? Bah - an acceptance rate of 14% is a doozy. How about Harvard? A 4% acceptance rate; frankly decadent.
If you want to get that job, you will need to work very hard, exhaust every opportunity you have, and really – really, genuinely – be a little bit lucky. You have more options than you realise – and these are what they are.
Get into banking by doing a degree apprenticeship
The absolute earliest you can get a job in an investment bank is when you’re applying for university </news/finance/the-top-100-universities-globally-for-careers-in-banking-and-finance>.
Degree apprenticeships – during which you’re employed by the bank while you’re studying – only exist in the UK, and they’re insanely competitive to get into: one program participant in 2023 told us that </news/2023/09/banking-degree-apprenticeship> there were over 4,000 applicants to 28-degree apprenticeship roles at his bank last year, an acceptance rate of less than 0.7%. That’s on par with Goldman Sachs or JPMorgan’s “normal” internship acceptance rates.
It might be worth doing a degree internship though, even if you have to start preparing for a banking job at age 17. At the end of your four-year course, you have years of part-time experience, much better finances than your average university graduate, and a graduate offer to return to the firm as a full-time employee.
Degree apprenticeships have grown in popularity in recent years. Goldman Sachs, for example, launched its first one in London in 2016 <https://www.goldmansachs.com/pressroom/press-releases/2016/announcement-degree-apprenticeship-jan-2016>, and it expanded the program in October last year, announcing a partnership <https://www.goldmansachs.com/pressroom/press-releases/2025/degree-apprenticeship-programme-in-birmingham> with the University of Warwick in Birmingham. The firm offers degree apprenticeships in FICC and equities, engineering, and operations in London, as well as digital & technology solutions in Birmingham.
A number of banks offer degree apprenticeships. Aside from Goldman, they are also offered by JPMorgan, Deutsche Bank, HSBC, and Santander.
Get a banking internship as an undergraduate and convert it
Summer internships </news/internship-applications-banks> are the most typical way to get a job in an investment bank, be it a front- or back-office </news/front-office-middle-office-back-office-banking> job.
Among summer internships, the most numerous are summer analyst programs, which are meant for university/college students to do in their penultimate year of undergraduate study. Other types exist, and we’ll talk about them later, but it’s the summer analyst programs that are the gold standard of bank recruitment.
If you’re an undergraduate right now, most of your energy should be going into applying for these and getting into these.
Be warned, however – they are very competitive. The 0.7% figure in the introduction to this article is mostly comprised of summer internship applications. Data from UK-based student application Trackr suggested in April </news/how-many-intern-applications> that the average student who received an internship offer from an investment bank made over 100 applications on average.
What can help improve your odds? Going to the right university </news/target-universities-finance-uk>, for one. Playing the right kind of sport </news/best-sport-for-hedge-fund-job>. Having a bomb CV/resume </news/the-absolutely-perfect-investment-banking-cv>. Writing a fire cover letter </news/cover-letter-template-will-get-job-banking>. And – especially if you’re pursuing a career in Europe – getting a spring internship </news/spring-week-bank> or two under your belt.
Spring internships </news/spring-week-bank> are week-long taster sessions in your first year of university that, hopefully, can lead to receiving an offer to join a bank for a second-year summer internship, although not all banks offer this. They’re extremely important </news/internship-applications-banks>. Some banks offer similar “sophomore” internships in the US, but those are going out of fashion, as they are/were mostly used </news/bank-diversity-programs> for diversity recruitment.
Get a banking internship as a postgraduate and convert it
If you don't get into a summer analyst programme while you're at university, you can try when you've graduated. But this is a controversial topic.
If they don't get a job in a bank or financial services firm while at university, most students aspiring for a career in finance either try to get a masters in finance </news/the-top-30-masters-in-finance-for-getting-a-job-in-investment-banking> degree or a masters in financial engineering </news/top-masters-in-financial-engineering-courses> (MFE). It's worth noting, however, that doing a master’s degree (no matter that kind) still only gets you a summer analyst internship. You will be at the bottom of the bank’s hierarchy </news/banks-weird-hierarchies-analysts-associates-vps-mds>. Therefore, what you’re really doing is paying five or six figures and spending an extra one or two years to get an opportunity that you should have gotten well before.
Additionally, not all master’s in finance degrees </news/the-top-30-masters-in-finance-for-getting-a-job-in-investment-banking> are made equal. Former careers coach Mark Ross told us last year </news/masters-in-finance-worth-it> that a lot of degrees are “ripping off candidates” and “need to be shut down”. Many graduates of these programs say that they feel like their employment prospects were misrepresented to them, and that they’re unhappy with the impact their masters degree had on their careers.
Masters in financial engineering degrees are more mathematical and designed for quantitative careers. The gold standard is the one offered by Baruch College in New York, where the MFE can cost as little as $29k (if you're a New York State resident) and has a $217k median compensation for first-year graduates.
If you don't want to do an expensive masters, careers coach and former Morgan Stanley & Goldman Sachs intern Brian Landeros </news/how-to-get-a-goldman-sachs-internship> says that you can “buy time” by delaying your graduation.
That doesn’t mean failing exams or modules or whatever else – you can just delay the graduation date. The reason for that is simple: banks require you to have a graduation date no later or earlier than a certain time period to apply for an opportunity. If you can delay your graduation date, you can apply for next year’s internships. That might be better (and cheaper) than doing a master’s degree.
Get a banking internship as an MBA and convert it
Doing a two-year masters in business administration (MBA) and getting an internship as a summer associate during it was once the gold standard of getting an investment banking job.
Although the route is not as popular as it used to be, there are still opportunities to get on a summer associate internship during your MBA </news/mba-salaries-banking>, and converting that into a full-time job in the same way as if you were an undergraduate student.
And yes, it’s summer associate, not analyst </news/banks-weird-hierarchies-analysts-associates-vps-mds>. One of the big benefits of doing an MBA over other ways into banking is that you get to jump up the ladder </news/banks-weird-hierarchies-analysts-associates-vps-mds> a bit. But the biggest benefit is that you get to skip the abysmal work-life balance that analysts </news/banking-jobs-with-the-best-lifestyle> suffer - because you usually need a few years’ work experience to do an MBA, don’t consider it a way to start climbing the ladder earlier in life.
Like master’s degrees, not all MBAs are made equal. Banks are just as picky with who they hire from MBA programs as they are from undergrad ones, and there’s very much a hierarchy of top business schools </news/best-mba-for-finance-banking> that they recruit from.
Another benefit to starting your banking career with an MBA is that associates are better paid than analysts. A top MBA can lead to an investment banking job that pays up to </news/mba-salaries-banking> $350k in starting salary (although the median is usually around $175k). That's much higher than an analyst, who typically earns around </news/investment-banking-salaries-bonuses> $110k - $120k in salary their first year. Bonuses for associates are generally more generous, too.
Get a banking internship as a PhD and convert it
More studying? Well, if you’re this far down the list, you’ve already missed undergraduate, postgraduate, and MBA opportunities. What’s a few more years of studying to you? Besides, PhD holders are well-appreciated by banks, who itch for them to join their quantitative </news/quant-jobs-finance> research teams.
For example, Goldman Sachs is looking for a number of people to join its quant strats (sales & trading </news/how-do-you-get-a-job-in-sales-and-trading-in-a-bank>) team as summer associates in London.
Citi is also currently looking for a lot of PhDs in various roles. One of the roles, an AVP in risk decision model development <https://jobs.citi.com/job/mumbai/risk-decision-model-development-assistant-vice-president/287/93655063648>, is particularly curious – it notes that it’s either looking for seven years of experience, “or PhD degree in statistics, economics, or equivalent experience.” A PhD can jump a few more rungs up the banking ladder than a plain old MBA could.
Other banks have more interesting uses for PhDs. JPMorgan has a famous quantum computing team </news/jp-morgan-is-hiring-quantum-computing-ph-ds-on-260k-salaries-again>, and it hires a lot of PhDs for it, paying them salaries of up to $260k.
Not all quantitative positions requite PhDs, however. Jane Street, the electronic trading firm par excellence, does not exclusively hire PhDs </news/jane-street-employees-reveal-what-degrees-matter-when-applying-for-quant-jobs> – in fact, it mostly does not. “PhD or other research experience is a plus,” the firm wrote in a job opening for a quantitative researcher. “But ultimately, we’re more interested in how you think and learn, than what you currently know.”
Get a banking internship after you graduate
Okay, you might be starting to get desperate now. You should be: if you’ve done an undergraduate degree, a master’s degree, an MBA, and a PhD, congratulations: you’re the best-qualified person who still can’t get into banking alive today.
But let's roll the clocks back a bit. If you're graduated from your bachelors and masters degrees (and haven't committed to an MBA or PhD), two options exist for you to get that all-important internship: graduate schemes and off-cycle internships. A graduate scheme without prior internship experience is pretty much impossible, but off-cycle internships are still an option.
Off-cycle are longer (3 to 12 months long at UBS <https://www.ubs.com/global/en/careers/early-careers/off-cycle-internship-program.html>, for example) internships working as an analyst. You get some training, and then you're basically a full-time analyst who isn't there full-time. Off-cycle internships have had a bad rap over the years, being seen as cheap and disposable labour that was treated rather poorly. That’s less so the case these days: in fact, one Goldman Sachs off-cycle research intern </news/goldman-sachs-internship> in London received a credit in a research note alongside his senior colleagues. We talk more about off-cycle internships here </news/no-spring-internship-bank>.
Get a job in banking by joining the army
Admittedly a bit of an insane step to take for the sake of any internship, and probably one your mother would disapprove of, but there are plenty of examples of banks having specific outreach schemes for military veterans that you can apply to.
Most opportunities for veterans are in the US. JPMorgan <https://www.jpmorganchase.com/newsroom/stories/corporate-world-help-veterans-navigate-the-reverse-military-transition>, Morgan Stanley <https://www.morganstanley.com/people/experienced-professionals/veterans>, and Goldman Sachs <https://www.goldmansachs.com/careers/programs-for-professionals/veterans-integration-program> are all known to have special programs for military personnel transitioning into the private sector, as does Barclays <https://www.hiringourheroes.org/career-services/fellowships/internships/cfp/> (in the USA). Some banks, including JPMorgan, also have EMEA opportunities for veterans <https://www.jpmorganchase.com/careers/explore-opportunities/programs/military-uk>.
One of the most famous veterans recently in finance was Leo Lukenas </news/leo-lukenas>. A former green beret and Bank of America associate, Lukenas died of an "acute coronary artery thrombus," during a period in which he was allegedly working 120-hour weeks. His twin brother, Les Lukenas </news/goldman-sachs-hired-les-lukenas-twin-of-bof-a-associate-who-died-as-a-summer-intern>, who was also a green beret, was hired by Goldman Sachs after his brother's passing.
Start the CFA program
A bit of a hardcore option given the workload, but if you’re already in an established career, doing at least the CFA Level I will help show recruiters and banks that you’re serious about finance, as well as teach you some of the basic concepts that should apply directly to your future work.
Doing a CFA is particularly useful for international students </news/doing-cfa-level-i-student> or professionals </news/is-cfa-worth-it> seeking to move into a finance career, due to its perceived value to Western institutions. Many students also do the CFA Level I during their university years </news/doing-cfa-level-i-student> as social proof, too.
The downside (or upside, to an employer) of the CFA is that you do actually have to work for it - it is more than possible to fail any of the three stages. CFA Institute indicates <https://www.cfainstitute.org/sites/default/files/docs/programs/cfa-program/cfa-exam-results-pass-rate-infographic.pdf> that the pass rates for Levels I, II, and III were around 43%, 42%, and 50% respectively in the latest testing period available (November 2025).
Get a job in banking by coming in through accounting
Getting into banking via accounting takes a very long time, courtesy of all those years of extra studying you also have to do for the ACA/ACCA/etc.
Accountants also tend to be a bit of a desperate pick for banks. They’re usually brought in when dealmaking is booming to work on what seems like an endless supply of business. Alas, dealmaking is not exactly booming at the moment – not the way it was in 2021, at least.

Law isn’t as popular an entry point as it used to be, but it definitely has at least one significant previous example of success – former Goldman Sachs CEO Lloyds Blankfein, who left law to join a trading firm that was a subsidiary of Goldman’s as a trader. The 80s were a very different time compared to now, though.

Management consulting </news/pay-mckinsey-bain-bcg-big-four> is probably the best-positioned way to transition from a different industry into banking. item.title: Morning Coffee: Most desirable hedge fund offers employees a highly priced gift. JPMorgan deploys its $67m bankers on SpaceX IPO
item.description:
There is more than one way of making a lot of money when you work for a hedge fund </news/how-to-get-a-hedge-fund-job>. The second way comes after you have already made money the first way.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
The first way of making considerable money at a hedge fund is to be a portfolio manager </news/finance/portfolio-manager-job-hedge-fund-lifestyle> whose investment strategies pay off. At this point you will typically receive a bonus </news/hedge-fund-bonus> based on the percentage of the profits you generated for investors. This percentage was traditionally 20%. However, as funds battle for talent the percentage paid in bonuses has been rising. In 2025, it was nearly 25% </news/hedge-fund-bonus> at some top multistrategy </news/multistrategy-hedge-funds-hiring>hedge funds.
The second way is to reinvest some or all of this bonus in your employer's own funds. This is becoming increasingly common. At Citadel, </news/2022/10/how-to-get-a-job-at-citadel> for example, employees must invest 50% of their bonuses above a certain threshold in the firm's Wellington Fund for three and a half years.
Reinvesting your bonus can be lucrative. Since its inception in 1990, Citadel's Wellington Fund has generated average returns of nearly 20% a year. Last year, Citadel's Wellington Fund generated only half this amount, but $1m invested over three years would still become $1.35m even at 10% returns. Citadel employees presumably quite like this.
Now quantitative </news/how-to-get-a-quant-research-job-in-a-hedge-fund>hedge fund DE Shaw is also giving its employees a similar gift, but at a price.
Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-06-03/d-e-shaw-extends-client-exit-time-to-4-years-shuts-two-funds?srnd=undefined>reported yesterday that DE Shaw is starting a special new fund which only its staff will be allowed to invest in. The Financial Times <https://www.ft.com/content/f7270182-3c89-45a1-a670-39cf543847a4?syn-25a6b1a6=1> said this new fund will be called Phasor. Phasor will initially trade systematic equities and systematic futures.
Given that DE Shaw generated returns of over 18% in both 2024 and in 2025, investing your DE Shaw bonus in Phasor could a really great thing. However, it comes at a cost. DE Shaw will be charging its people a management fee of 4.5% to invest in Phasor and a performance fee 45%. If you're an employee who invests $1m in Phasor and it becomes $1.2m, you will need to give back around $99k to DE Shaw in fees.
That's a lot, particularly given that Citadel merely charges employees investing in its own Wellington Fund a performance fee of 20%, and that 20% is the industry standard. DE Shaw possibly figures that it's been twice as profitable recently and that charging employees 45% is therefore fine. It's also worth noting that since 2022, <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2022-10-03/hedge-fund-giant-d-e-shaw-plans-to-increase-fees-as-high-as-40>DE Shaw has also charged outside investors an unusually high performance fee of 30%-40%, and an unusually high management fee of between 2.5% and 3.5%. But still.
DE Shaw says the new employee fund with its new employee fees is designed to attract and retain talent in an increasingly aggressive hiring market. Instead of grumbling about the fees, employees may want to think only of the Renaissance Technologies Medallion Fund. This has been closed to outside investors since 2005 and has made some RenTech employees very, very rich.
DE Shaw employees may also want to overlook the fact that as well as creating the new employee fund, DE Shaw is simultaneously restricting withdrawals at its two key funds (Composite and Oculus) so that it will take investors four and three years respectively to get all their money out. Bloomberg says this reflects a "tightening" of liquidity terms across the whole hedge fund industry. It might also suggest that funds see more difficult times ahead.
Separately, JPMorgan did not spend 20 years wooing Elon Musk </news/goldman-sachs-david-solomon-on-the-art-of-wooing-elon-musk>and it does not have silver rockets in its lobby like Goldman Sachs for the SpaceX IPO, but it is one of the 23 other banks working on the listing and it's really making the most of that.
Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-06-03/jamie-dimon-to-pitch-jpmorgan-s-ultra-rich-clients-on-spacex-ipo>reports that both Jamie Dimon and Mary Callahan Erdoes, the CEO and the CEO of its asset and wealth management division, yesterday ran a "live interactive session" for wealth management clients on investing in next week's $1.75 trillion SpaceX IPO. Dimon and Callahan Erdoes are JPMorgan's most expensive people </news/jpmorgan-pay-executives> and earn $67m between them. Maybe they will personally donate to help get the one million person Martian colony underway. <https://www.ft.com/content/625ae455-3b7b-454e-82f3-b22414459cc0?syn-25a6b1a6=1>
Meanwhile...
Goldman Sachs is telling investors it expects revenues from SpaceX's AI business to increase 100 times by 2030. This would mean that Grok's models would have to surpass Anthropic, Google and OpenAI's and is despite the fact that SpaceX's AI business has underperformed rivals to date. (Financial Times) <https://www.ft.com/content/516cd0e5-a402-4b6c-8035-d688dc5f0cf4?syn-25a6b1a6=1>
Investors in Blackstone's $45bn Bcred Private Credit Fund, attempted to withdraw 10% of assets in Q2. It let them withdraw 5%. (Financial Times) <https://www.ft.com/content/4eca0255-27e7-47cc-8ebf-c9cc00c62e29?syn-25a6b1a6=1>
Cliffwater’s private credit fund aimed at retail clients received 17% redemption requests in Q2 and also limited withdrawals. (Financial Times) <https://www.ft.com/content/dfdfee64-d3fb-487a-9019-1926387b0ef1?syn-25a6b1a6=1>
Partners Group said redemption requests at its US private equity fund for wealthy individuals reached 6% in Q2. (FT) <https://www.ft.com/content/33a78e0c-39ea-4a72-b188-b19f753247e7?syn-25a6b1a6=1>
Deutsche Bank shares fell as much as 5.4% yesterday after the bank said it's setting aside an extra €100 million ($116 million) to cover non performing loans in Q2. (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-06-03/deutsche-bank-to-set-aside-more-than-expected-for-souring-credit>
Schonfeld is opening a Swiss office and it hired Yohan Hwang and Pierre Kling to work there. They were at Millennium. (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-06-04/schonfeld-plans-zurich-office-hires-from-rival-millennium?utm_source=website&utm_medium=share&utm_campaign=twitter>
Mirco Bulega, </news/mirco-bulega-macro> who left ExodusPoint, is going to Millennium. (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-06-04/exoduspoint-partner-bulega-to-join-rival-hedge-fund-millennium?utm_source=website&utm_medium=share&utm_campaign=twitter>
London added 67,000 jobs in banking and finance since Brexit. (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/opinion/articles/2026-06-04/a-decade-after-brexit-the-city-of-london-is-fine?taid=6a20f96eeb9d510001ebdc57&utm_campaign=trueanthem&utm_content=business&utm_medium=social&utm_source=twitter>
Citi has got a new chief of staff in the form of Margo Pilic for its 'next phase of our strategy and growth.' Tim Karpoff, its former head of strategy and M&A is leaving the firm after overseeing a period of many job cuts. Karpoff says it was a lot of fun. (Barrons) <https://www.barrons.com/articles/citi-stock-new-strategy-hire-97fa7351>
Barclays surveyed 410 buyside investors across North America, EMEA and Asia and only 7% said they're planning meaningful reductions in staff because of AI. (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-06-04/ai-isn-t-replacing-credit-hedge-fund-traders-yet-barclays-says>
David Solomon </news/goldman-sachs-david-solomon-on-the-art-of-wooing-elon-musk> is saying densely worded things about the impact of AI on engineering jobs at Goldman Sachs. “You’re going to see nuanced changes that probably to some degree reduce the number of people that we start with over the next few years.” (Bloomberg) < https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-06-04/goldman-ceo-says-ai-may-halt-steep-rise-in-banks-engineer-ranks>
Bank of America hired 4,000 summer interns </news/internship-investment-bank> and full-time campus recruits this year, split roughly evenly between the two. This was the same as last year. Only 0.8% of applicants were accepted. AI enabled more people to apply. (Business Insider) <https://www.businessinsider.com/bank-of-america-summer-interns-wall-street-ai-2026-6>
Kirkland & Ellis has agreed a multiyear deal with Palantir to develop its own AI technology. Last year its lawyers received communication training after it was discovered they could be uncooperative. The AI will embed the new “different cultural touch." (Financial Times) <https://www.ft.com/content/4243f9c0-63eb-48d8-b395-bd1ca5cc37a2?syn-25a6b1a6=1>
OpenAI solved a famous maths problem that humans had been unable to solve for a century. It took less than 32 hours and $1k in tokens. (WSJ) <https://www.wsj.com/tech/ai/ai-math-solves-erdos-problem-openai-c4029e84?mod=e2tw>
Rob Priestley, a former VP in operational risk management at Deutsche Bank is a barista after making 100 unsuccessful job applications. “I’ve got children to support, financial commitments everywhere, and here I am: unemployed. Who is going to take on a 53-year-old in a fiercely competitive corporate environment?” (Telegraph) <https://www.telegraph.co.uk/money/consumer-affairs/worked-in-finance-28-years-redundancy-now-barista/>
Follow me on X <https://x.com/MadameButcher>. Follow me on LinkedIn. <https://www.linkedin.com/in/sarahbutcherjournalist/>
Have a confidential story, tip, or comment you’d like to share? Contact: +44 7537 182250 (SMS, Whatsapp or voicemail). Telegram: @SarahButcher. Signal: sarahbutcher.22 Click here to fill in our anonymous form </about/editor-tips>, or email editortips@efinancialcareers.com.

item.title: If you want one of Millennium's 2027 investment internships you are already too late
item.description:
Earlier this year, hedge fund </news/how-to-get-a-hedge-fund-job>Millennium </news/how-to-get-a-job-at-hedge-fund-millennium> launched a new investment internship program commencing in 2027. Given that various hedge funds are currently recruiting </news/hedge-fund-internships-london> interns for next year, you might think now's the time to apply.
It's not.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Places on Millennium's investment internship program have been filled already.
Millennium declined to comment for this article. Business Insider <https://www.businessinsider.com/inside-the-hedge-fund-hiring-frenzy-career-ladder-talent-wars-2026-2>reported on the launch of Millennium's investment internship in February. At that time, Business Insider said the fund was thinking of hiring 20 "investment obsessed college students" globally. In fact, it's hired 30. They will be dispersed between Millennium's offices New York, London and Hong Kong.
Millennium's investment internship is a highly desirable entity. Interns on the program will work directly with Millennium's senior portfolio managers and be considered for full time analyst </news/analyst-hedge-fund> roles if they sufficiently impress.
It's not clear how many people applied for the positions exactly, but it's understood that a large number of high calibre candidates very quickly put themselves forward for the investment internship roles. The average acceptance rate for Millennium's other internships, which are in areas like engineering, quantitative </news/quant-jobs-finance> research </news/what-is-a-quant-researcher-hedge-fund> and operations, </news/operations-jobs-banking> is just 0.5%. </news/millennium-internships-hedge-fund>The firm will begin accepting applications to these roles from August.
If you've missed the investment internship opportunities at Millennium for 2027, Citadel </news/2020/04/citadel-datathon>is still advertising for equity analysis interns. </news/hedge-fund-internships-london>Various other hedge funds listed here </news/hedge-funds-with-graduate-training-programmes>offer internships too, although not all will give you a clear path to portfolio management. This was presumably why Millennium's roles were so popular.
Follow me on X <https://x.com/MadameButcher>. Follow me on LinkedIn. <https://www.linkedin.com/in/sarahbutcherjournalist/>

item.title: 42-year old ex-Deutsche Bank AVP retires a billionaire
item.description:
There is a veritable list of billionaires who started their careers in banking: Jamie Dimon, Stephen Schwarzman, Leon Black, Paul Taubman, and many others. The list of former banking technologists </news/how-to-get-a-technology-job-bank> to have become billionaires is much smaller – but it grew by one.
Click here to join the bubble by eFinancialCareers, our new anonymous community. <https://bubble.efinancialcareers.com/login?_gl=1*ut8n6u*_gcl_au*MTg4MDY2NTExNi4xNzYxNTU3NDIz*_ga*ODczNzI2NTA0LjE3NTM2OTk2ODI.*_ga_NZFQV4MXPM*czE3NjM2NDM0MDYkbzI1NiRnMSR0MTc2MzY0MzQzOSRqMjckbDAkaDMyMjc4OTUzMw..>✍️
Vladyslav Yatsenko is stepping down as CTO of Revolut. Yatsenko co-founded Revolut in 2014 with Nik Storonsky, serving as CTO at the firm from its inception. He held a slew of roles before that, none longer than three years, including as an AVP in market risk for Deutsche Bank, as a senior Java developer at UBS, and as a senior software engineer at Credit Suisse.
Pay for banking technologists can be pretty good. Our 2026 Compensation & Lifestyle Report </banking-compensation-guide> found that it can even reach $720k per year </banking-compensation-guide> – a tidy sum. But Yatsenko is far ahead of what any bank technologist in the world can earn, no matter how successful. Forbes listed him <https://www.forbes.com/profile/vlad-yatsenko/> as having a net worth of $2.2bn, which is derived from his ~3% stake in Revolut, which was estimated in November 2025 to be worth $75bn.
An official statement from Revolut said that Yatsenko would transition to a role as non-executive director on 1 July. “We are pleased that Vlad will continue to lend his expertise to the company,” the firm said.
Yatsenko’s replacement as VP of technology is Revolut’s head of technology, Donato Lucia, who does not have any financial services experience. Instead, the Italian – who is around 38 years old – has credits at more traditional tech companies: HP and IBM, as well as online travel site lastminute.com.
Lucia joined Revolut in 2018 as a senior software engineer and was named with 16 people in the firm’s first global partnership round. An advert published in the Financial Times <https://x.com/TommyTudehope/status/1421376388104441860/photo/1> by Revolut at the time curiously foresaw Lucia’s place in Revolut’s executive team: “The partnership lays the foundation for a senior leaders to propel our business forward in the years to come.” Prophetic indeed.
Have a confidential story, tip, or comment you’d like to share? Contact: +44 7537 182250 (SMS, WhatsApp or voicemail). Telegram: @SarahButcher. Signal: sarahbutcher.22 Click here to fill in our anonymous form </about/editor-tips>, or email editortips@efinancialcareers.com.

item.title: Citadel Securities’ youthful Zurich office grew, courtesy of Google
item.description:
In a time when tech firms are cutting back <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-06-04/us-tech-sector-announces-most-job-cuts-in-nearly-two-years> like they haven’t in years, their very best people can still find their places in the world – such as at the world’s largest electronic market making firms.
Click here to join the bubble by eFinancialCareers, our new anonymous community. <https://bubble.efinancialcareers.com/login?_gl=1*ut8n6u*_gcl_au*MTg4MDY2NTExNi4xNzYxNTU3NDIz*_ga*ODczNzI2NTA0LjE3NTM2OTk2ODI.*_ga_NZFQV4MXPM*czE3NjM2NDM0MDYkbzI1NiRnMSR0MTc2MzY0MzQzOSRqMjckbDAkaDMyMjc4OTUzMw..>✍️
Ferenc Tóth joined Citadel Securities in Zurich this week as a software engineer in its platform research team. He arrived from Google, where he spent seven years, also in Zurich, and was most recently a staff engineer.
Tóth might find himself around a few familiar faces – CitSec’s Zurich office is flush with ex-Googlers. His immediate boss, platform research head Costas Bekas, is a former IBM distinguished researcher, though. Hedge funds </news/how-to-get-a-hedge-fund-job> are fond of IBM’s machine learning team; veteran Vaibhava Goel joined Millennium </news/hedge-fund-millennium-hires-ibm-watson-veteran-to-run-machine-learning> in New York last year.
Citadel Secruties’ Zurich office is as good as they come. Founded in 2019, it was initially led by award winning statistician <https://math.ethz.ch/news-and-events/news/d-math-news/2016/08/nicolai-meinshausen-copss-award.html> Nicolai Meinshausen, who took a brief break from the firm in 2021 before returning in August 2025 as head of principal research.
Despite the Zurich office’s small footprint, it’s an outsized employee of local quant </news/quant-jobs-finance> talent. Of the 15 open internships </news/hedge-fund-internships-london> on Citadel Securities’ website, three are in Zurich – including for quantitative research </news/what-is-a-quant-researcher-hedge-fund>, as either an analyst, PhD, or engineer.

item.title: Goldman Sachs' David Solomon on the art of wooing Elon Musk
item.description:
Goldman Sachs has got the lead role </news/space-x-ipo-goldman-sachs-morgan-stanley> on the SpaceX IPO. Morgan Stanley has not. To mark this achievement, Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-06-04/goldman-erects-lobby-rockets-as-morgan-stanley-ipo-rivalry-heats-up?utm_medium=email&utm_source=author_alert&utm_term=260604&utm_campaign=author_21835441> says that Goldman Sachs has erected some silver rockets in its lobby. Morgan Stanley has got some SpaceX branding on its turnstiles. Someone at Morgan Stanley suggested to the Financial Times <https://www.ft.com/content/10cecc4e-dbce-477d-9f10-068581c6e650?syn-25a6b1a6=1>that Morgan Stanley only seems subordinate to Goldman on the SpaceX deal because Goldman Sachs comes first alphabetically; maybe not.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Speaking to Bloomberg's Odd Lots <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-06-04/goldman-sachs-ceo-david-solomon-on-running-a-bank-in-the-age-of-ai?srnd=phx-oddlots>podcast, Goldman Sachs' CEO David Solomon </news/david-solomon-goldman-sachs> said Goldman's lead role in financing mankind's foray to Mars is the result of 20 years' schmoozing of Elon Musk. Rumours that Solomon won the deal by sliding into Musk's DMs turn out to be apocryphal.
"I never asked Elon in a text message about, about the IPO," said Solomon, who went back through his Musk DMs specifically to check this. He did send Musk messages about a "whole variety of different things," though.
Those messages were not what won Elon for Goldman. That was the result of "a cumulative effort of lots and lots of people over 20 years," said Solomon. Some of that effort came from Stuart Bernstein, a former Goldman Sachs partner who retired after 31 years in 2018. In a piece of foresightful flattery 13 years ago, Bernstein declared <https://www.washingtonpost.com/business/economy/teslas-feat-of-financial-engineering/2013/09/20/c4d4667e-e26a-11e2-a11e-c2ea876a8f30_story.html>: "We think Elon Musk is one of the most exciting entrepreneurs of the century. He's got qualities of Henry Ford, Steve Jobs and Bill Gates all rolled up in one."
When Bernstein left Goldman, Solomon said head of banking Dan Dees </news/dan-dees-goldman-sachs> started building "a personal relationship" and a "wonderful dialogue" with Musk. Like Kim Posnet </news/space-x-ipo-goldman-sachs-morgan-stanley>t, Dees is high on charisma; he has a hoodie named after him by Gwyneth Paltrow.
Dees' relationship with Musk appears to have survived Dees' 2023 comment <https://cms.efinancialcareers.com/cms/content-manager/collectionType/api::article.article/60922>to a jury that he was "surprised" by a Musk Tweet of 2018 in which Musk said he was thinking of taking Tesla private. Musk was subsequently acquitted of securities fraud in relation to this Tweet. Following the surprise revelation that Musk was considering the take-private deal, Dees reportedly told him: “At GS, we are standing by and ready to help...We are ready to fly in and share some views and dig in to help whenever you’re ready.”
Speaking on the Podcast this week, Solomon said winning the SpaceX deal was about more than Goldman bankers ingratiating themselves with Musk. It was also about Goldman bankers ingratiating themselves with SpaceX CEO Brett Johnson, whom Solomon says is "a hugely, hugely important guy."
Goldman has "had people spending time" with Johnson "for years and years and years," proclaimed Solomon. "That's what our, that's what our business is...building trusting relationships over time with people, giving the right advice, taking a long-term view, not being transactional, and trying to earn trust."
If Morgan Stanley wants some rockets of its own, it may need to do the same.
Image: Joe Rogan Experience/YouTube

item.title: How to stay healthy in a banking job when “everything is urgent”
item.description:
Sophia Mullins knows what it’s like to work in financial services. She also knows what it’s like to work in financial services, to burn out, and to do something different. She is trying to impart her learnings to other people.
Mullins, who is now based in Miami, spent nearly two years working as an investment banking </news/what-is-an-investment-bank> analyst </news/banks-weird-hierarchies-analysts-associates-vps-mds> at Barclays in New York City, nearly five years in investor relations in private equity </news/how-to-get-a-job-in-private-equity> and a year at Third Point, the hedge fund </news/how-to-get-a-hedge-fund-job> run by Dan Loeb.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Now Mullins runs Wall Street Wellness LLC, which she describes as a “concierge wellness consultancy” helping high achieving individuals thrive and stay healthy in demanding jobs. This includes financial services professionals like Mullins’ former self.
Mullins admits a propensity for overworking. “I have a tendency to get my head down for 10 hours without checking in on my own mental wellbeing,” she says. When she worked in banking and private equity, this translated to jumping at every request and driving herself into the ground. “Everything is urgent in finance, but I was taking that urgency to the next level. I was constantly sprinting through deadlines and leaning into the spring mentality with no reprieve,” says Mullins. “It was sending me into physical and mental burnout.”
She became ill. Mullins lost weight and was exhausted. Her lymph nodes swelled. She was screened for lymphoma. “Fortunately, I didn’t have that but it was scary being in my early 20s and hearing the C word,” she recalls.
In fact, Mullins had Hashimoto’s disease, an autoimmune condition where the body attacks the thyroid gland. It’s often hereditary but can be triggered by chronic stress. Mullins says she initially wrote off her symptoms as the result of working in a high stress environment. “For example, I was tired and my energy levels were inconsistent,” she tells us.
The solutions to burnout aren’t simple and Mullins isn’t alone in propagating them. Lucy Bullman, a London-based coach who was previously Morgan Stanley’s head of EMEA client onboarding, works with banks and hedge funds helping employees build sustainable careers. “There’s growing openness around the fact that high performance isn’t just about technical capability,” she says. “People are talking much more honestly about cognitive overload, sleep disruption, anxiety and the challenge of maintaining perspective in very demanding environments.”
Much like Goldman Sachs </news/how-to-get-a-job-goldman-sachs>’ CEO David Solomon </news/david-solomon-goldman-sachs>, Bullman advises her clients to get a life outside of work. “The senior portfolio managers </news/finance/portfolio-manager-job-hedge-fund-lifestyle> I work with now often emphasise the importance of not over-indexing on your career”, she says. “When identity and self-worth are too closely tied to professional performance, drawdowns can feel disproportionately destabilising. Having perspective outside work helps build longer-term resilience.”
Bullman is not a fan of Mullins’ former method of jumping at every command. “If you approach a finance career at the pace of a sprint, it becomes very difficult to sustain over the long term,” she says. “The people who perform best tend to think much more strategically about sustainability and recovery.”
These days, Mullins is a reformed character. Having been diagnosed with Hashimoto’s, she says became “aware that as a high achiever I was often pouring fuel on the fire.” Lifestyle changes resulted in a less combustible lifestyle. “I started journaling in the morning and waking up earlier than I had to have a morning walk and nutritious breakfast. I started to allow myself to take time out.”
Mullins now works with clients to encourage them to take their health more seriously. Moving from banking to private equity is often seen as a panacea for overwork but Mullins says this is a misconception. “Private equity was more stressful than banking. I was working in investor relations and covering multiple time zones and my hours were often even longer than banking,” she tells us. “I was working with prospective investors all over the world and often burning the midnight oil.”
If you don’t address your health, no one else will do it for you.
Sophia Mullins will be answering your questions about jobs in banking, private equity and hedge funds and discussing how to prevent and survive burnout in the Bubble <https://bubble.efinancialcareers.com/>until June 11th.

item.title: Morning Coffee: JPMorgan employees began spending huge amounts of money. How to behave when you didn't win a place on the big IPOs
item.description:
Some bankers have always seen their expense account as part of their compensation; others have made it a matter of honour to do more client entertainment than anyone else. But even the most profligate and high-rolling Managing Director would have to be impressed by the employees at JPMorgan <https://www.semafor.com/newsletter/06/03/2026/semafor-tech-lets-get-physical?utm_source=newslettershare&utm_medium=technology&utm_campaign=flagshipnumbered2#d> who are running up company tabs that exceed their own salary.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
They’re not spending it on food, drink, golf or any other kind of entertainment, though – these are middle office </news/front-office-middle-office-back-office-banking>professionals working in the Payments division, and they are spending the money on AI tokens. Zachary Anderson, the division’s chief data and analytics officer, told Semafor <https://www.semafor.com/newsletter/06/03/2026/semafor-tech-lets-get-physical?utm_source=newslettershare&utm_medium=technology&utm_campaign=flagshipnumbered2#d>it's already having a noticeable effect on his budget.
Several companies appear to be running into trouble from “tokenmaxxing <https://www.businessinsider.com/uber-coo-andrew-macdonald-ai-token-spending-harder-justify-2026-5>” at the moment. The problem seems to be that lots of employees perceive a need to demonstrate their talents with AI technology, before cost controls have been put in place. Anderson claims that JPM “isn’t the type” of company that has AI leaderboards to encourage maxxers, but it does monitor usage, and that might be all that’s needed to create an incentive not to end up on the “naughty list </news/jp-morgan-ai-naughty-list>” of sceptics and refusers.
At the other end of the scale, there is considerable demand from the AI true believers. Writing code yourself can be demanding and boring. And while honing prompts isn’t necessarily thrilling, it’s at least a novel way to work, and one which lets you feel that you’re on the cutting edge of something. It also, potentially, gives you an extra excuse if there’s an error, which is always a useful thing to have.
So although Anderson says that there is no company-wide effort to ration the use of CoPilot, Claude and the rest, one might become necessary before long. The implementation of AI was meant to replace employees and reduce costs, not give them a new way to waste money.
It's conceivable that AI tokens might end up subject to the same level of scrutiny as any other kind of expenses claim. You’ll need to demonstrate that it was for a valid business purpose, and be restricted to a relatively measly stipend for most applications. If that doesn’t work, then AI coding tools for techies could become the equivalent of Bloomberg Terminal subscriptions for front office bankers – extremely expensive status symbols that are only handed out to a small number of elite managers, who hardly use them but would cut throats and start wars rather than be deprived. The ordinary employees may end up being reduced to having to queue up to use the department’s single Claude subscription.
Elsewhere, Goldman Sachs and Morgan Stanley seem to be setting up a fantastic revenue quarter courtesy of the Big Tech industry. They are leading the SpaceX IPO </news/space-x-ipo-goldman-sachs-morgan-stanley>, leading the Anthropic IPO <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-06-03/anthropic-said-to-pick-morgan-stanley-goldman-sachs-to-lead-ipo?utm_medium=email&utm_source=author_alert&utm_term=260603&utm_campaign=author_23597220> and they’re bookrunners for the $85bn secondary issue from Alphabet. But what about the banks that didn’t get into the elite syndicate?
Jefferies, for example <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-06-03/shorting-spacex-jefferies-becomes-go-to-bank-after-missing-ipo?srnd=qatar-economic-forum&embedded-checkout=true>, might have reasonably felt disappointed that they weren’t in the SpaceX deal at all, not even in a lowly role. They are a top ten firm for global equity IPOs, after all, and have hired good tech bankers like Becky Steinthal and Stefani Silverstein. Ironically, this might have been the reason for their exclusion; bulge bracket banks don’t always like to give their near competitors a chance to show what they can do, and often prefer to be in a syndicate with regional and second tier players, so that there’s not so much overlap in the clients they’re talking to.
But there’s no use crying over spilt milk, and sometimes there is good business to be written by outsiders to a big deal. Although the company is diplomatic, saying that it is “not promoting any shorting activity regarding SpaceX or any IPO”, Jefferies’ traders will be keenly aware that if someone were to want to bet against Elon Musk, they aren’t conflicted out. They will also be able to deal with investors who want to “flip” their shares immediately when SpaceX starts trading.
Schadenfreude is not an attractive emotion. Although there is a little bit of every banker that wants to see a competitor’s deal fail, it would be quite bad for the overall market if the tech mega-deals were to faceplant. But, in that unfortunate occurrence, some traders might reflect that there’s good wine that can be made out of sour grapes.
Meanwhile …
“We are blown away about the junior talent we can hire. They are so AI native, they use these tools as if the world had never existed without them. So perhaps a bit counter to many narratives out there, we have not reduced our junior intake, and we are very happy with that”. The CEO of BCG is feeling bullish – revenue was up 7% last year and the demand for AI consulting is “insatiable”. (WSJ <https://www.wsj.com/tech/ai/ai-is-changing-how-consultants-get-paidand-much-more-bcgs-ceo-says-c009b111?st=9u5Cg2&reflink=desktopwebshare_permalink>)
Bank of America isn’t quite so effusive about this year’s crop of university students (“we need young people”, says Brian Moynihan), but it is also still recruiting – a class of 2,000 summer interns and 2,000 graduate recruits are set to join. (Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-06-03/bank-of-america-bucks-ai-job-fears-with-2-000-summer-interns?utm_medium=email&utm_source=author_alert&utm_term=260603&utm_campaign=author_19908303>)
How big is the private credit </news/private-credit> market? It depends on whether you include the entire banking industry, mortgages and trade finance. (FT Alphaville <https://www.ft.com/content/dcc7c956-858c-4fed-9820-f87f73f7bf8d?shareType=nongift>)
Fully one third of the employees at Revolut work on fraud prevention, and that is with the assistance of an AI-powered tool that can process money-laundering queries twelve times faster than a human. (Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/features/2026-06-02/how-revolut-s-founder-is-building-a-76-billion-fortune?cmpid=060326_morningemea&utm_campaign=morningemea&utm_medium=email&utm_source=newsletter&utm_term=260603&utm_content=5148>)
The Financial Conduct Authority has warned EPL clubs that they aren’t exempt from the rules on financial promotion, as a surprising number of them have shirt sponsorship from crypto and trading firms. (Financial News <https://www.fnlondon.com/articles/fca-warns-football-clubs-against-dodgy-sponsorship-deals-3d3b262b?mod=homepage>)
Some bankers want “to feel that their outward appearance reflects their capability and energy”, others are concerned about “feeling overlooked in environments where youth is often associated with innovation, productivity and adaptability”. Or just possibly, they are going for lunchtime Botox appointments because they want to look good? (FT <https://www.ft.com/content/3089e2a9-27d8-4176-ab9b-582f659ddd11?syn-25a6b1a6=1>)

item.title: The mystery of the MIT professor who joined the secretive trading firm (maybe)
item.description:
Where is Yufei Zhao, a popular MIT associate math professor who once won a gold at an international mathematics Olympiad? Not, it seems, at MIT. Maybe, it seems, at secretive electronic trading firm TGS Management. </news/tgs-management-company-llc>
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
We don't know that Zhao is definitely at TGS because TGS didn't respond to our email. Nor did Zhao. Nor does Zhao indicate that he's at TGS on his resume <https://yufeizhao.com/cv.pdf>, nor on LinkedIn. However, multiple sources tell us that Zhao has gone to TGS and former colleagues confirm that he's certainly no longer at MIT.
It's not clear when Zhao (apparently) joined TGS: it may have been over a year ago. Google Scholar <https://scholar.google.com/citations?hl=en&user=H1vNRiUAAAAJ&view_op=list_works&sortby=pubdate> suggests that he hasn't published any new academic papers since early last year. Nor has he said much on Twitter for a while, even though he was once <https://x.com/yufeizhao> very active there.
Zhao may be holed up in an underground bunker. Or he may be in TGS Management's offices in Irving or Princeton. Unconfirmed reports suggest the firm, which has been described as the OG of quant trading and "mid-frequency zero," expects its employees to maintain zero presence on social media. If you join, digital invisibility follows.
This means that we also don't know if Zhao has hired any of his former students or vice versa. - There are suggestions (again unconfirmed) that a former student may have recommended Zhao.
To the extent that Zhao is at TGS, his presence might be taken as another indication of the kind of person the quant trading firm likes to hire. Zhao has won an array of math competitions alongside the Olympiad. He's also received an array of academic scholarships, fellowships and honourable mentions. As TGS expands into its twice as large new office in Irvine, people like Zhao might be who it's looking for.

item.title: Hedge fund Qube Research hired an ex-Citadel Securities trader as its US COO
item.description:
Qube Research and Technologies </news/qrt-hedge-fund> rose to prominence over the past few years by hiring hundreds of people, </news/qube-research-and-technologies-pay-quant-hedge-fund> all the while staying out of the US. The UK/France based quant </news/quant-jobs-finance> fund, which returned ~30% last year, <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-01-08/qube-s-main-hedge-fund-gained-30-torus-made-20-last-year> changed that by opening multiple US office since last summer. Qube has also been pushing into electronic market making, <https://rupakghose.substack.com/p/the-rise-of-qrt#:~:text=Meanwhile%2C%20quant%20hedge,than%20research%20ideas.>and its latest New York hire made her name at one of the biggest players in that space.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Holly Lau joined Qube as its US COO, based in New York. She previously spent eight years at Citadel Securities, where she was a senior trader and head of delta one and ADR. Lau left Citadel Securities in 2024; she was most recently COO at Summit Securities, a New York-based market maker </news/high-frequency-trading-hiring-and-pay> and prop trading firm.
Qube already had a very small New York office. It also has a very small office in Chicago and a separate commodities trading entity called Q-TX based in Houston.
Qube has 16 openings in the New York, including an FPGA engineer and a crypto C++ developer. The roles offer salaries of up to $280k and $270k, respectively. Qube only has one opening in Chicago, also for an FPGA engineer, while Q-TX has three including one internship.

item.title: Now London bankers are wondering why that JPMorgan guy didn't go to Citi
item.description:
As we observed here </news/jpmorgan-didnt-make-md>yesterday, Shameer Patel, a mainstay on the JPMorgan equity capital markets </news/capital-markets-jobs-ecm-and-dcm> (ECM) syndicate desk for many years, who was nonetheless only an executive director, is going to BNP Paribas. There, he will become head of ECM syndicate globally.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
It's quite a step-up. We have not spoken to Shameer, but his motivation for the move was presumably the fact that he was offered an enormous promotion at BNP Paribas involving a role that was reportedly created especially for him.
Despite this, some of Patel's former colleagues have been querying his choice of destination. If he wanted to leave JPMorgan, they say he should have tried Citi. </news/is-citi-preparing-for-another-hot-summer-of-jp-morgan-hires>After all, Citi is now home to various ex-JPMorgan ECM bankers, including Vis Raghavan </news/vis-raghavan-fell-out-with-plenty-of-people-at-jp-morgan-but-he-was-worth-it>, Achtinya Mangla </news/achintya-mangla-citi>, Aloke Gupte </news/aloke-gupte-jpmorgan>and Bernal Vargas, among many others. Citi would have been a home from home. Even Ed Sankey, </news/hsbc-lost-best-banker-in-world-as-researchers-wait-to-learn-their-fates> the "best ECM banker in the world," whom Citi hired from HSBC and who previously spent 15 years at Deutsche Bank, worked with Vis at JPMorgan in the very distant past. Citi is also hiring: Vis Raghavan said last month </news/citi-investor-day-2026> that he wants to increase senior banker headcount by 15% this year.
So why hasn't Patel gone to Citi too? One former colleague suggests it's because Patel is not one of Vis and Achtinya's people. Another suggests it's because the lure of higher pay and a bigger title at BNP Paribas was so great. Another suggests the non-solicitation agreements signed by people like Aloke Gupte won't lapse until the end of this year and that Citi can't hire ECM people directly from JPMorgan until this happens.
JPMorgan and Citi declined to comment for this article. One former colleague of Patel said he's a talented banker who might have chosen Citi were it not for BNP Paribas' brilliant offer and the fact that Citi is already "full" with some of the most experienced and talented ECM bankers you will find in the industry. He said many of these very talented bankers have been looking askance at the FT's recent article on Vis Raghavan <https://www.ft.com/content/6584b0fc-ea9e-4a0d-80ea-dde8b1063545?syn-25a6b1a6=1> and have concluded that JPMorgan is just unhappy because one of its "best bankers left." - "It is not worthwhile even reading it," he said.
