item.title: Hedge funds are opening up internships in London for 2027
item.description:
If you want to work in a hedge fund </news/how-to-get-a-hedge-fund-job>, you have two paths. The more common one is to be a top trader in a bank’s sales & trading </news/how-do-you-get-a-job-in-sales-and-trading-in-a-bank> team and move over. This is common, by industry standards. The alternative is to find a hedge fund internship </news/hedge-funds-with-graduate-training-programmes>. These are less common – but, if you’re a student in London, your chance to find one for next year has just opened.
Click here to join the bubble by eFinancialCareers, our new anonymous community. <https://bubble.efinancialcareers.com/login?_gl=1*ut8n6u*_gcl_au*MTg4MDY2NTExNi4xNzYxNTU3NDIz*_ga*ODczNzI2NTA0LjE3NTM2OTk2ODI.*_ga_NZFQV4MXPM*czE3NjM2NDM0MDYkbzI1NiRnMSR0MTc2MzY0MzQzOSRqMjckbDAkaDMyMjc4OTUzMw..>✍️
The largest batch of internships that have recently appeared at quant fund D.E. Shaw <https://www.deshaw.com/careers/internships>. The firm is currently offering three internships in London in 2027: a systems engineering one, an investor relations one, and a trading/analyst one. It’s also offering a “systems administrator” industry placement year.
Point72 <https://careers.point72.com/?experience=early%20career;internships&location=london> is offering a number of internships too, for both quantitative research and development both. Both internships can be done in London, New York, and Paris. The quantitative research opportunity is also available in Tokyo.
Multi-strategy fund Capula <https://apply.workable.com/capula-investment-management-ltd/j/A15A62A8BE/> is also offering a trading and research summer internship, based in London. And, not a hedge fund, but electronic trading firm Aquatic Capital Management <https://job-boards.greenhouse.io/aquaticcapitalmanagement/jobs/8489186002> has two open internships – including for a quantitative research intern in summer 2027, either in Chicago or London.
Another hedge fund that appears to have internships open in London is Citadel <https://www.citadel.com/careers/open-opportunities?experience-filter=internships&location-filter=london&selected-job-sections=388,389,387,390&current_page=1&sort_order=DESC&per_page=10&action=careers_listing_filter>. The firm currently has four listed internships in the UK, two of which can also be done in Paris or Zurich. There are two roles open for quantitative research, one for software engineering, and one for equities (analysis). The listings do not have listed dates on them nor any suggestion as to whether they are holdovers from last year.
And there are some internships that have already opened and closed. Millennium </news/millennium-investment-internship>, for example, opened its investment internship back in February for London, Hong Kong, and New York. It's since been filled by 30 young aspirants and closed - more than the 20 that Business Insider reported <https://www.businessinsider.com/inside-the-hedge-fund-hiring-frenzy-career-ladder-talent-wars-2026-2> earlier in the year that it was looking for.
Some internships are also open outside of the UK. Balyasny <https://bambusdev.my.site.com/s/> and Bridgewater <https://job-boards.greenhouse.io/bridgewater89/jobs/8395041002> have internships available, in Hong Kong for quantitative research and New York City for investments, respectively. And Florida-based quant fund Voloridge Investment Management <https://voloridge-investment-management.hiringthing.com/job/1010132/quantitative-research-intern-2027> also has a number of internships open in its Jupiter, Florida headquarters. Voloridge notes being “five minutes from the beach” as a benefit.

item.title: The wit and wisdom of David Solomon at Goldman Sachs: "I was extremely emotional"
item.description:
David Solomon </news/kathy-ruemmler-goldman-sachs>, CEO of Goldman Sachs </news/how-to-get-a-job-goldman-sachs>, does not always seem like a laid back person. During the high stress events that are Goldman Sachs' quarterly investor calls, he can sometimes seem dry and abrupt. It turns out this is not always so. Speaking last week to students at the 2026 Wharton MBA graduation event <https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=PSop6XzzY9w>, Solomon was warm and witty and treated his audience to an upbeat house music track created by AI.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
When he began his career back in the "prehistoric days" of the 1980s, Solomon said he had "long flowing hair" and was able to astonish clients with his "brilliance" by spending six hours researching historical stock prices in the Wall Street Journal and plotting them on graph paper. He was eager and ambitious.
He could also be volatile. "I was extremely emotional," said Solomon, recalling a time in his early 30s when he was working at Bear Stearns and a deal collapsed. It felt like a "spectacular failure," he recalled. Solomon had a mentor in the form of Richard Metrick, one of Bear Stearns' most senior investment bankers. He stormed into Metrick's office "all bent out of shape" and with his arms waving and shouted "Yes!" when Metrick asked him if he wanted to talk about the failed deal. Metrick told him to calm down and come back in. It was a lesson in self-control.
On another occasion, Solomon said he was a 29-year-old investment banker at Bear Stearns and was trying to ingratiate himself with an "impressive entrepreneur" planning to shake up Las Vegas casinos. The entrepreneur was invited for lunch at Bear Stearns but turned up two hours late and then left in a huff when he was served sandwiches. Instead of also getting into a huff himself, Solomon said he spent the next "several years" flying to Boston for hot lunches with the entrepreneur.
Seven years later, Solomon said he and the entrepreneur had developed "a deeply trusting relationship," and that the entrepreneur wanted to do a $1bn refinancing. The client duly asked Solomon at Bear Stearns to help, but said that he wanted Goldman Sachs on the deal too. Solomon could have succumbed to another huff: "I'd spent 7 years cultivating this opportunity, and now I had to share it with someone else," he recalled. He didn't get in a huff. Instead, he worked with Jon Winkelreid at Goldman Sachs on the deal and Winkelreid ended up offering Solomon a job at Goldman. Now he's the Goldman Sachs CEO.
The moral of the stories might be that if you want to get ahead in banking you need to swallow your pride, to level your emotions, not get in a huff, and keep your ego heavily in check. Solomon framed it differently: "Find strong mentors, embrace criticism, and be open to change," he instructed the students.
Solomon also said it's important to "show up when it matters" in your personal life. When his daughters were five and seven years old, he said they wanted a magician at their birthday parties. He already knew some magic tricks from his dad and spent "months" practicising a metal ring trick before appearing at their parties in a tuxedo. They still talk about the occasion 25 years later.
Solomon didn't say so explicitly, but his father - who ran a small publishing company - may have influenced his approach to managing people. He said his father never told him what to do, but always showed him a different way of looking at things. On one occasion, young Solomon was scooping ice cream at Baskin Robbins and wanted more money. When he complained about his lack of cash to his father, his father told him to get a second job. Solomon said he was too busy for that, but his father told him to "take out a calendar and write down everything I did each day." It turned out that Solomon was wasting a lot of time. Three weeks later, he was more intentional and "working a second job flipping burgers at McDonalds."
While this might suggest that Solomon likes his time heavily scheduled, he's also a fan of friendships and side passions. When people are in "challenging moments", Solomon says he calls them to say "Hey, I am thinking of you. How are you?," with no agenda. "The telephone is one of the most underrated technologies in the world," he adds. "A phone call to someone is 10 times more effective than a text or an email."
Lastly, while Solomon says it's important to be passionate about your job, you need to be passionate about other things too. He himself is passionate about being DJ D-Sol and 120bpm music. This has long been the case. At college, Solomon was the guy who made the mixtapes.
"It'll be a lot easier to pick yourself up, dust yourself off, if you stay connected to what it is that lights you up," said D-Sol. He proceeded to play the students his new AI house track with lyrics like, "We’re fully alive. Hands up high, let it come. New world calling, here it comes."

item.title: Fintech startups are making "a bunch of AI slop" but it's okay
item.description:
You'd be hard pressed to find a fintech </news/how-to-get-a-job-in-fintech>, or indeed any startup, not using AI in any capacity. It has taken the industry by storm, whether you like it or not. This is both a good thing and a bad thing.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Speaking on a panel at the South by Southwest London conference yesterday, Richard Muirhead, managing partner of VC firm Fabric Ventures, said that the pace at which fintechs are building software is "going parabolic." This means that there's way more valuable applications being built, but also "a bunch of slop."
Mariano Albera, fellow panelist and Checkout.com </news/employees-at-a-fast-rising-london-fintech-are-divided-on-its-culture> CTO, said that "everything touched by AI moves faster," but suggested that, in the payments industry, there's a tendency towards shiny object syndrome instead of developing all these ideas; "there are more prototypes, protocols and blog posts than agentic transactions," he said.
The most common application of AI is in technology teams. Albera said that "the majority of [Checkout's] software is built with AI now," but that there's a misconception that this is reducing workload for technologists. "Every engineer I know is working a lot more hours and a lot later."
At New York fintech Ramp </news/ramp-fintech-jobs-london>, unlimited AI tokens are a company-wide benefit, and some teams use them much more than others. VP Jacob Wallenberg, also speaking at the conference, said that AI usage by non-technical teams has grown over the past year. Ramp's finance division is "the team that actually uses it the most now after engineering;" Wallenberg said they use Claude code to build "amazing apps that used to be these monstrosities in excel workbooks." Funding Circle CEO Lisa Jacobs, another attendee, said her fintech is "using generative AI across the business” to “enable us to be faster at getting to the market," but didn't expand on exact applications.
Other fintechs have used this productivity boost to justify mass layoffs. Coinbase </news/coinbase-employees-lament-same-pay-more-work-after-ai-layoffs> cut 14% of staff last month in an attempt to become "AI-native." Jack Dorsey's Block <https://x.com/jack/status/2027129697092731343?lang=en> cut ~40% of staff earlier this year, stating that modern tools are "enabling a new way of working which fundamentally changes what it means to build and run a company."

item.title: London's inflation traders are popular on the recruiting scene
item.description:
If you work on an inflation trading desk and you get it right, you may have a very good year. In 2021, Nikhil Choraria, who was then the top inflation trader at Goldman Sachs, reportedly generated $450m in revenues. <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/features/2022-02-21/how-wall-street-pros-from-goldman-to-jpmorgan-are-winning-the-inflation-trade>Choraria has since gone to Citadel Securities. </news/nikhil-choraria-citadel-securities> It turns out that he is not the only inflation trader on the move this year.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Inflation traders trade inflation-linked government bonds and their derivatives. The relatively small UK market has historically been known as the "widow maker" as it's easy to get trapped in losing positions. But London-based cover Europe too and hiring appears to be happening across the market.
The past few months have brought various moves. As we reported in February, </news/citi-s-top-inflation-trader-is-joining-brevan-howard-goldman-s-is-a-man-of-mystery> Douglas Cypel, Citi's star inflation trader in Paris is joining Brevan Howard. Oscar Eriksson, another trader on the Citi desk, has also left for an unnamed hedge fund. Citi declined to comment; Eriksson didn't respond.
Both traders themselves and headhunters confirm that inflation traders are suddenly popular in the London market. "GBP inflation traders are on the move again," says one trader. "Sterling rates is becoming a more precious market to be in." A headhunter says the market is subject to "lots of movement" and to "musical chairs."
Some of the chairs left empty are at NatWest, where people seem to have left - voluntarily or otherwise - before bonuses were paid. The departures include Anthony Wells, the head of linear rates trading at the bank and who left in February according to the FCA Register. They also include Alessandro Iannelli who left around the same time and who has just joined Santander. Justin Van Heerden, NatWest's head of XVA trading, left in February too. The bank declined to comment.
Various other hires are thought to be in process. "It was easy to find a new role," says one trader. "We are back in fashion again."

item.title: Asia’s darling young multistrategy fund is growing expeditiously
item.description:
Singapore-based hedge fund </news/how-to-get-a-hedge-fund-job> Arrowpoint Investment Partners is growing. Bloomberg reported <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-06-01/hedge-fund-arrowpoint-hires-10-trading-teams-seeks-more-capital> earlier today that the firm will increase its number of trading teams by 50%, from 20 to 30, until the end of the year. It already employs 140 people in total and is expected to reach 190 by the end of the year. Some of those have been hired already.
Click here to join the bubble by eFinancialCareers, our new anonymous community. <https://bubble.efinancialcareers.com/login?_gl=1*ut8n6u*_gcl_au*MTg4MDY2NTExNi4xNzYxNTU3NDIz*_ga*ODczNzI2NTA0LjE3NTM2OTk2ODI.*_ga_NZFQV4MXPM*czE3NjM2NDM0MDYkbzI1NiRnMSR0MTc2MzY0MzQzOSRqMjckbDAkaDMyMjc4OTUzMw..>✍️
We've identified at least six portfolio managers (each, presumably, leading a trading team) that have joined Arrowpoint in 2026 - most of them in January. The firm currently has offices in Singapore, Hong Kong, and Dubai.
The firm added a number of traders in Dubai. It brought in macro portfolio managers Yannick Velho and Christophe Moulin courtesy of BNP Paribas’ London office and Carmginac’s Paris office, respectively. Moulin in particular is a big signing for the firm; he was global head of multi-asset at BNP and deputy head of cross-asset at Carmginac. He is a senior portfolio manager at Arrowpoint now. Both men joined Arrowpoint in January.
Also in Dubai was Abdelak Adjriou and Patrice Ktorza, who joined the firm from asset manager Carmignac and Makor Securities respectively – both as FX portfolio managers. Both also joined Arrowpoint in January.
The firm also added two portfolio managers in its home market – Singapore. Su Lynn Lee joined as a treasury PM from First Abu Dhabi Bank, and Fish Shihao Fu joined after a career break. He was last at Southern Ridges Capital, another Singaporean hedge fund. Fish joined the hedge fund in January, while Su joined in May.
Arrowpoint was founded by ex-Millennium Asia co-CEO Jonathan Xiong. The firm has gone from strength to strength – it launched </news/hong-kong-hedge-funds-hiring-2024> with around $500m in assets under management in 2024, has around $2bn now </news/jpmorgan-didnt-make-md>, and is aiming for $3bn by the end of the year.
Arrowpoint’s expansion is funded by its successful strategies - equities, fixed income, commodities and quantitative trading. Bloomberg reported that the fund was up by 14% in the 12 months to May 2026.
Arrowpoint's growth pattern is similar to its cross-town big brother, Dymon Asia Capital </news/dymon-capital-asia>, which opened a Dubai office at the end of last year </news/dymon-asia-hiring>.
Arrowpoint Investment Partners is not to be confused with Arrowpoint Advisory, which is the middle market M&A </news/2023/02/middle-market-banking-jobs> business of boutique investment bank </news/boutique-investment-banks> Rothschild. Arrowpoint Investment Partners declined a request for comment on this article.

item.title: Morning Coffee: Top hedge fund manager was exhausted by his bad employees. Goldman Sachs and Morgan Stanley have to deal with some difficult clients
item.description:
It’s hard to drum up much sympathy for the stress felt by hedge fund </news/how-to-get-a-hedge-fund-job> managers – many people would say that’s what they're paid the big money for. But the stress is real, and very unpleasant at times. Simon Sadler, the founder of Segantii Capital Management claimed this week that his fund was “built on borderline exhaustion <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-06-02/segantii-s-sadler-details-rising-stress-at-time-of-esprit-trades?taid=6a1eade8ba61b200016938a7&utm_campaign=trueanthem&utm_content=business&utm_medium=social&utm_source=twitter&embedded-checkout=true>”.
Sadler is on trial in Hong Kong, over some trades back in 2017 which happened shortly after an employee of Segantii spoke to a sell-side contact about a potential block trade. It’s one of those tricky, technical questions about the boundary between “exploratory conversations” and “inside information”, which also seems to turn on questions of who said exactly what to exactly who exactly when. As a result, detailed recollections of contemporary events are very important.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
But it seems that when Simon Sadler casts his mind back nine years, he mostly finds memories of what a terrible time he was having. The trades that are now under scrutiny were relatively trivial compared to Segantii’s biggest position at the time, which was going wrong. The market in general was going through a period of volatility, and Sadler was trying to prepare to take his first proper holiday in a decade and spend some time with his family. He had also started to attend morning meetings of Alcoholics Anonymous “after a period of heavy drinking”.
Alongside all these significant life stressors, though, his testimony in court referred to “difficult conversations with underperforming employees”. There's a sense that these might have been at least as heavy on Sadler’s mind as any of Segantii’s other problems.
When you make a bad trade yourself, you only have one decision to make – cut losses, or wait for it to turn around. That can be tough enough. When one of your employees makes a bad trade, you have that problem, plus the problem of deciding whether it’s just a piece of bad luck or a sign that the employee isn’t up to their job. If it’s the second, you have a further decision to make as to whether their performance can be turned round, or whether they need to be sacked. Even without the social awkwardness that comes in a small team where employees are also your friends, other people’s mistakes can be three times as stressful as your own.
The trial continues, and Sadler and his colleagues continue to deny all wrongdoing. Segantii Capital itself returned capital to investors a while ago; Sadler said that “as soon as we were charged, there was little doubt in my mind that the right thing to do was shut the business”. Ironically, it seems that the fund may have been named <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Setantii> after a Celtic legend in which someone irresponsibly left the floodgates open and allowed a great city to be drowned.
Elsewhere, it’s unlikely that the bankers on the SpaceX IPO </news/space-x-ipo-goldman-sachs-morgan-stanley> ever thought that the difficult part was over when they won the mandate. Now it seems that Elon Musk wants to negotiate the fees <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-06-02/spacex-ipo-spacex-wants-fee-cut-from-ipo-bankers-chasing-500-million-windfall?utm_medium=email&utm_source=author_alert&utm_term=260602&utm_campaign=author_17510379&embedded-checkout=true>; rather than the standard 4% to 7%, he thinks that 0.75% might be reasonable.
Some sort of discount was inevitable – SpaceX is aiming to raise $75bn, and the banks could hardly have expected to be paid multiple billions, with a “b” for bookrunning and roadshows, no matter how complicated. Even at 0.75% there are still more than $500m in fees to share, in line with expectations of “hundreds of millions <https://www.wsj.com/finance/banking/spacex-eyeing-roughly-1-75-trillion-valuation-in-ipo-next-week-57066029>”, the majority of which will go to Goldman and Morgan Stanley.
A fee of 0.75% is aggressive, though. Previous mega-deals like Alibaba and Saudi Aramco have taken IPO fees down to around 1%, but the only previous large offering at 0.75% price was the relaunch of General Motors after its bailout (at a time in 2010 when the banks were in an unusually poor position to negotiate with the US government). Not only that, but SpaceX employees seem to have taken after their founder, and are asking for sweetened deals on wealth management <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-06-02/spacex-ipo-employees-negotiate-together-for-lower-fees-tax-saving-tools>. The sensation grows that bankers are going to earn their money on this one.
Meanwhile …
Citadel has launched a program which allows other hedge funds to input their trade ideas into an “alpha capture” system and feed into its own quant strategies. This is a fairly common thing to do with sell-side recommendations, but quite avant garde in terms of soliciting input from nominal competitors and probably demonstrates how Citadel really doesn’t see itself as playing in the same league. (Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-06-02/citadel-set-to-pay-for-trading-ideas-from-other-hedge-funds?utm_medium=email&utm_source=author_alert&utm_term=260602&utm_campaign=author_19458291&embedded-checkout=true>)
This year’s “Hedge Fund Rising Stars” seems to include some people who have built entire new businesses, and others who have just come up with a pretty good trade. Perhaps surprisingly, not all of them are AI-focused. (Institutional Investor <https://www.institutionalinvestor.com/article/these-are-institutional-investors-2026-hedge-fund-rising-stars>)
David Solomon is seeing “more greed than fear” at the moment, while John Waldron notes that Goldman Sachs has its biggest lead ever in the advisory league tables, before worrying that he might have “jinxed” himself. (Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-06-02/goldman-s-solomon-says-there-s-more-greed-than-fear-in-markets?utm_medium=email&utm_source=author_alert&utm_term=260602&utm_campaign=author_21835441>)
The timing might be coincidental, but Jérome Brassart is leaving Perella Weinberg to go to Guggenheim Securities, and so won’t have to go through the forthcoming headcount reduction. (Financial News <https://www.fnlondon.com/articles/guggenheim-securities-hires-perella-dealmaker-brassart-for-energy-push-8bf63425?mod=topic_news>)
Academic research discovers that, with the exception of Elliott Advisors, most hedge funds who call themselves “activist investors” don’t actually seem to do very much activism. (FT Alphaville <https://www.ft.com/content/7d2d497c-dc21-4fa4-8135-5f04a3d6e591>)
Now that Andrew Left of Citron Resarch has been convicted, others in the market are asking what this means for the short-selling industry. In particular, it seems that the business model of being a high-profile hater with an active social media presence could be risky. (Business Insider <https://www.businessinsider.com/reactions-andrew-left-securities-fraud-verdict-short-selling-investors-analysts-2026-6>)
Potential future Prime Minister Andy Burnham was scheduled to have a conference call with hedge fund investors about “bond market pressure”, but decided to go and drink Guinness at an Irish club instead. (FT <https://www.ft.com/content/0c2d9e3f-4207-4b39-b3e8-7425e93af5dc?syn-25a6b1a6=1>)
Follow me on X <https://x.com/MadameButcher>. Follow me on LinkedIn. <https://www.linkedin.com/in/sarahbutcherjournalist/>

item.title: "There is an idiot on my macro trading floor"
item.description:
I am a member of a macro trading team at a bank in New York. I have worked at this bank for over five years and throughout my time here, a colleague has annoyed me. He is an idiot. It has got to the stage where I find it impossible to be around him.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
He seems to be trying to embody the epithet of 'fake it until you make it.' He is not all that much more senior than me and has almost no trade ideas of his own. He relies on the rest of us to do the trading.
Despite this, he consider himself a big shot. He walks around the trading floor talking in a booming voice and making himself the centre of attention. If he can put someone else down, he will.
I know that I'm not the only person annoyed by him. I also know that these kinds of people exist across the industry. I'm not sure if he understands how much he irritates everyone. I can't understand why the bank continues to tolerate his loudness and arrogance, though. He is a toxic influence and yet somehow he survives. Every day I hope he will have been fired.
Germain Ponce is a pseudonym
Follow me on X <https://x.com/MadameButcher>. Follow me on LinkedIn. <https://www.linkedin.com/in/sarahbutcherjournalist/>

item.title: Ramp, the Revolut of New York, is hiring in London
item.description:
New York's hottest fintech </news/how-to-get-a-job-in-fintech>is coming to London. </news/top-london-fintechs>Ramp, a cards and expense management fintech valued at $40bn earlier this year, is planning to open an office in the city "later this summer" according to Jacob Wallenberg, Ramp's VP of international expansion. It has a propensity to pay big salaries (upwards of $400k in New York), and has a hiring strategy that bears a lot of similarities to London's current largest fintech, Revolut. </news/revolut-the-fintech-hiring-0-15-of-applicants-raised-pay>
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Ramp is hiring for just two jobs in London at present. Wallenberg said that Ramp "care[s] less about the individual credential[s]" when hiring and instead focuses on hiring people who can learn new things quickly. Revolut CEO Nik Storonsky said in a hiring playbook released last year </news/six-red-flags-that-revolut-hates-in-its-engineers> that "slow career progression" and "working in a slow-paced environment" are red flags when hiring.
Wallenberg said it's also important to "convey the story of how you're evolving over time" during your job interview, and to show examples of your creativity. While he said this is "easier than ever to do... fewer people take the initiative to put themselves out in the world."
Ramp also has an aversion to "tried and true expert" corporate types. Storonsky, similarly, lamented hiring "experienced executives" </news/the-most-exciting-and-bizarre-job-for-24-year-olds-at-revolut-and-other-fintechs>early into his time at Revolut. Instead, Ramp likes to promote young and promote from within; Wallenberg said this model is "such an effective incentive for people to do well." A junior role in Ramp's London office, therefore, could become lucrative very quickly.
Some of the potential negatives of working at Ramp are the same as those at Revolut, not least the intensity of the work. Ramp staff in the US that we spoke to last year </news/ramp-culture-new-york-fintech>said the culture is "all about results," and described it as "a bit of a meat grinder." They also said that "people who make their whole life about Ramp love it." Revolut's top performers have a similar mindset. </news/2023/01/revolut-culture>
Ramp, moreso than Revolut, has a reputation for paying a lot of money. Among its open listings in New York, two roles in its design team pay up to $440k in base salary alone, while sales staff can also receive upwards of $400k in total compensation. The London office isn't quite as lucrative as yet; one of the two open roles is a customer success associate that pays up to £43k ($57.9k) per year on an hourly basis.
Have a confidential story, tip, or comment you’d like to share? item.title: Optiver interns need to pass an 88-question theory exam… Claude outperforms most of them
item.description:
Getting an internship </news/high-frequency-trading-internship>or graduate job at a major electronic trading firm </news/high-frequency-trading-hiring-and-pay>is no easy feat. There are horror stories of obtuse brainteasers </news/electronic-trading-interviews>asked in job interviews, but some firms have their own unique challenges. Interns at Dutch trading house Optiver, for example, must also pass an expansive theory test.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
A recent post by Optiver, which pays up to $90k to its interns </news/electronic-trading-summer-internship-pay> for just a few weeks of work, compared the performance of various different Claude models on its intern theory exam made up of 88 questions. It's not clear whether interns encounter this during the application process or during the internship itself; Optiver did not respond to a request for comment but we suspect the latter.
The test includes simple questions on mathematical theory or trading concepts, as well as more niche and complex questions, including how to make markets around elections or data releases.
The average intern scores just 61% on this exam according to a report <https://www.optiver.com/insights/technology-blog/where-ai-trading-models-work-and-where-they-still-fall-short/?utm_campaign=LI_photo_NassrAIpost&utm_source=linkedin,twitter&utm_medium=social&utm_content=1780085858> released in April which analyzed the performance of older models on the same exam. Opus 4.8 scored upwards of 70%.
Getting a job at Optiver presumably comes down to more than just your final score. The report said that the two biggest problems LLMs encountered in the exam were "failing to incorporate new information after acting" and "struggling to reason more than one or two steps ahead." As electronic trading interns have told us in the past, </news/how-to-convert-quant-trading-internship>this kind of thinking is perhaps the most important part of being a trader and you're continually assessed on this during the internship itself. Optiver said that, in the exam, these LLMs "rarely demonstrated the more nuanced aspects of how our traders think and make decisions."
AI won't be replacing Optiver interns anytime soon, it seems, but that doesn't mean the trading firm isn't interested in the technology. We reported in April that the firm is building out an independent AI lab </news/optiver-ai-lab>, and it also has an applied AI team working on using the tech in a trading context. The experiment above was carried out by Quintin Brown, a graduate trader who joined in 2025 before transitioning into its applied AI team.

item.title: Hedge fund ExodusPoint parted company with a senior portfolio manager who is going elsewhere
item.description:
Mirco Bulega has gone from ExodusPoint. The former European economist at Credit Suisse and macro portfolio manager at Alphadyne Asset Management, is understood to have exited the fund. It's thought that he has something else lined up.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
ExodusPoint declined to say anything for this article and Bulega didn't respond to a request to comment. Bulega joined ExodusPoint in London in July 2022. In July 2023 we observed that he was making some big hires for his pod </news/2023/07/mirco-bulega-exoduspoint>, including Jacques Mouawad from Morgan Stanley, Vittorio Martino from Deutsche Bank, and a junior named Vladimir. It's thought they're still there.
Bulega is understood to have made a small loss during the volatility of March </news/2023/07/mirco-bulega-exoduspoint> after years of positive returns. He is thought to have lined up his exit well before March. However, there are well documented perils of joining multistrategy hedge funds </news/multistrategy-hedge-funds-hiring>during difficult markets given the sector's notoriously low tolerance of drawdowns.
It's not clear where Bulega is going next, but it's thought that he may be launching a new macro platform somewhere else. Maybe he's having a summer off first.

item.title: It's the best of times and the worst of times to be an M&A banker
item.description:
M&A deals are back. Goldman Sachs is predicting a "surge" in M&A this year <https://www.goldmansachs.com/insights/articles/ma-volume-expected-to-surge-this-year-despite-economic-uncertainty>as businesses seek to reposition themselves for the AI world, despite geopolitical uncertainty. "CEOs are confident," declared Goldman Sachs' president John Waldron </news/john-waldron-goldman>last week. They are also "uncertain and worried." It's a "winner-take-most-environment," said Waldron. CEOs need to merge with other corporates simply to survive.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
While this sounds good for M&A bankers, the reality is not so simple. As the Bernstein analyst interviewing Waldron observed, the "winner-takes-most environment" for corporates applies equally to banks themselves. Goldman Sachs currently has a $300bn lead in the M&A league table, said Waldron. This is the firm's largest ever lead at this point in the year, Waldron added.
This is why, while some Goldman Sachs bankers and their teams are working hard on live deals, boutique bank </news/boutique-investment-banks> Perella Weinberg is simultaneously cutting 10% of its headcount </news/perella-weinberg-partners-cut>and talking about <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-05-27/perella-weinberg-to-cut-10-of-workforce-including-partners> the "fog of war." Deals in the current market are not distributed equally.
Based on data from Dealogic, the charts below explain why. While the value of M&A deals in most key sectors is up so far this year, the number of closed M&A deals is not. As a result, the average M&A deal size in most sectors has risen considerably. If you're working on one of these big deals, well done. If you're not, bad luck.
Among those in the doldrums are the financial sponsors </news/financial-sponsors-bankers-2026> bankers credited with being the industry's most talented schmoozers. Waldron noted last week that corporate M&A is up 63% year-on-year while financial sponsor M&A involving private equity firms is down 4%. As ever, though, Waldron also noted that financial sponsor firms are sitting on $1 trillion of dry powder. When and if they spend it, it will be a tremendous time for everyone everywhere.

item.title: Banking internships: A complete guide to summer analyst programs
item.description:
Getting a job in an investment bank </news/what-is-an-investment-bank> is tough. If you want to work in popular front office roles like M&A </news/what-are-ma-jobs> or sales & trading </news/how-do-you-get-a-job-in-sales-and-trading-in-a-bank> you need to get in very early in your career. If you wait and decide you want to work in banking in your 30s, it's too late. It's often even too late if you wait until you've graduated. So how do you get into an investment bank? It's simple: with an internship.
Click here to join the bubble by eFinancialCareers, our new anonymous community. <https://bubble.efinancialcareers.com/login?_gl=1*ut8n6u*_gcl_au*MTg4MDY2NTExNi4xNzYxNTU3NDIz*_ga*ODczNzI2NTA0LjE3NTM2OTk2ODI.*_ga_NZFQV4MXPM*czE3NjM2NDM0MDYkbzI1NiRnMSR0MTc2MzY0MzQzOSRqMjckbDAkaDMyMjc4OTUzMw..>✍️
Investment banks recruit their graduates from the 10 week+ summer internships they offer to penultimate-year students. In the UK at least, these students are often already prepped and groomed through a spring internship </news/spring-week-bank> in the first year of their studies.
This internship pipeline gives banks a steady stream of fresh talent that they've had a chance to test in the workplace. As a candidate, once you’re in the internship pipeline, you’ve got a great chance to stay in it. At least half of students who complete summer internships usually get return offers. The hardest part is getting an internship in the first place. Goldman Sachs and JPMorgan, which run some of the largest intern classes in the industry, have acceptance rates of around 0.7% each.
Getting a summer internship – and understanding the machinery and logic behind them, as well as how to excel on yours – are therefore critical if you are aiming for a career in an investment bank, particularly if you want to be a banker </news/the-investment-banking-recruitment-process> or a trader </news/how-do-you-get-a-job-in-sales-and-trading-in-a-bank>. All roles in banks offer internships though, including in areas like technology </news/how-to-get-a-technology-job-bank>, risk </news/risk-job-investment-bank> and compliance </news/how-to-get-a-job-in-compliance>.
What are investment banking summer internships exactly?
Summer internships in banks are eight- to 12- week long programs, with some variation in the exact length depending on bank and program. There are two main types of summer internships: summer analyst programs, for penultimate-year undergraduates, and summer associate programs, for MBA students </news/best-mba-for-finance-banking>. In the investment banking hierarchy </news/banks-weird-hierarchies-analysts-associates-vps-mds>, associates are a step above analysts, reflecting the fact that MBA students typically have a few years of experience under their belts.
There are also off-cycle internships, typically for last-year undergraduate students who missed out on summer internships. Off-cycle internships </news/no-spring-internship-bank>can last anywhere from 3 to 12 months and are available in a wide variety of locations and business areas.
Banks usually offer internships across all their divisions. In the front office </news/front-office-middle-office-back-office-banking> of an investment bank, this includes global markets, which comprises sales and trading </news/how-do-you-get-a-job-in-sales-and-trading-in-a-bank> (and research </news/research-job-investment-bank>), as well as corporate finance, mergers and acquisitions </news/what-are-ma-jobs>, and capital markets </news/capital-markets-jobs-ecm-and-dcm>. Internships are also available across everything from risk </news/risk-job-investment-bank> and quantitative </news/quant-jobs-finance> finance to operations </news/operations-jobs-banking> and compliance </news/how-to-get-a-job-in-compliance>.
As well as summer internships and off-cycle internships, banks offer spring internships </news/spring-week-bank>. These are week long events for first-year students and run during the Easter break. Spring internships are short introductions to banking, but they're increasingly important. Some banks recruit summer interns from them. They're common in Europe – but they’re starting to be phased out in America </news/bank-diversity-programs?utm_source=chatgpt.com>, where they were a lynchpin in how banks organized their diversity hiring.
Spring internships can be very useful for your application. Data from Trackr (formerly the Bristol Trackr) suggested in April </news/how-many-intern-applications> that students with spring internships under their belt had to make just 73 applications on average for a successful summer internship acceptance, compared to 138 on average for students without previous spring internships.
What are my chances of getting in?
Low. And getting lower.
As we noted above, at Goldman Sachs, the acceptance rate </news/goldman-sachs-internship-acceptance-rate> of interns declined from 1.27% in 2022 to 0.72% in 2025. Why? Because while the number of open internship places increased from 3,000 to 3,600 in that period, the number of people applying to them increased from 236k to around 359k.
The change was even more stark in other places. JPMorgan had 505k applicants to 4,500 internships in 2024; the next year, it had 630k applicants to just 4,100 internships.
In all likelihood, things will get even worse in the future. Two years ago </news/jpmorgan-ai>, there were whispers that Goldman Sachs and Morgan Stanley would cut their summer internship cohorts by two-thirds to accommodate the increased productivity offered by AI-powered bankers. That hasn’t come to pass, but class sizes are shrinking, and AI adoption is rising.
It also matters where and what you study </news/finance/the-top-100-universities-globally-for-careers-in-banking-and-finance>. Although banks are increasingly interested in hiring from a wide group of universities, it still helps to be at a university with a history of placing people into banking, so that it can coach you on how to handle the internship. "It really helps for them to have a conversation with people who went through the application process and had their first steps in the job previously. It also helps them to understand more about the bank, the culture, the teams,” said an HEC professor who previously worked for Barclays.
Trackrr, formerly the Bristol Tracker, said in a report </news/how-many-intern-applications> in April that the average student who received an internship offer in the UK in 2026 applied to over 100 programs, up from 68 in 2024 and 75 in 2025. The figures were slightly better for diverse students, including women, who applied to just 79 programs on average, and black people who applied to less than 50 programs on average.
What can I expect from a banking internship?
Different banks structure their internships in different ways. Internships are also typically structured differently depending upon the area of the bank you're working in.
Summer internships typically start with one week of online and classroom training followed by hands-on work experience with a particular “desk”, or team. In some banks (such as Goldman Sachs </news/how-to-get-a-job-goldman-sachs>), sales & trading </news/how-do-you-get-a-job-in-sales-and-trading-in-a-bank> internships will offer a “rotation”, where you work on different desks to gain broader experience and see which of the teams you best mesh with.
Generally speaking, interns do the same things that analysts do, but on a smaller scale. In a 2024 video, former JPMorgan analyst Cameron Galbraith said <https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=EB-kSZEP6Z4> that an intern would be handed “a few slides” of a large deck to work on and to received feedback on. Galbraith did say, however, that you should do more of the deck as a learning exercise, and to be useful to your team.
Galbraith also suggests asking “thoughtful” questions to your analyst, and to “impress” them by suggesting ideas – even if 99% of the time they will definitely not be used. But merely showing bigger picture thinking will do a lot to impress.
Kunal Shah, Co-CEO of Goldman Sachs International (the firm’s UK branch) and its global co-head of fixed income, currencies, and commodities, said recently <https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=qFKSW5a7q_c> that his 10 week internship at the firm was where he could “really get tested out”, in situations where his supervisors could see not just what he knew, but how he reacted to new bits of information.
Aside from what’s traditionally important, interns in today’s day and age are expected to have extreme AI fluency. Fabian Figi, the head of campus recruiting at market making firm Citadel Securities, told Business Insider <https://www.businessinsider.com/wall-street-summer-interns-ai-banks-2026-5> in May that the firm’s "AI native" interns would take on more "meaningful responsibility very early on, including work focused on applying AI to complex real-world problems."
Shah said today's interns can make themselves valuable by displaying their technological prowess. “Junior talent are inherently tech-savvy, and they don't have the legacy of why we do things in a certain way. They can see how to disrupt us,” he said. Shah also told students not to fear the risk of AI killing the job you're moving into. When he did his internship in 2003, he was told not to rotate onto the fixed income desk as an intern due to pending automation. In fact, he said automation simply meant that he did not have to collect physical trade tickets or book travel or type so many things into spreadsheets.
For the AI-powered interns of the future, this means automated building of spreadsheet models and standardizing PowerPoint formats. The job became more interesting without spending time booking travel – and the hope is that it can become more interesting without fixing pitchbooks.
Will I get paid for the internship?
Yes. Interns in investment banks are paid the same as full time junior analysts (although without the bonuses </news/banker-bonuses>) and intern salaries are pro-rated for the length of the internship.
You can therefore expect to earn around $20k (£15k) from a summer analyst internship, but given what you could earn as a banker, the money is not as important as the experience.
For MBA students, the figures are similar. NYU’s employment report <https://www.gsb.stanford.edu/organizations/recruit/strategies-resources/employment-reports/summer> for MBA students showed that the average investment banking summer intern earned $3.3k in weekly pay, or around $26k - $30k depending on the length of the internship.
How do I apply for a banking internship?
The investment banking recruitment process </news/the-investment-banking-recruitment-process> is a long thing. Generally speaking, the process goes like this:
In your first year at a top university, you are a top student academically with a wide range of extracurricular pastimes, especially sports. You put together your CV expertly </news/the-absolutely-perfect-investment-banking-cv>, and if you're in Europe you apply for spring weeks at the most prestigious investment banks. They like your CV, they offer you an interview, you ace the interview, and then they offer you a spring week. You can do multiple spring weeks, if you have the time or inclination. It’s not unheard of to complete as many as nine </news/spring-week-internship-banking>.
Some banks offer summer internships to spring interns during the spring internship. If you weren’t offered one, whether through your own failure or because the bank didn’t offer them, you’ll want to start applying to summer internships manually.
These can open really, really early, depending on where you are in the world (US opens earlier than EMEA – more on that here </news/the-best-time-to-apply-for-a-banking-job>). You’ll want to get your application in as soon as you can - there are limited places, and programs close when they are filled in many locations.
If banks like your resume, you can expect a barrage of digital interviews and online tests, such as HireVue </news/2022/10/hirevue-interview-banking> or HackerRank </news/2022/04/how-to-pass-a-hackerrank-test> tests, which banks use to screen applicants before interviewing </news/70-top-banking-interview-questions-and-how-to-answer-them> in person, usually at a “super day </news/if-a-bank-doesn-t-hire-you-after-5-interviews-you-re-in-trouble>” with multiple interviews. If you’re successful, you can expect an internship offers shortly after.
Brian Landeros </news/how-to-get-a-goldman-sachs-internship>, a former Morgan Stanley and Goldman Sachs intern (as well as an analyst at boutique bank </news/boutique-investment-banks> Evercore) says that the key is good timing and "an immense level of grit". Research is the key to both: "I literally came across all these resources online telling me how to network," says Landeros. "I started reaching out to alumni. There was all this stuff that I realised I needed to do." Networking and performing well on the internship are both important, but the critical factor is getting the internship to begin with. "The door opens up for one semester, and then it closes forever," Landeros warns.
How do I turn my internship into an analyst job?
This is also called “converting </news/finance/7-step-guide-converting-banking-summer-internship-job>” your internship.
The usual answers for converting an internship are to work as hard as you can, network like crazy, and try to fit into the firm’s culture. Get the coffee often and generally speaking be as helpful as you can.
One M&A MD told us </news/internship-conversion-banking> that banks value “proactive” employees and expect you to be both ask questions and to be intellectually curious. Other roles might expect you to solve issues by yourself, but banks value you more for doing something right, rather than necessarily independently.
“My mentor told me four tricks that turned the situation around,” the MD also told us. “Firstly, she said that every time I completed an assignment, I needed to ask for feedback right away. Secondly, she said that every time I completed a project, I needed to ask for informal feedback. Thirdly, she said I needed to arrange coffee chats with associates, directors and MDs in my team and adjacent teams to get to know people and learn about their roles. And fourthly, she said I needed to make it very clear to everyone that I was really enjoying my time with the bank.”
Don’t complain about the hours. Banks say they are keen to encourage a better work-life balance, but you’re still expected to answer a request for help at 7pm, even if you were planning to meet friends. Interns work shorter hours than analysts, but not a traditional 9-5. If you can’t stand intern hours, then analyst hours will drive you mad, and banking probably isn’t for you.
In an extensive X/Twitter post/tweet <https://x.com/HighyieldHarry/status/2058991029399810102?s=20> in May, finance influencer HighYieldHarry <https://x.com/HighyieldHarry/> detailed his advice for students who wanted to maximise their internships. Some of the advice is very curious: Harry recommends overcommunicating by saying “will do”, “sounds good”, and “on it” when given an instruction to demonstrate that they have been recognized.
His other advice includes not asking questions that have already been answered in an email, CIM, PowerPoint, or otherwise. “Any question you have should be an expansion or clarification point NOT something already clearly stated,” HighYieldHarry said.
He also recommends not taking a vacation during your internship. “You are trying out for a sports team – you haven’t made the team yet, but you’re getting the chance to make the big leagues. Would you leave the team in the middle of preseason before the roster is finalized?”
Lastly. HYH also suggested hard-saving your files frequently, networking with as many people as possible, maximising office times, avoiding the AI/Social Media brain rot rabbit hole, and being careful with how much you drink during happy hour.
What happens if I don’t get a job offer after my internship?
You might have an issue. Banks are extremely reliant on internship programs and actively seek to recruit people from them. Not being hired by the bank you interned for is not only a bad situation to be in in the moment, but could also potentially be a red flag to other prospective employers.
For one, you can apply directly to graduate analyst programs. This is what banks put analysts through when they are recruited. These are two-year programs, and at many banks you can apply to them “normally”. This is your first port of call.
Your second is an off-cycle internship. These are also for graduates and are generally longer (three to six months, sometimes up to 12) than summer internships, but not multi-year in length. Doing an off-cycle internship abroad, especially in Europe </news/no-spring-internship-bank>, can be a good “resetting” of your career. Off-cycle internships can sometimes be better than summer internships, as they offer you broader work experience opportunities.
It's also worth noting that you can still apply for internships even as you go into your final year at university. It's becoming increasingly common for students to complete internships during the summer after they graduate – although it might not be possible in investment banking, where your graduation date is part of the requirements.
How do you get around that? By doing a top-quality master’s degree </news/the-top-30-masters-in-finance-for-getting-a-job-in-investment-banking>, of course. It doesn’t put you in a better position to do one, but it does give you another shot at a career in banking. All it costs you is five figures (potentially six, in the USA) of debt. Hey, don’t look at us – you should have secured and converted your second-year summer internship to begin with.
The quality of a master’s in finance degree </news/masters-in-finance-worth-it> is also worth considering. While a top masters from a top university - such as St. Gallen or HEC - can effectively give you an extra year in which to "lock in" and get the job you've been praying for, others "will charge you $100k and leave you to rot," we were told by former careers coach Mark Ross.
If you're in the US, Brian Landeros </news/how-to-get-a-goldman-sachs-internship> also thinks that delaying your graduation can be worth it. "There are candidates who get to the second year of college and who had no idea what banking was but are suddenly interested in the industry," Landeros says. He suggests delaying your graduation date as much as possible and applying like crazy to the internships that you are still eligible for. Internships are really, really important.
Have a confidential story, tip, or comment you’d like to share? Contact: +44 7537 182250 (SMS, WhatsApp or voicemail). Telegram: @SarahButcher. Signal: sarahbutcher.22 Click here to fill in our anonymous form </about/editor-tips>, or email editortips@efinancialcareers.com.
Bear with us if you leave a comment at the bottom of this article: comments are moderated intermittently by human beings. Sometimes these humans might be asleep, or away from their desks, so it may take a while for your comment to appear. You must take sole responsibility for comments you post on this site. We will take reasonable steps to weed out anything that we consider to be offensive or inappropriate.
item.author: zeno.toulon@efinancialcareers.com (Zeno Toulon)

item.title: Banking internships: A complete guide to summer analyst programs
item.description:
Getting a job in an investment bank </news/what-is-an-investment-bank> is tough. If you want to work in popular front office roles like M&A </news/what-are-ma-jobs> or sales & trading </news/how-do-you-get-a-job-in-sales-and-trading-in-a-bank> you need to get in very early in your career. If you wait and decide you want to work in banking in your 30s, it's too late. It's often even too late if you wait until you've graduated. So how do you get into an investment bank? It's simple: with an internship.
Click here to join the bubble by eFinancialCareers, our new anonymous community. <https://bubble.efinancialcareers.com/login?_gl=1*ut8n6u*_gcl_au*MTg4MDY2NTExNi4xNzYxNTU3NDIz*_ga*ODczNzI2NTA0LjE3NTM2OTk2ODI.*_ga_NZFQV4MXPM*czE3NjM2NDM0MDYkbzI1NiRnMSR0MTc2MzY0MzQzOSRqMjckbDAkaDMyMjc4OTUzMw..>✍️
Investment banks recruit their graduates from the 10 week+ summer internships they offer to penultimate-year students. In the UK at least, these students are often already prepped and groomed through a spring internship </news/spring-week-bank> in the first year of their studies.
This internship pipeline gives banks a steady stream of fresh talent that they've had a chance to test in the workplace. As a candidate, once you’re in the internship pipeline, you’ve got a great chance to stay in it. At least half of students who complete summer internships usually get return offers. The hardest part is getting an internship in the first place. Goldman Sachs and JPMorgan, which run some of the largest intern classes in the industry, have acceptance rates of around 0.7% each.
Getting a summer internship – and understanding the machinery and logic behind them, as well as how to excel on yours – are therefore critical if you are aiming for a career in an investment bank, particularly if you want to be a banker </news/the-investment-banking-recruitment-process> or a trader </news/how-do-you-get-a-job-in-sales-and-trading-in-a-bank>. All roles in banks offer internships though, including in areas like technology </news/how-to-get-a-technology-job-bank>, risk </news/risk-job-investment-bank> and compliance </news/how-to-get-a-job-in-compliance>.
What are investment banking summer internships exactly?
Summer internships in banks are eight- to 12- week long programs, with some variation in the exact length depending on bank and program. There are two main types of summer internships: summer analyst programs, for penultimate-year undergraduates, and summer associate programs, for MBA students </news/best-mba-for-finance-banking>. In the investment banking hierarchy </news/banks-weird-hierarchies-analysts-associates-vps-mds>, associates are a step above analysts, reflecting the fact that MBA students typically have a few years of experience under their belts.
There are also off-cycle internships, typically for last-year undergraduate students who missed out on summer internships. Off-cycle internships </news/no-spring-internship-bank>can last anywhere from 3 to 12 months and are available in a wide variety of locations and business areas.
Banks usually offer internships across all their divisions. In the front office </news/front-office-middle-office-back-office-banking> of an investment bank, this includes global markets, which comprises sales and trading </news/how-do-you-get-a-job-in-sales-and-trading-in-a-bank> (and research </news/research-job-investment-bank>), as well as corporate finance, mergers and acquisitions </news/what-are-ma-jobs>, and capital markets </news/capital-markets-jobs-ecm-and-dcm>. Internships are also available across everything from risk </news/risk-job-investment-bank> and quantitative </news/quant-jobs-finance> finance to operations </news/operations-jobs-banking> and compliance </news/how-to-get-a-job-in-compliance>.
As well as summer internships and off-cycle internships, banks offer spring internships </news/spring-week-bank>. These are week long events for first-year students and run during the Easter break. Spring internships are short introductions to banking, but they're increasingly important. Some banks recruit summer interns from them. They're common in Europe – but they’re starting to be phased out in America </news/bank-diversity-programs?utm_source=chatgpt.com>, where they were a lynchpin in how banks organized their diversity hiring.
Spring internships can be very useful for your application. Data from Trackr (formerly the Bristol Trackr) suggested in April </news/how-many-intern-applications> that students with spring internships under their belt had to make just 73 applications on average for a successful summer internship acceptance, compared to 138 on average for students without previous spring internships.
What are my chances of getting in?
Low. And getting lower.
As we noted above, at Goldman Sachs, the acceptance rate </news/goldman-sachs-internship-acceptance-rate> of interns declined from 1.27% in 2022 to 0.72% in 2025. Why? Because while the number of open internship places increased from 3,000 to 3,600 in that period, the number of people applying to them increased from 236k to around 359k.
The change was even more stark in other places. JPMorgan had 505k applicants to 4,500 internships in 2024; the next year, it had 630k applicants to just 4,100 internships.
In all likelihood, things will get even worse in the future. Two years ago </news/jpmorgan-ai>, there were whispers that Goldman Sachs and Morgan Stanley would cut their summer internship cohorts by two-thirds to accommodate the increased productivity offered by AI-powered bankers. That hasn’t come to pass, but class sizes are shrinking, and AI adoption is rising.
It also matters where and what you study </news/finance/the-top-100-universities-globally-for-careers-in-banking-and-finance>. Although banks are increasingly interested in hiring from a wide group of universities, it still helps to be at a university with a history of placing people into banking, so that it can coach you on how to handle the internship. "It really helps for them to have a conversation with people who went through the application process and had their first steps in the job previously. It also helps them to understand more about the bank, the culture, the teams,” said an HEC professor who previously worked for Barclays.
Trackrr, formerly the Bristol Tracker, said in a report </news/how-many-intern-applications> in April that the average student who received an internship offer in the UK in 2026 applied to over 100 programs, up from 68 in 2024 and 75 in 2025. The figures were slightly better for diverse students, including women, who applied to just 79 programs on average, and black people who applied to less than 50 programs on average.
What can I expect from a banking internship?
Different banks structure their internships in different ways. Internships are also typically structured differently depending upon the area of the bank you're working in.
Summer internships typically start with one week of online and classroom training followed by hands-on work experience with a particular “desk”, or team. In some banks (such as Goldman Sachs </news/how-to-get-a-job-goldman-sachs>), sales & trading </news/how-do-you-get-a-job-in-sales-and-trading-in-a-bank> internships will offer a “rotation”, where you work on different desks to gain broader experience and see which of the teams you best mesh with.
Generally speaking, interns do the same things that analysts do, but on a smaller scale. In a 2024 video, former JPMorgan analyst Cameron Galbraith said <https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=EB-kSZEP6Z4> that an intern would be handed “a few slides” of a large deck to work on and to received feedback on. Galbraith did say, however, that you should do more of the deck as a learning exercise, and to be useful to your team.
Galbraith also suggests asking “thoughtful” questions to your analyst, and to “impress” them by suggesting ideas – even if 99% of the time they will definitely not be used. But merely showing bigger picture thinking will do a lot to impress.
Kunal Shah, Co-CEO of Goldman Sachs International (the firm’s UK branch) and its global co-head of fixed income, currencies, and commodities, said recently <https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=qFKSW5a7q_c> that his 10 week internship at the firm was where he could “really get tested out”, in situations where his supervisors could see not just what he knew, but how he reacted to new bits of information.
Aside from what’s traditionally important, interns in today’s day and age are expected to have extreme AI fluency. Fabian Figi, the head of campus recruiting at market making firm Citadel Securities, told Business Insider <https://www.businessinsider.com/wall-street-summer-interns-ai-banks-2026-5> in May that the firm’s "AI native" interns would take on more "meaningful responsibility very early on, including work focused on applying AI to complex real-world problems."
Shah said today's interns can make themselves valuable by displaying their technological prowess. “Junior talent are inherently tech-savvy, and they don't have the legacy of why we do things in a certain way. They can see how to disrupt us,” he said. Shah also told students not to fear the risk of AI killing the job you're moving into. When he did his internship in 2003, he was told not to rotate onto the fixed income desk as an intern due to pending automation. In fact, he said automation simply meant that he did not have to collect physical trade tickets or book travel or type so many things into spreadsheets.
For the AI-powered interns of the future, this means automated building of spreadsheet models and standardizing PowerPoint formats. The job became more interesting without spending time booking travel – and the hope is that it can become more interesting without fixing pitchbooks.
Will I get paid for the internship?
Yes. Interns in investment banks are paid the same as full time junior analysts (although without the bonuses </news/banker-bonuses>) and intern salaries are pro-rated for the length of the internship.
You can therefore expect to earn around $20k (£15k) from a summer analyst internship, but given what you could earn as a banker, the money is not as important as the experience.
For MBA students, the figures are similar. NYU’s employment report <https://www.gsb.stanford.edu/organizations/recruit/strategies-resources/employment-reports/summer> for MBA students showed that the average investment banking summer intern earned $3.3k in weekly pay, or around $26k - $30k depending on the length of the internship.
How do I apply for a banking internship?
The investment banking recruitment process </news/the-investment-banking-recruitment-process> is a long thing. Generally speaking, the process goes like this:
In your first year at a top university, you are a top student academically with a wide range of extracurricular pastimes, especially sports. You put together your CV expertly </news/the-absolutely-perfect-investment-banking-cv>, and if you're in Europe you apply for spring weeks at the most prestigious investment banks. They like your CV, they offer you an interview, you ace the interview, and then they offer you a spring week. You can do multiple spring weeks, if you have the time or inclination. It’s not unheard of to complete as many as nine </news/spring-week-internship-banking>.
Some banks offer summer internships to spring interns during the spring internship. If you weren’t offered one, whether through your own failure or because the bank didn’t offer them, you’ll want to start applying to summer internships manually.
These can open really, really early, depending on where you are in the world (US opens earlier than EMEA – more on that here </news/the-best-time-to-apply-for-a-banking-job>). You’ll want to get your application in as soon as you can - there are limited places, and programs close when they are filled in many locations.
If banks like your resume, you can expect a barrage of digital interviews and online tests, such as HireVue </news/2022/10/hirevue-interview-banking> or HackerRank </news/2022/04/how-to-pass-a-hackerrank-test> tests, which banks use to screen applicants before interviewing </news/70-top-banking-interview-questions-and-how-to-answer-them> in person, usually at a “super day </news/if-a-bank-doesn-t-hire-you-after-5-interviews-you-re-in-trouble>” with multiple interviews. If you’re successful, you can expect an internship offers shortly after.
Brian Landeros </news/how-to-get-a-goldman-sachs-internship>, a former Morgan Stanley and Goldman Sachs intern (as well as an analyst at boutique bank </news/boutique-investment-banks> Evercore) says that the key is good timing and "an immense level of grit". Research is the key to both: "I literally came across all these resources online telling me how to network," says Landeros. "I started reaching out to alumni. There was all this stuff that I realised I needed to do." Networking and performing well on the internship are both important, but the critical factor is getting the internship to begin with. "The door opens up for one semester, and then it closes forever," Landeros warns.
How do I turn my internship into an analyst job?
This is also called “converting </news/finance/7-step-guide-converting-banking-summer-internship-job>” your internship.
The usual answers for converting an internship are to work as hard as you can, network like crazy, and try to fit into the firm’s culture. Get the coffee often and generally speaking be as helpful as you can.
One M&A MD told us </news/internship-conversion-banking> that banks value “proactive” employees and expect you to be both ask questions and to be intellectually curious. Other roles might expect you to solve issues by yourself, but banks value you more for doing something right, rather than necessarily independently.
“My mentor told me four tricks that turned the situation around,” the MD also told us. “Firstly, she said that every time I completed an assignment, I needed to ask for feedback right away. Secondly, she said that every time I completed a project, I needed to ask for informal feedback. Thirdly, she said I needed to arrange coffee chats with associates, directors and MDs in my team and adjacent teams to get to know people and learn about their roles. And fourthly, she said I needed to make it very clear to everyone that I was really enjoying my time with the bank.”
Don’t complain about the hours. Banks say they are keen to encourage a better work-life balance, but you’re still expected to answer a request for help at 7pm, even if you were planning to meet friends. Interns work shorter hours than analysts, but not a traditional 9-5. If you can’t stand intern hours, then analyst hours will drive you mad, and banking probably isn’t for you.
In an extensive X/Twitter post/tweet <https://x.com/HighyieldHarry/status/2058991029399810102?s=20> in May, finance influencer HighYieldHarry <https://x.com/HighyieldHarry/> detailed his advice for students who wanted to maximise their internships. Some of the advice is very curious: Harry recommends overcommunicating by saying “will do”, “sounds good”, and “on it” when given an instruction to demonstrate that they have been recognized.
His other advice includes not asking questions that have already been answered in an email, CIM, PowerPoint, or otherwise. “Any question you have should be an expansion or clarification point NOT something already clearly stated,” HighYieldHarry said.
He also recommends not taking a vacation during your internship. “You are trying out for a sports team – you haven’t made the team yet, but you’re getting the chance to make the big leagues. Would you leave the team in the middle of preseason before the roster is finalized?”
Lastly. HYH also suggested hard-saving your files frequently, networking with as many people as possible, maximising office times, avoiding the AI/Social Media brain rot rabbit hole, and being careful with how much you drink during happy hour.
What happens if I don’t get a job offer after my internship?
You might have an issue. Banks are extremely reliant on internship programs and actively seek to recruit people from them. Not being hired by the bank you interned for is not only a bad situation to be in in the moment, but could also potentially be a red flag to other prospective employers.
For one, you can apply directly to graduate analyst programs. This is what banks put analysts through when they are recruited. These are two-year programs, and at many banks you can apply to them “normally”. This is your first port of call.
Your second is an off-cycle internship. These are also for graduates and are generally longer (three to six months, sometimes up to 12) than summer internships, but not multi-year in length. Doing an off-cycle internship abroad, especially in Europe </news/no-spring-internship-bank>, can be a good “resetting” of your career. Off-cycle internships can sometimes be better than summer internships, as they offer you broader work experience opportunities.
It's also worth noting that you can still apply for internships even as you go into your final year at university. It's becoming increasingly common for students to complete internships during the summer after they graduate – although it might not be possible in investment banking, where your graduation date is part of the requirements.
How do you get around that? By doing a top-quality master’s degree </news/the-top-30-masters-in-finance-for-getting-a-job-in-investment-banking>, of course. It doesn’t put you in a better position to do one, but it does give you another shot at a career in banking. All it costs you is five figures (potentially six, in the USA) of debt. Hey, don’t look at us – you should have secured and converted your second-year summer internship to begin with.
The quality of a master’s in finance degree </news/masters-in-finance-worth-it> is also worth considering. While a top masters from a top university - such as St. Gallen or HEC - can effectively give you an extra year in which to "lock in" and get the job you've been praying for, others "will charge you $100k and leave you to rot," we were told by former careers coach Mark Ross.
If you're in the US, Brian Landeros </news/how-to-get-a-goldman-sachs-internship> also thinks that delaying your graduation can be worth it. "There are candidates who get to the second year of college and who had no idea what banking was but are suddenly interested in the industry," Landeros says. He suggests delaying your graduation date as much as possible and applying like crazy to the internships that you are still eligible for. Internships are really, really important.
Have a confidential story, tip, or comment you’d like to share? Contact: +44 7537 182250 (SMS, WhatsApp or voicemail). Telegram: @SarahButcher. Signal: sarahbutcher.22 Click here to fill in our anonymous form </about/editor-tips>, or email editortips@efinancialcareers.com.
Bear with us if you leave a comment at the bottom of this article: comments are moderated intermittently by human beings. Sometimes these humans might be asleep, or away from their desks, so it may take a while for your comment to appear. You must take sole responsibility for comments you post on this site. We will take reasonable steps to weed out anything that we consider to be offensive or inappropriate.
