item.title: Banking internships: A complete guide to summer analyst programs
item.description:
Getting a job in an investment bank </news/what-is-an-investment-bank> is tough. If you want to work in popular front office roles like M&A </news/what-are-ma-jobs> or sales & trading </news/how-do-you-get-a-job-in-sales-and-trading-in-a-bank> you need to get in very early in your career. If you wait and decide you want to work in banking in your 30s, it's too late. It's often even too late if you wait until you've graduated. So how do you get into an investment bank? It's simple: with an internship.
Click here to join the bubble by eFinancialCareers, our new anonymous community. <https://bubble.efinancialcareers.com/login?_gl=1*ut8n6u*_gcl_au*MTg4MDY2NTExNi4xNzYxNTU3NDIz*_ga*ODczNzI2NTA0LjE3NTM2OTk2ODI.*_ga_NZFQV4MXPM*czE3NjM2NDM0MDYkbzI1NiRnMSR0MTc2MzY0MzQzOSRqMjckbDAkaDMyMjc4OTUzMw..>✍️
Investment banks recruit their graduates from the 10 week+ summer internships they offer to penultimate-year students. In the UK at least, these students are often already prepped and groomed through a spring internship </news/spring-week-bank> in the first year of their studies.
This internship pipeline gives banks a steady stream of fresh talent that they've had a chance to test in the workplace. As a candidate, once you’re in the internship pipeline, you’ve got a great chance to stay in it. At least half of students who complete summer internships usually get return offers. The hardest part is getting an internship in the first place. Goldman Sachs and JPMorgan, which run some of the largest intern classes in the industry, have acceptance rates of around 0.7% each.
Getting a summer internship – and understanding the machinery and logic behind them, as well as how to excel on yours – are therefore critical if you are aiming for a career in an investment bank, particularly if you want to be a banker </news/the-investment-banking-recruitment-process> or a trader </news/how-do-you-get-a-job-in-sales-and-trading-in-a-bank>. All roles in banks offer internships though, including in areas like technology </news/how-to-get-a-technology-job-bank>, risk </news/risk-job-investment-bank> and compliance </news/how-to-get-a-job-in-compliance>.
What are investment banking summer internships exactly?
Summer internships in banks are eight- to 12- week long programs, with some variation in the exact length depending on bank and program. There are two main types of summer internships: summer analyst programs, for penultimate-year undergraduates, and summer associate programs, for MBA students </news/best-mba-for-finance-banking>. In the investment banking hierarchy </news/banks-weird-hierarchies-analysts-associates-vps-mds>, associates are a step above analysts, reflecting the fact that MBA students typically have a few years of experience under their belts.
There are also off-cycle internships, typically for last-year undergraduate students who missed out on summer internships. Off-cycle internships </news/no-spring-internship-bank>can last anywhere from 3 to 12 months and are available in a wide variety of locations and business areas.
Banks usually offer internships across all their divisions. In the front office </news/front-office-middle-office-back-office-banking> of an investment bank, this includes global markets, which comprises sales and trading </news/how-do-you-get-a-job-in-sales-and-trading-in-a-bank> (and research </news/research-job-investment-bank>), as well as corporate finance, mergers and acquisitions </news/what-are-ma-jobs>, and capital markets </news/capital-markets-jobs-ecm-and-dcm>. Internships are also available across everything from risk </news/risk-job-investment-bank> and quantitative </news/quant-jobs-finance> finance to operations </news/operations-jobs-banking> and compliance </news/how-to-get-a-job-in-compliance>.
As well as summer internships and off-cycle internships, banks offer spring internships </news/spring-week-bank>. These are week long events for first-year students and run during the Easter break. Spring internships are short introductions to banking, but they're increasingly important. Some banks recruit summer interns from them. They're common in Europe – but they’re starting to be phased out in America </news/bank-diversity-programs?utm_source=chatgpt.com>, where they were a lynchpin in how banks organized their diversity hiring.
Spring internships can be very useful for your application. Data from Trackr (formerly the Bristol Trackr) suggested in April </news/how-many-intern-applications> that students with spring internships under their belt had to make just 73 applications on average for a successful summer internship acceptance, compared to 138 on average for students without previous spring internships.
What are my chances of getting in?
Low. And getting lower.
As we noted above, at Goldman Sachs, the acceptance rate </news/goldman-sachs-internship-acceptance-rate> of interns declined from 1.27% in 2022 to 0.72% in 2025. Why? Because while the number of open internship places increased from 3,000 to 3,600 in that period, the number of people applying to them increased from 236k to around 359k.
The change was even more stark in other places. JPMorgan had 505k applicants to 4,500 internships in 2024; the next year, it had 630k applicants to just 4,100 internships.
In all likelihood, things will get even worse in the future. Two years ago </news/jpmorgan-ai>, there were whispers that Goldman Sachs and Morgan Stanley would cut their summer internship cohorts by two-thirds to accommodate the increased productivity offered by AI-powered bankers. That hasn’t come to pass, but class sizes are shrinking, and AI adoption is rising.
It also matters where and what you study </news/finance/the-top-100-universities-globally-for-careers-in-banking-and-finance>. Although banks are increasingly interested in hiring from a wide group of universities, it still helps to be at a university with a history of placing people into banking, so that it can coach you on how to handle the internship. "It really helps for them to have a conversation with people who went through the application process and had their first steps in the job previously. It also helps them to understand more about the bank, the culture, the teams,” said an HEC professor who previously worked for Barclays.
Trackrr, formerly the Bristol Tracker, said in a report </news/how-many-intern-applications> in April that the average student who received an internship offer in the UK in 2026 applied to over 100 programs, up from 68 in 2024 and 75 in 2025. The figures were slightly better for diverse students, including women, who applied to just 79 programs on average, and black people who applied to less than 50 programs on average.
What can I expect from a banking internship?
Different banks structure their internships in different ways. Internships are also typically structured differently depending upon the area of the bank you're working in.
Summer internships typically start with one week of online and classroom training followed by hands-on work experience with a particular “desk”, or team. In some banks (such as Goldman Sachs </news/how-to-get-a-job-goldman-sachs>), sales & trading </news/how-do-you-get-a-job-in-sales-and-trading-in-a-bank> internships will offer a “rotation”, where you work on different desks to gain broader experience and see which of the teams you best mesh with.
Generally speaking, interns do the same things that analysts do, but on a smaller scale. In a 2024 video, former JPMorgan analyst Cameron Galbraith said <https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=EB-kSZEP6Z4> that an intern would be handed “a few slides” of a large deck to work on and to received feedback on. Galbraith did say, however, that you should do more of the deck as a learning exercise, and to be useful to your team.
Galbraith also suggests asking “thoughtful” questions to your analyst, and to “impress” them by suggesting ideas – even if 99% of the time they will definitely not be used. But merely showing bigger picture thinking will do a lot to impress.
Kunal Shah, Co-CEO of Goldman Sachs International (the firm’s UK branch) and its global co-head of fixed income, currencies, and commodities, said recently <https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=qFKSW5a7q_c> that his 10 week internship at the firm was where he could “really get tested out”, in situations where his supervisors could see not just what he knew, but how he reacted to new bits of information.
Aside from what’s traditionally important, interns in today’s day and age are expected to have extreme AI fluency. Fabian Figi, the head of campus recruiting at market making firm Citadel Securities, told Business Insider <https://www.businessinsider.com/wall-street-summer-interns-ai-banks-2026-5> in May that the firm’s "AI native" interns would take on more "meaningful responsibility very early on, including work focused on applying AI to complex real-world problems."
Shah said today's interns can make themselves valuable by displaying their technological prowess. “Junior talent are inherently tech-savvy, and they don't have the legacy of why we do things in a certain way. They can see how to disrupt us,” he said. Shah also told students not to fear the risk of AI killing the job you're moving into. When he did his internship in 2003, he was told not to rotate onto the fixed income desk as an intern due to pending automation. In fact, he said automation simply meant that he did not have to collect physical trade tickets or book travel or type so many things into spreadsheets.
For the AI-powered interns of the future, this means automated building of spreadsheet models and standardizing PowerPoint formats. The job became more interesting without spending time booking travel – and the hope is that it can become more interesting without fixing pitchbooks.
Will I get paid for the internship?
Yes. Interns in investment banks are paid the same as full time junior analysts (although without the bonuses </news/banker-bonuses>) and intern salaries are pro-rated for the length of the internship.
You can therefore expect to earn around $20k (£15k) from a summer analyst internship, but given what you could earn as a banker, the money is not as important as the experience.
For MBA students, the figures are similar. NYU’s employment report <https://www.gsb.stanford.edu/organizations/recruit/strategies-resources/employment-reports/summer> for MBA students showed that the average investment banking summer intern earned $3.3k in weekly pay, or around $26k - $30k depending on the length of the internship.
How do I apply for a banking internship?
The investment banking recruitment process </news/the-investment-banking-recruitment-process> is a long thing. Generally speaking, the process goes like this:
In your first year at a top university, you are a top student academically with a wide range of extracurricular pastimes, especially sports. You put together your CV expertly </news/the-absolutely-perfect-investment-banking-cv>, and if you're in Europe you apply for spring weeks at the most prestigious investment banks. They like your CV, they offer you an interview, you ace the interview, and then they offer you a spring week. You can do multiple spring weeks, if you have the time or inclination. It’s not unheard of to complete as many as nine </news/spring-week-internship-banking>.
Some banks offer summer internships to spring interns during the spring internship. If you weren’t offered one, whether through your own failure or because the bank didn’t offer them, you’ll want to start applying to summer internships manually.
These can open really, really early, depending on where you are in the world (US opens earlier than EMEA – more on that here </news/the-best-time-to-apply-for-a-banking-job>). You’ll want to get your application in as soon as you can - there are limited places, and programs close when they are filled in many locations.
If banks like your resume, you can expect a barrage of digital interviews and online tests, such as HireVue </news/2022/10/hirevue-interview-banking> or HackerRank </news/2022/04/how-to-pass-a-hackerrank-test> tests, which banks use to screen applicants before interviewing </news/70-top-banking-interview-questions-and-how-to-answer-them> in person, usually at a “super day </news/if-a-bank-doesn-t-hire-you-after-5-interviews-you-re-in-trouble>” with multiple interviews. If you’re successful, you can expect an internship offers shortly after.
Brian Landeros </news/how-to-get-a-goldman-sachs-internship>, a former Morgan Stanley and Goldman Sachs intern (as well as an analyst at boutique bank </news/boutique-investment-banks> Evercore) says that the key is good timing and "an immense level of grit". Research is the key to both: "I literally came across all these resources online telling me how to network," says Landeros. "I started reaching out to alumni. There was all this stuff that I realised I needed to do." Networking and performing well on the internship are both important, but the critical factor is getting the internship to begin with. "The door opens up for one semester, and then it closes forever," Landeros warns.
How do I turn my internship into an analyst job?
This is also called “converting </news/finance/7-step-guide-converting-banking-summer-internship-job>” your internship.
The usual answers for converting an internship are to work as hard as you can, network like crazy, and try to fit into the firm’s culture. Get the coffee often and generally speaking be as helpful as you can.
One M&A MD told us </news/internship-conversion-banking> that banks value “proactive” employees and expect you to be both ask questions and to be intellectually curious. Other roles might expect you to solve issues by yourself, but banks value you more for doing something right, rather than necessarily independently.
“My mentor told me four tricks that turned the situation around,” the MD also told us. “Firstly, she said that every time I completed an assignment, I needed to ask for feedback right away. Secondly, she said that every time I completed a project, I needed to ask for informal feedback. Thirdly, she said I needed to arrange coffee chats with associates, directors and MDs in my team and adjacent teams to get to know people and learn about their roles. And fourthly, she said I needed to make it very clear to everyone that I was really enjoying my time with the bank.”
Don’t complain about the hours. Banks say they are keen to encourage a better work-life balance, but you’re still expected to answer a request for help at 7pm, even if you were planning to meet friends. Interns work shorter hours than analysts, but not a traditional 9-5. If you can’t stand intern hours, then analyst hours will drive you mad, and banking probably isn’t for you.
In an extensive X/Twitter post/tweet <https://x.com/HighyieldHarry/status/2058991029399810102?s=20> in May, finance influencer HighYieldHarry <https://x.com/HighyieldHarry/> detailed his advice for students who wanted to maximise their internships. Some of the advice is very curious: Harry recommends overcommunicating by saying “will do”, “sounds good”, and “on it” when given an instruction to demonstrate that they have been recognized.
His other advice includes not asking questions that have already been answered in an email, CIM, PowerPoint, or otherwise. “Any question you have should be an expansion or clarification point NOT something already clearly stated,” HighYieldHarry said.
He also recommends not taking a vacation during your internship. “You are trying out for a sports team – you haven’t made the team yet, but you’re getting the chance to make the big leagues. Would you leave the team in the middle of preseason before the roster is finalized?”
Lastly. HYH also suggested hard-saving your files frequently, networking with as many people as possible, maximising office times, avoiding the AI/Social Media brain rot rabbit hole, and being careful with how much you drink during happy hour.
What happens if I don’t get a job offer after my internship?
You might have an issue. Banks are extremely reliant on internship programs and actively seek to recruit people from them. Not being hired by the bank you interned for is not only a bad situation to be in in the moment, but could also potentially be a red flag to other prospective employers.
For one, you can apply directly to graduate analyst programs. This is what banks put analysts through when they are recruited. These are two-year programs, and at many banks you can apply to them “normally”. This is your first port of call.
Your second is an off-cycle internship. These are also for graduates and are generally longer (three to six months, sometimes up to 12) than summer internships, but not multi-year in length. Doing an off-cycle internship abroad, especially in Europe </news/no-spring-internship-bank>, can be a good “resetting” of your career. Off-cycle internships can sometimes be better than summer internships, as they offer you broader work experience opportunities.
It's also worth noting that you can still apply for internships even as you go into your final year at university. It's becoming increasingly common for students to complete internships during the summer after they graduate – although it might not be possible in investment banking, where your graduation date is part of the requirements.
How do you get around that? By doing a top-quality master’s degree </news/the-top-30-masters-in-finance-for-getting-a-job-in-investment-banking>, of course. It doesn’t put you in a better position to do one, but it does give you another shot at a career in banking. All it costs you is five figures (potentially six, in the USA) of debt. Hey, don’t look at us – you should have secured and converted your second-year summer internship to begin with.
The quality of a master’s in finance degree </news/masters-in-finance-worth-it> is also worth considering. While a top masters from a top university - such as St. Gallen or HEC - can effectively give you an extra year in which to "lock in" and get the job you've been praying for, others "will charge you $100k and leave you to rot," we were told by former careers coach Mark Ross.
If you're in the US, Brian Landeros </news/how-to-get-a-goldman-sachs-internship> also thinks that delaying your graduation can be worth it. "There are candidates who get to the second year of college and who had no idea what banking was but are suddenly interested in the industry," Landeros says. He suggests delaying your graduation date as much as possible and applying like crazy to the internships that you are still eligible for. Internships are really, really important.
Have a confidential story, tip, or comment you’d like to share? Contact: +44 7537 182250 (SMS, WhatsApp or voicemail). Telegram: @SarahButcher. Signal: sarahbutcher.22 Click here to fill in our anonymous form </about/editor-tips>, or email editortips@efinancialcareers.com.
Bear with us if you leave a comment at the bottom of this article: comments are moderated intermittently by human beings. Sometimes these humans might be asleep, or away from their desks, so it may take a while for your comment to appear. You must take sole responsibility for comments you post on this site. We will take reasonable steps to weed out anything that we consider to be offensive or inappropriate.

item.title: Using English as a coding language won't get you a $600k programming job
item.description:
Many engineers in finance and beyond are using AI </news/junior-banker-ai>to make themselves more productive, but vibe coding alone won't get you the most lucrative engineering roles in finance. The highest paying coding jobs often involve C++, and its creator refutes claims from those at Goldman Sachs </news/english-as-a-coding-language-llm> and beyond that English is taking over as the new programming language. </news/best-paid-programming-language>
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Bjarne Stroustrup, creator of C++, said on a podcast last month <https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=U46fJ2bJ-co> that English is unsuitable as a programming language in the low-latency space because it's "flexible" and "often very ambiguous." He said that C++, on the other hand, is "precise," with a foundation in mathematics. Because of this, hedge funds and trading firms pay up to $600k for C++ engineers </news/low-latency-c> who are specialists in low latency coding. Coding at this level often requires engineers to fine-tune their code to an extreme degree. Stroustrup said "the examples I've seen of attempts for AI to generate code in this domain have not been successful." Part of this is due to the fact that AI agents will edit across your entire codebase whereas human coders will edit code on a much more localised basis. "If an AI writes it, you don't actually know where it has changed [the code]... you need to try and figure it out," Stroustrup said.
Stroustrup also said that AI-generated low latency C++ code is "bloated" and has a higher propensity for bugs and security issues. The talent pool for elite C++ engineers has been notoriously dry for years </news/finance/why-is-there-a-drought-in-the-talent-pool-for-c-developers>, which means the AI will often train itself on subpar developers, "imitating old code and getting old performance and old bugs." He said it's such an issue that senior C++ developers are retiring as they don't want to spend all their time evaluating and editing buggy, bloated, AI-written code.
Not all of the top paying firms use C++, however. Jane Street, the trading firm that pays $2.7m per head, uses OCaml, </news/2023/11/ocaml-vs-c-high-frequency-trading>another, more niche low-latency language. Ron Minsky, Jane Street's CTO, seems to echo Stroustrup's assertion that programming languages need a mathematical foundation. Speaking on Jane Street's Signals and Threads podcast <https://signalsandthreads.com/the-network-as-a-program/> yesterday, he said that "a lot of the best ideas in programming languages come from relating the thing you’re doing to very simple mathematical models."
Jane Street seems to prefer a bit more flexibility than Stroustrop, however. Minsky said coding is a "messy and very human process," and that it picked OCaml to use as it accommodates for that; he said it's "like a fancy, wild, high level language," but also "easy to understand from looking at the OCaml code, how that code is going to execute."
Have a confidential story, tip, or comment you’d like to share? Contact: WhatsApp: http://wa.me/442079977910 <http://wa.me/442079977910> (+44 20 7997 7910), Telegram: @AlexMcMurray, Signal: @AlexMcMurrayEFC.88 Click here to fill in our anonymous form </about/editor-tips>, or email editortips@efinancialcareers.com.
Bear with us if you leave a comment at the bottom of this article: comments are moderated intermittently by human beings. Sometimes these humans might be asleep, or away from their desks, so it may take a while for your comment to appear. You must take sole responsibility for comments you post on this site. We will take reasonable steps to weed out anything that we consider to be offensive or inappropriate.

item.title: Morning Coffee: JPMorgan's overlooked banker found a bigger job elsewhere. Inside the Goldman Sachs client menu
item.description:
If you're planning an IPO in London and are looking for Shameer Patel on the JPMorgan London syndicate desk, you will need to cast your eyes elsewhere. After 18 years at JPMorgan, IFR reported yesterday <https://www.ifre.com/people-and-markets/2436084/moves-patel-to-run-equity-syndicate-at-bnp-paribas> that Shameer has taken himself to BNP Paribas instead.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
At JPMorgan, Shameer was just an executive director in equity capital markets </news/capital-markets-jobs-ecm-and-dcm> (ECM) syndicate in EMEA. He didn't respond to a request to comment for this article, but we understand that he was around five notches below the bank's global markets leadership. At BNP Paribas, by comparison, Shameer will be running the whole of global equity syndicate. BNP Paribas wanted Shameer so much that Bloomberg reported (on the terminal) that the French bank created a whole new role for him. He will move up several notches and report to Andreas Bernstorff, BNP's global head of ECM.
Shameer's exit looks like a bit of a loss for JPMorgan, which didn't even promote him to managing director. The bank declined to comment for this article, but it's thought that Shameer had been a mainstay on the JPM syndicate desk for years. BNP Paribas presumably saw something in him that JPM had overlooked.
Shameer's departure is understood to have been a while in the offing, but it follows the arrival of ex-Citi banker Suneel Hargunani </news/jp-morgan-s-latest-big-citi-hire-might-be-joined-by-more-after-bonuses> as head of EMEA syndicate at JPMorgan in March. Hargunani's arrival is not thought to have provoked Shameer's departure, but nor is it inconceivable that Shameer may have wanted Hargunani's job.
None of this matters now, because Shameer is now an even bigger beast at BNP Paribas. Moving to a European bank </news/i-left-a-top-us-bank-for-a-european-rival-and-it-was-pitiful>is often an opportunity to take on more responsibility. Hopefully Shameer will thrive in the more European culture </news/i-left-a-top-us-bank-for-a-european-rival-and-it-was-pitiful>there. BNP Paribas declined to comment.
Separately, Goldman Sachs has been entertaining its most important clients at its annual annual leveraged finance and credit conference in California. Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-06-01/for-goldman-s-top-bankers-it-s-all-ai-data-centers-all-the-time?srnd=phx-finance>reports that nearly 500 clients attended and that the main topic of conversation was funding AI and its enormous infrastructure requirements. “There’s such an enormous capex need across data centers, power, chips, that is so large that it really touches every market that we are involved in,” Miriam Wheeler, Goldman Sachs global head of leveraged finance, declared in a side interview. “For our capital solutions group right now, AI is probably the number one theme that we’re spending time on.”
The conference took place in the Waldorf Astoria's Monarch Beach Resort <https://www.waldorfastoriamonarchbeach.com/>, which offers a private beach club and an array of blue umbrellas. While they considered how to muster up the trillions required <https://www.goldmansachs.com/insights/articles/tracking-trillions-the-assumptions-shaping-scale-of-the-ai-build-out> to sustain the AI boom, Bloomberg says attendees were treated to a combination of mahi-mahi, crumbed chicken, lemon potatoes and gnocchi. Mahi-mahi is a reportedly a dolphin-like fish associated with food poisoning in some situations but there is no sign that the data centre developers of the future experienced any indigestion as they discussed invetment to come.
Meanwhile...
Goldman Sachs, JPMorgan and Morgan Stanley are expected to feature on Anthropic's enormous IPO. (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-06-01/anthropic-files-confidentially-for-ipo-as-claude-demand-surges>
Deutsche Bank's fixed income traders are unhealthily successful. Since Christian Sewing took as CEO over in 2018, FIC revenues have grown over 30 percentage points more than those in the corporate bank. “The plan is for the corporate bank to grow faster than the investment bank,” says Fabrizio Campelli, head of the corporate and investment bank. (Financial Times) <https://www.ft.com/content/7699c69c-59c1-4a93-8ea5-ce2bda84bfec?syn-25a6b1a6=1>
ArrowPoint Investment Partners, an Asia-focused hedge fund, has $2bn of capital and wants $3bn by the second half. It has 20 investment teams and has signed up 10 more. (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-06-01/hedge-fund-arrowpoint-hires-10-trading-teams-seeks-more-capital>
Cambridge University graduate and quant trader Richard Ho was charged with using Headlands Technologies' source code to set up his own firm. He argued that the charges were too vague, but the case is now going ahead in the US. (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-06-01/quant-fails-to-get-1-billion-source-code-theft-charge-tossed?taid=6a1dc7cfabb4210001d0841a&utm_campaign=trueanthem&utm_content=business&utm_medium=social&utm_source=twitter>
Questions are being asked about interactions between Mary Erdoes at JPMorgan and Jeffrey Epstein. “If Erdoes was not as senior or as powerful as she currently is at JPMorgan, I don’t think she would still be working there. They would have most likely let her go,” says one investor. (The Banker) <https://www.thebanker.com/content/68728ef9-d3e9-41f4-9c11-5c69e5980d73?utm_medium=social&utm_source=linkedin>
Paul V. Morris, a private banker who worked with Jeffrey Epstein, left his job at Bank of America. (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-06-01/former-epstein-banker-paul-morris-departs-bofa-s-merrill-unit?srnd=phx-finance>
Goldman lost US$960 million on three days during the first quarter, followed by Bank of America with $376 million and JP Morgan with US$182 million on two days apiece. (Global Trading) <https://www.globaltrading.net/us-banks-use-intraday-trading-to-hide-us1-6bn-of-var-backtest-losses/>
Christopher Willcox, head of Nomura's investment bank, says banks will cut thousands of jobs due to AI but it will be a good thing for small banks. (Financial News) <https://www.fnlondon.com/articles/nomuras-wholesale-boss-willcox-ai-arms-race-will-see-big-banks-cut-thousands-of-jobs-a6cf7db6>
This summer's internships will be about AI and intuition. "It's much less about pure technical ability, and it's much more about emotional intelligence," he said. "We need kids that are going to become great long-term professionals, not kids that are going to make great analysts, because they're going to have to go out of that analyst job very, very quickly." (Business Insider) <https://www.businessinsider.com/wall-street-summer-interns-ai-banks-2026-5>
Revolut wants 200 people in France. (Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-06-01/revolut-vows-to-hire-200-more-france-staff-in-europe-growth-push?srnd=homepage-uk>)
BlackRock, Fidelity, Neuberger, Allianz, AllianceBernstein and Schroders all set up wholly owned mutual fund businesses after China opened up the sector in 2020. They have a 0.1% market share. (Financial Times) <https://www.ft.com/content/cd3a8ce6-9d2c-4dc5-8fee-377b9a9a5a7b?syn-25a6b1a6=1>
It's not easy being Andrew Bailey at the Bank of England; it's not bad either. "He’s got a shocking giggle. He’s quite a gossip. You’d wander by his desk, sit down, have a Quality Street — there was always a box of chocolates open — and share some stories or funny things that were happening. He was just quite fun to be with in a surprising way, at all times.” (Politico) <https://www.politico.eu/article/andrew-bailey-two-years-bank-of-england/>
25% of Americans in their 40s go at least five years without a real boost in pay or position. (WSJ) <https://www.wsj.com/lifestyle/careers/white-collar-workers-career-nyu-study-a81a7d9c?mod=hp_lead_pos11>
Inside a contemporary maths exam. 'Students were pulling out phones and taking photographs of the test to submit to LLMs before copying down machine-written responses into their blue books.' (TheNewCritic) <https://www.thenewcritic.com/p/the-great-zombification>

item.title: Meet A Recruiter
item.description:
In our recent LinkedIn ‘Meet a Recruiter’ series we highlighted three of our global recruiters - Supriya Davda, Grace Duggan and Yogitha Kotian.
This new series was all about looking inside our Talent Acquisition team at Northern Trust and sharing a bit about the people tasked with bringing in great talent to our organization.
Read more below from our recruiters as they give an insight into who they are, the work they do and their top tips when applying to Northern Trust.
Meet a Recruiter: Supriya Davda, London <https://www.linkedin.com/pulse/meet-recruiter-supriya-davda-northern-trust-xqvle/?trackingId=uW3G5qYgqD9Fs%2B3HInIDuA%3D%3D>
Supriya Davda <https://www.linkedin.com/in/supriya-davda-76b096b/?lipi=urn%3Ali%3Apage%3Ad_flagship3_pulse_read%3BBEFs%2FvzoSau8vMK2vB2%2FTA%3D%3D> currently recruits for Northern Trust Asset Servicing and Technology across MENA (Middle East and North Africa) and EMEA (Europe, Middle East and Africa).
Supriya is based in London and is always on the lookout for Fund Accounting, Financial Reporting, Project Managers and Client Services experts in the UK, Saudi Arabia and Abu Dhabi.
Here's more from Supriya:
💡 What’s the best part of your job as a recruiter for Northern Trust?
The best part of my job as a recruiter at Northern Trust is the variety it offers—I’m constantly working on different roles and remits, which keeps the work dynamic and engaging. I also truly enjoy collaborating with our stakeholders and partners, who are not only supportive but also open to bringing in fresh talent to help the business thrive.
Northern Trust stands out as an organization that genuinely cares for its employees and consistently strives to create an environment that fosters growth, well-being, and success for everyone involved.
💡 As a recruiter, what makes an impact on you / creates an impression?
As a recruiter, what truly makes an impact on me is encountering candidates who demonstrate honesty, ambition, and enthusiasm. I am impressed by individuals who show a genuine openness to learn, grow, and contribute meaningfully to the organization.
Additionally, I value compassion—those who show care and consideration for others around them often stand out as future collaborators and leaders.
💡 What is your top tip for someone applying to a role at Northern Trust?
My top tip for someone applying to a role at Northern Trust is to come prepared. Take the time to research who we are as an organization and what we stand for. Understanding our values, culture, and commitment to excellence will not only help you align your goals with ours but also allow you to present yourself as a well-informed candidate.
💡 Best piece of advice you’ve ever received (career or otherwise)?
The best piece of advice I’ve ever received is to remain true to who you are and stay authentic. Authenticity builds trust, fosters meaningful connections, and ensures that your personal and professional choices align with your values.
Read more from Supriya here in the full LinkedIn article. <https://www.linkedin.com/pulse/meet-recruiter-supriya-davda-northern-trust-xqvle/?trackingId=uW3G5qYgqD9Fs%2B3HInIDuA%3D%3D>
Meet a Recruiter: Grace Duggan, Chicago <https://www.linkedin.com/pulse/meet-recruiter-grace-duggan-northern-trust-ogeve/?trackingId=aXyiy%2B4Y04J9bHHEh%2Ff02A%3D%3D>
Grace Duggan <https://www.linkedin.com/in/grace-duggan-58a381b8/?lipi=urn%3Ali%3Apage%3Ad_flagship3_pulse_read%3BjXDGgDKjRHyJRUgQfu%2FcqA%3D%3D> recruits for Northern Trust Wealth Management and is on the lookout for people with these skillsets in Chicago, IL 1) wealth planning 2) client service 3) business development and 4) project management skills.
Hear her perspective on Northern Trust and our hiring process below.
💡 What’s the best part of your job as a recruiter for Northern Trust?
Getting to offer people jobs and hear their excitement!
A career move is a big decision for most people and I really enjoy being a small part of that.
Additionally, as a recruiter, you have the opportunity to work with a variety of different stakeholders and people within the organization, and each time is a new learning opportunity for me. It keeps things exciting and challenging.
💡 What are your recruiting areas of focus?
Wealth Management – specifically our Global Family Office, Program Management Office and Special Assets. These areas all seek unique skillsets and backgrounds.
💡 As a recruiter, what makes an impact on you / creates an impression?
First, is preparation - when a candidate is prepared for their interview and demonstrates a genuine interest in both the role they are interviewing for, and for Northern Trust, that makes an impact.
Second, is enthusiasm – candidates that have made an impression on me are those that are excited about the work they do and the potential work and impact they can have at Northern Trust.
💡 What is your top tip for someone applying to a role at Northern Trust?
My top tip for someone applying to a role at Northern Trust is to be thoughtful and do your research. It’s important to understand the organization, the people and the culture. It’s also important to be open-minded as you look at different roles.
💡 Tell us your top interview tip?
Similar to the above - prepare and relax!
We are interviewing you, but you are also interviewing us. It's important that you are assessing if the role and an organization is a right fit for you as well.
💡 Best piece of advice you’ve ever received (career or otherwise)?
Be a team player and say yes!
Get involved and always be willing to help others. This advice has served me well both professionally and personally.
Read more from Grace here in the full LinkedIn article. <https://www.linkedin.com/pulse/meet-recruiter-grace-duggan-northern-trust-ogeve/?trackingId=aXyiy%2B4Y04J9bHHEh%2Ff02A%3D%3D>
Meet a Recruiter: Yogitha Kotian, Bangalore <https://www.linkedin.com/pulse/meet-recruiter-yogitha-kotian-northern-trust-xnh3e/>
Yogitha Kotian <https://www.linkedin.com/in/yogitha-kotian-1757595a/?lipi=urn%3Ali%3Apage%3Ad_flagship3_pulse_read%3BHjZr64I8Q0qVmJTagMMX3w%3D%3D> currently recruits for Northern Trust Asset Servicing and is on the lookout for people with Client Reporting, Risk Analytics, Fund Accounting, Business Analysis, Transfer Agencies and Change Management skills and experience.
💡 What’s the best part of your job as a recruiter for Northern Trust?
The best part of my job as a recruiter for Northern Trust is helping individuals take significant steps in their careers and witnessing their excitement when they see how our opportunities align with their aspirations.
It's deeply fulfilling to connect talented professionals with roles where they can grow, make an impact and thrive within an organization that genuinely values their potential and contributions.
💡 As a recruiter, what makes an impact on you / creates an impression?
What also makes an impact is when individuals show genuine enthusiasm for the role and organization and with a clear understand of how they can contribute to its success.
I am inspired by individuals who share stories of overcoming challenges and how those experiences shaped their growth stand out and left a lasting impression.
💡 What is your top tip for someone applying to a role at Northern Trust?
My top tip for someone applying to a role with us is to thoroughly understand the company’s values of service, expertise and integrity. You can learn more about our values and our principles here.
Ensure that you share your previous experiences and how they align with our values and demonstrate how you can positively contribute to our organization's culture.
💡 Tell us your top interview tip?
Research all about the company you are applying for. From their careers website to their social media presence and what their leaders are talking about in public.
Have a strong understanding of the roles and responsibilities within the given position you are applying for.
Please make sure to highlight your achievements during the interview, be punctual, show enthusiasm, stay confident and remain calm throughout.
Read more from Yogitha here in the full LinkedIn article. <https://www.linkedin.com/pulse/meet-recruiter-yogitha-kotian-northern-trust-xnh3e/>
Come find your greater at Northern Trust. Review our open roles here <https://careers.northerntrust.com/>.
item.author: editor@efinancialcareers.com (Northern Trust)

item.title: "I left a top US bank for a European rival and it was pitiful"
item.description:
I spent most of my career working for a US investment bank </news/what-is-an-investment-bank>. It was one of the US investment banks that everyone wants to work for, but after two decades and a role running a major team, I took a call from a headhunter. He was offering me a chance to take a bigger job at a well known European bank that was expanding in New York.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
It seemed like an interesting proposition. I took the job. I have regretted it ever since.
When you are used to working for a US bank, European banks are completely different. When I moved, it was like night and day, with the European bank as the dark of night.
People at the European bank had no sense of urgency. At the American bank, you would never dream of booking a dentist appointment at 4.30pm or leaving the office at 5.01pm. At the European bank I worked for, this was the norm. People there had no urge to be the best; they just wanted to be enough. It was pitiful.
It became incredibly frustrating. US banks are like the special forces. They push you to use every single brain cell that you have. They hire the best people they can and they want to make them the better. Most people don't make the grade, but US banks can sniff out good people. Many people stay for 30 years. They are amazing places - the training, the development, the culture. Everything works. My days at the US bank were fabulous from start to finish.
I left because I wanted to push myself. I wanted to see if I could deliver in the bigger role and to know how I would function away from that well oiled machine. When you go somewhere that is less functional, you learn all the pieces that you don’t have in your jigsaw puzzle.
What I instead found was that the puzzle I was moving into was not a fit for me. The bank was not set up for someone used to working at my pace. I hired various colleagues from my former US employer and we all found the same thing. The atmosphere was so stifling that none of us could achieve anything.
We have all left again. I am currently out of the market. I made a terrible mistake. US banks are the creme de la creme. If you have a job with one, trust me that you must never be persuaded to leave.
Sidney Jordan is a pseudonym

item.title: Goldman Sachs and JPMorgan tech MDs are leaving for hedge fund Millennium
item.description:
It's a tale as old as time for a banking technologist to be tempted over to </news/2022/12/investment-banks-vs-hedge-funds-for-technologists> a multistrategy hedge fund </news/hedge-fund-technology-jobs>. Two MDs have recently trodden that beaten path and joined Millennium </news/how-to-get-a-job-at-hedge-fund-millennium>, despite working at different banks and in different continents.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Katarina Hunt joined Millennium in London as its head of risk </news/risk-job-investment-bank>engineering. She spent the last 13 years at Goldman Sachs, where she was most recently global head of engineering for equity derivatives and equity risk. We highlighted her as one of the fastest rising VPs at Goldman </news/2016/04/top-banking-vps-in-london> just over a decade ago; she made MD five years after that, and five years further on, she's now joining Millennium.
In New York, meanwhile, Millennium hired James Clennell from JPMorgan as its head of credit technology. He spent over two decades at the bank, starting as an analyst in its middle office and working his way up to MD in 2024. He was most recently global head of credit trading and risk management technology; he left the bank late last year and spent the first half of 2026 working on a motorsports data platform. </news/g-research-veteran-swaps-electronic-trading-for-ai-sports-analytics>
It's not all about hiring technologists from banks, or even hedge funds these days. Electronic trading firms </news/high-frequency-trading-hiring-and-pay> like Jane Street </news/how-to-get-a-job-at-jane-street>are on the rise and funds are building out their low latency architecture to compete with them. In April, we noted that Millennium hired Alexandre Fournier </news/millennium-quietly-hired-the-cto-of-a-major-hft-firm-to-work-on-nanosecond-latency-architecture>, former CTO of Tower Research Capital, for a senior role in its algorithmic execution team.
Millennium did not respond to a request for comment.

item.title: HSBC cut some bankers and its ex-trader CEO is pitching some deals
item.description:
It's not unusual for the CEOs of investment banks to personally pitch deals in an effort to make clients feel special. At Goldman Sachs, CEO David Solomon, for example, reportedly <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-05-20/goldman-ceo-slides-into-musk-s-dms-during-bid-to-lead-spacex-ipo> helped Kim Posnett </news/space-x-ipo-goldman-sachs-morgan-stanley>et al to win Goldman's lead role on the SpaceX IPO by sending some DMs to Elon Musk. With this possibly in mind, Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-05-31/hsbc-ceo-pitches-hk-clients-hires-more-bankers-after-setbacks?cmpid=060126_morningemea&utm_campaign=morningemea&utm_medium=email&utm_source=newsletter&utm_term=260601&utm_content=5039> reports that HSBC has deployed CEO Georges Elhedery to boost its fortunes in Asia.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Unlike Solomon, who cut his teeth as a high yield debt originator, Elhedery is a former rates trader. </news/georges-elhedery-hsbc>However, as the architect of HSBC's "ruthless" <https://www.ft.com/content/b18d7451-4703-49a2-a687-e56a375118d6?syn-25a6b1a6=1> overhaul </news/hsbc-s-self-proclaimed-ruthless-ceo-is-not-its-most-feared-person>in which he merged the corporate and investment banks and cut the M&A </news/get-a-job-in-ma> and equity capital markets </news/capital-markets-jobs-ecm-and-dcm> businesses outside North America, Asia and the Middle East </news/hscb-closure-europe-us-job-cuts>, he is well-placed to understand the strengths of its new slimline model. Bloomberg reports that Elhedery has been both pitching clients in Hong Kong and sending special video messages to clients in China. HSBC has also added over 12 new bankers for its China business in the past year.
Asia is important to HSBC. In client presentations <https://www.hsbc.com/investors/results-and-announcements/investor-events-and-presentations?page=1&take=20> last week, the bank said 65% of its profit before tax last year came from Asia (although only 16% came from the investment bank) and that the Asian business now accounts for around 50% of revenues in its corporate and institutional bank (CIB). Growing the CIB in Asia is key to growing the wealth business, declared Jo Miyake, head of banking for Asia and the Middle East at HSBC. HSBC has a "unique superpower" in having an international network.
There are signs of success. Bloomberg says HSBC is working on 40 Hong Kong IPOs. Last year it worked on five.
There are also signs of not-success. HSBC has a mere 0.8% market share of IPOs in Asia ex-Japan this year, says Bloomberg. As we were first to note </news/hsbc-s-pre-bonus-equities-job-cuts>in February, the bank hasn't won a role on CK Hutchison Holding's $2bn dual UK-Hong Kong listing of health and beauty retailer Watson Group. Insiders at the bank have suggested that this is because HSBC cut its equity capital markets bankers in London last year. "We don't have the London capabilities any more," said one insider. "If you want the big international clients, you at least need to have the front shop." Some insiders suggested previously </news/hsbc-cuts>that the bank had fired too many "hunters" who originate deals and kept too many expensive buffaloes who execute them.
HSBC declined to comment for this article. A spokesperson told Bloomberg that the bank is a "market leader" in Asia with "strong capital markets and advisory capabilities." Someone also said that HSBC may yet win a role on Watson Group mandate, so maybe there are still people in London after all.
Follow me on X <https://x.com/MadameButcher>. Follow me on LinkedIn. <https://www.linkedin.com/in/sarahbutcherjournalist/>

item.title: Goldman Sachs staff use their AI tools more than Citi colleagues
item.description:
With banks spending exorbitant amounts of money on building their AI </news/how-to-get-a-banking-ai-job> infrastructure, it helps to know that their employees are actually using it. Goldman Sachs released usage statistics of its AI-assistant platform, </news/junior-banker-ai> suggesting that tens of thousands of prompts </news/hedge-fund-says-it-s-not-easy-being-a-prompt-engineer> are made by Goldman's bankers, traders and engineers each day.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Goldman Sachs COO John Waldron, speaking at the Bernstein Strategic Decisions Conference last week, said that the bank had onboarded 40,000 staff to its AI platform, "which is darn near all of our employees." Last month, Waldron said they wrote a collective 2 million prompts. Usage has doubled over the past year; in July, Goldman staff averaged one million prompts per month.
This seems like a lot at first glance, but Goldman's current AI usage equates to just 1.7 prompts per employee per day. It's more than Citi employees, who created roughly one prompt per day in Q1 </news/citi-s-ai-driven-productivity-boom-spells-hard-times-for-banking-contractors#:~:text=For%20full%2Dtime,get%20too%20comfortable.> of this year using the bank's AI tools. Citi has over 5 times the total number of employees though, and employs more people in less technical roles like customer service.
Waldron said at the conference that Goldman is equally focused on "pushing hard to increase the literacy level" of staff, and said Goldman is "doing a lot of work teaching people, including myself, how to be a better prompter." This is done through the bank's 'digital advocacy' team, which is responsible for "driving [AI] adoption, developing and delivering training, and owning communications," according to open job listings. There are three openings available, based across Dallas and New York, all of which are focused on Goldman's asset management arm. </news/goldman-sachs-wealth-management-jobs>
Citi's process is different. It has a team of over 4,000 'AI accelerators' </news/citi-s-cto-says-these-are-the-kinds-of-jobs-he-s-eliminating-with-ai#:~:text=Griffiths%20said%20Citi,their%20AI%20usage.>who work on spreading AI awareness in the bank independently from their actual job. There are also ~30 AI 'champions' <https://www.businessinsider.com/citi-bank-ai-accelerators-volunteers-2025-12> who lead this effort for specific business lines. Other banks (and some hedge funds) often hire AI consultants to teach them how to prompt; this can earn you up to $12k per day, </news/jobs-teaching-ai-prompts>while associates in Goldman's digital advocacy teams are offered salaries of up to $160k in New York.
Some teams are using the tech more than others. Waldron highlighted that, in Goldman's tech teams </news/developers-are-doubling-their-code-output-with-ai-is-that-a-bad-thing>, there has been a 20% productivity increase broadly across the organization "but in some discrete cases, it's materially better than that."
Goldman Sachs and Citi staff still seem to be just dipping their toes into the water when it comes to AI. The same cannot be said of certain big tech firms who may be feeling some remorse for their rush to get their staff to use AI. Amazon had an internal leaderboard tracking AI usage <https://www.ft.com/content/b1a62a7f-6df5-4c90-94ce-64ce9c9961b6?syn-25a6b1a6=1>, but had taken it down after employees used it for superfluous actions to boost their scores at the expense of Amazon. Uber, meanwhile, is rumored to have burned through its entire AI coding tools budget <https://fortune.com/2026/05/26/uber-coo-ai-spending-tokens-claude-code/> in just four months.

item.title: A senior portfolio manager at Millennium in Hong Kong returned to where he came from
item.description:
The last time we wrote about Jonathan Vayn, it was 2015 </news/2015/07/the-ideal-recruit-for-this-high-paying-hedge-fund> and he had left SocGen for Capula in London.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Vayn did not respond to a request to comment for this article, but much has changed in his life since then. He moved to Hong Kong. He joined hedge fund </news/how-to-get-a-hedge-fund-job> Millennium </news/how-to-get-a-job-at-hedge-fund-millennium>. Then he left again.
Milllennium declined to comment for this article. Vayn, who specialises in trading volatility strategies, left Capula for Millennium in February 2022. He was a senior portfolio manager at Millennium for four years, but now he's back at Capula again.
We do not know why. Nor is it clear whether Vayn had a pod at Millennium which has been disbanded. Shao Ying, another senior Millennium trader specialising in relative value trades went to hedge fund Dymon Capital Asia </news/dymon-capital-asia>last year. The fund still employs people like Camille Modiano, a former JPMorgan MD who also trades volatility strategies.
Capula has been in Hong Kong since 2012.
Follow me on X <https://x.com/MadameButcher>. Follow me on LinkedIn. <https://www.linkedin.com/in/sarahbutcherjournalist/>

item.title: Morning Coffee: Citadel Securities is paying someone a lot of dividends. Hedge fund analysts vs. AI
item.description:
Citadel Securities </news/citadel-securities-secret-sauce-and-the-2m-pay-premium>, the electronic market making firm </news/difference-between-electronic-trading-firms>, is far more profitable than the trading arm of an investment bank </news/hudson-river-jane-street-trading-jobs>. But as it grows, there are signs that it's becoming proportionately less profitable on some measures than it used to be.
Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-05-29/citadel-securities-reels-in-record-4-3-billion-trading-haul> reported on Friday that revenues at the firm rose 28% year-on-year in the first quarter thanks to volatility, while profits rose by a lesser 10%. Citadel Securities generated $1.9bn of net income on $4.3bn of trading profits. A net profit margin of 44%.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
That's a fine amount, except that back in the first quarter of 2025, the <https://www.ft.com/content/bcec986d-52ac-47c5-a0c5-74cbe59dd524?syn-25a6b1a6=1>Financial Times <https://www.ft.com/content/d0f4e991-3f19-4ccd-9064-eaf7bfd5c474?syn-25a6b1a6=1> reported that Citadel Securities' generated a 70% increase in profits and a 45% increase in revenues, and had a margin of 50%.
Citadel Securities' falling net margin appears to be the result of its generous treatment of its equity holders. The firm is a partnership and in the first quarter of this year, Bloomberg noted that it made $1.25bn of "distributions" to equity holders. This includes many of its 1,800 employees who are also shareholders with substantial salaries and bonuses. </news/citadel-securities-pay-2m>
Citadel Securities is investing in growth, including in growing its people. In the recent past it's hired the likes of Nikhil Choraria </news/nikhil-choraria> from Goldman Sachs (who's due to arrive in September) and Elan Luger from JPMorgan (who's already there). Choraria </news/nikhil-choraria-citadel-securities>was Goldman's head of European rates trading and is unlikely to have come cheap. Luger was JPMorgan's global head of high touch equity sales trading and will also have commanded a large package.
Luger's arrival (along with a variety of other equities hires <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2025-11-06/citadel-securities-hires-sales-co-heads-for-block-equity-trades>) reflects Citadel Securities' move into the sorts of trades that require higher levels of human intervention. Speaking last month, Citadel Securities' president Jim Esposito </news/jim-esposito-citadel-securities-president> said the firm will be going after any trading product market where clients don't have access to liquidity or the best technology. That also includes investment grade credit.
While net profits at Citadel are falling, we understand that the EBITDA margin at the firm has remained stable at around 53%. Underlying profitability is therefore steady. It's just that the "distributions" to employees and other shareholders are rising.
Separately, being a hedge fund analyst </news/what-does-a-hedge-fund-analyst-do>in 2026 isn't the same as being a hedge fund analyst in 1996. In some ways, it's worse. In some ways, it's better.
It's worse because people like Joe O’Donnell, a 42 year-old "veteran" hedge fund analyst are pitching AI products to help do the jobs of analysts. The Wall Street Journal <https://www.wsj.com/finance/investing/the-hedge-fund-veteran-trying-to-make-his-past-self-obsolete-with-ai-714b58e4?mod=panda_wsj_author_alert> reports that O'Donnell has got something called Canary that can scour the internet for data that hasn't been publicly divulged and that can identify stocks that have been mispriced.
It's better because even in the age of AI, the best bit of being a hedge fund analyst </news/analyst-hedge-fund> still requires a human being. Speaking on a recent podcast <https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=vhTi_8QwXjg>, Dan Loeb at Third Point said that when he began his career as an analyst in the 1990s, he differentiated himself by spending a weekend reading a 3 inch thick file of papers on Drexel Burnham's bankruptcy. "It was super complicated," Loeb explained. "The claims were overstated, the assets were understated." Much money was made by people who'd got to grips with the document.
Today, AI can read the three inch stack of complicated papers. Loeb said that what makes an analyst great now is therefore not the willingness to grind through complex details all weekend, but being able to deeply understand "the nuances of a company of a technology." He used the example of an analyst who went into Casey's General Stores and ate pizza, thereby realising that it was a "pizza store masquerading as a general store" and was highly successful as a result.
Even O'Donnell admits that his AI cannot unearth these kinds of hidden facts. AI cannot converse with company management and quizzing them in person on granular aspects of their business, notes Bloomberg. Hedge fund analysts of the future need to be conversational sleuths. They may also be intrepid adventurers; witness Citrini Research </news/war-is-coming-for-banking-jobs>.
Meanwhile...
SquarePoint is building a prop trading arm under Kirill Gelman. Yiming Zhang is building a new prop trading business at Millennium. Voleon Group, run by DE Shaw executives, has set up Voleon Securities; Adam Kravetz is in charge of it. (Alphaville Substack) <https://ftav.substack.com/p/the-big-quant-convergence?utm_source=substack&publication_id=6156105&post_id=199577950&utm_medium=email&utm_content=share&utm_campaign=email-share&triggerShare=true&isFreemail=true&r=qrn8&triedRedirect=true>
UBS cut several hundred jobs in EMEA. They were mostly support staff but some bankers too. (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-05-29/ubs-sheds-hundreds-in-latest-job-cuts-after-credit-suisse-deal?utm_medium=email&utm_source=author_alert&utm_term=260529&utm_campaign=author_21003048>
Deutsche Bank fired some people who had relationships with Jeffrey Epstein. It didn't say who. (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-05-28/deutsche-bank-terminated-staff-over-epstein-ties-sewing-says?taid=6a18448d16ca9c00012da5b5&utm_campaign=trueanthem&utm_content=business&utm_medium=social&utm_source=twitter>
Nomura's executives are being paid 40% more on average. (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-05-28/nomura-top-executives-get-bigger-pay-rise-as-profit-hits-record?utm_medium=email&utm_source=author_alert&utm_term=260529&utm_campaign=author_20195502>
John Waldron at Goldman Sachs: "I would say we have a mark-to-market culture. So we mark our books kind of all the time. We like that because it gives you clarity on kind of where real market prices are with liquidity." (Seeking Alpha) <https://seekingalpha.com/article/4910141-the-goldman-sachs-group-inc-gs-presents-at-bernstein-42nd-annual-strategic-decisions?mailingid=45939251&messageid=2800&position=rta_analysis_fullsummary_main_3_textlink&serial=45939251.8279&source=email_2800&utm_campaign=rta-stock-article&utm_medium=email&utm_source=seeking_alpha&utm_term=45939251.8279>
If you earn more than £207k in the UK, you're in the top 1%. (The Times) <https://www.thetimes.com/money/tax/article/are-you-in-the-new-top-1-percent-of-earners-wk58bvzsr>
A lot of women in Manhattan are on the hunt for the same type of man: men in finance who wear tailored suits, take you to Michelin-starred dinners, fly you business class to luxurious destinations, and buy you endless Chanel. It sounds deluded, but these men actually exist. (InternetBimbo) <https://internetbimbo.substack.com/p/inside-a-high-end-manhattan-strip?utm_source=multiple-personal-recommendations-email&utm_medium=email&triedRedirect=true>
Follow me on X <https://x.com/MadameButcher>. Follow me on LinkedIn. <https://www.linkedin.com/in/sarahbutcherjournalist/>

item.title: The Coffee Run: An investment bank paying $900k is cutting jobs. It’s not about AI
item.description:
We've launched a new weekly video series <https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PLqJc_c5JT66B3Cp_26xsN8Xsv2EtvtEQQ> to accompany Morning Coffee, in which we look at one story in more depth to give you the full picture.
The most common story you see in financial services today is firms using AI as a justification to cut jobs. It's happening everywhere from investment banking to fintech. The most recent bank to make cuts, however, is doing it for an entirely different reason.
Perella Weinberg Partners is cutting 10% of staff including 12 people in it's most lucrative partner roles. Why? Find out by watching the video below

item.title: HSBC’s head of German research is leaving for a very niche competitor
item.description:
People talk a lot about exit opportunities when you work in a bank – boutique banks </news/boutique-investment-banks> are popular options. But what if you’re not working as a banker, but rather an equity researcher </news/research-job-investment-bank>? Well, you'd be in luck.
Click here to join the bubble by eFinancialCareers, our new anonymous community. <https://bubble.efinancialcareers.com/login?_gl=1*ut8n6u*_gcl_au*MTg4MDY2NTExNi4xNzYxNTU3NDIz*_ga*ODczNzI2NTA0LjE3NTM2OTk2ODI.*_ga_NZFQV4MXPM*czE3NjM2NDM0MDYkbzI1NiRnMSR0MTc2MzY0MzQzOSRqMjckbDAkaDMyMjc4OTUzMw..>✍️
Christoph Dolleschal will join Münchmeyer Petersen Capital Markets as its head of equity research at the start of July. He joins the firm from HSBC, where he spent four years as its head of German equities research. Dolleschal was also global head of equity & strategy research at Commerzbank before that.
Münchmeyer Petersen Capital Markets is a very new boutique bank formed from a merger between two very old German banking houses. The firm provides research, sales & trading </news/how-do-you-get-a-job-in-sales-and-trading-in-a-bank>, and corporate finance services to small and medium size companies in the Mittelstand, Germany’s economic backbone..
Münchmeyer Petersen has a team of approximately ten analysts at the moment. Despite the team’s small size and relative novelty, it has some significant names such as Sebastian Ubert, a former SocGen midcap researcher who also served as head of investor relations </news/2023/09/moving-from-equity-research-to-investor-relations> for vehicle manufacturer Kion.
HSBC declined a request for comment.

item.title: Female bankers want to get their eggs frozen. These are their options
item.description:
Balancing the demands of a high-powered finance career and a very natural desire to start a family is difficult, especially if you are a woman; pregnancy is a year-long process that can disrupt your career. And to make matters worse, it’s also time-constrained to your early adulthood. Luckily, there are women out there that have solutions for you.
Click here to join the bubble by eFinancialCareers, our new anonymous community. <https://bubble.efinancialcareers.com/login?_gl=1*ut8n6u*_gcl_au*MTg4MDY2NTExNi4xNzYxNTU3NDIz*_ga*ODczNzI2NTA0LjE3NTM2OTk2ODI.*_ga_NZFQV4MXPM*czE3NjM2NDM0MDYkbzI1NiRnMSR0MTc2MzY0MzQzOSRqMjckbDAkaDMyMjc4OTUzMw..>✍️
Flavia De Prezzo is the founder of Sopotion <https://www.linkedin.com/safety/go?url=https%3A%2F%2Fsopotion.thrivecart.com%2Fra%2F&trk=flagship-messaging-web&messageThreadUrn=urn%3Ali%3AmessagingThread%3A2-NWFkNDU1OTMtYTNhNS00ZmM5LTlmNzItZTk2MWVmYjNjOTdhXzEwMA%3D%3D>, a consultancy that specialises in supporting women going through the egg freezing process. De Prezzo works with women in high-powered industries such as banking and consulting around the world, and most of her clients are around 33 or 34 years old. “That’s the age when fertility will drop by 65%, and you feel like it is your last chance to do it,” De Prezzo said.
A report last year </news/senior-finance-discrimination?utm_source=chatgpt.com> from the Diversity Project noted that pregnant women faced significant discrimination from their apparently well-intentioned male colleagues. There was also a perceived conflict between a woman’s career and having children in their early 30s: “if a woman has not made [portfolio manager </news/finance/portfolio-manager-job-hedge-fund-lifestyle>], she likely never will and is viewed as a career analyst,” the report said. Pregnancy can very much get in the way of that ambition.
For De Prezzo, that career pressure is also tied to financial concerns. “Women want financial security,” she explained. “They finally feel like they are at a stage in life where they can afford a kid. They’re more mentally prepared as well, secure in their partnership or marriage. They feel like they’re ready to go to the next stage.”
Despite that, women often do not realise how egg freezing works, and rush into complicated medical procedures that can be quite traumatizing. “If you do it at 33 or 34,” De Prezzo explained, “it’s already a low success rate. You will probably need two cycles, which will be more expensive, and your body will react worse.” On the other hand, “the earlier you do it the better. It would be best to do it when you are 25, because your body reacts better – you would just need one cycle, and the number of eggs would be 90% higher than at 33.”
It can be difficult to keep up with life at the best of times, let alone for ambitious people. “The majority of women that are ambitious tend to be single,” De Prezzo mused. “They rarely feel like they can get a year or two of maternity leave. If we can preserve the fertility we have in our 20s, and conceive later on in life, we can better balance societal and career pressure.”
Most women keep their eggs frozen for five or six years, and the process after that is straightforward. “When they’re ready, if they’ve found a partner or want to be as single mother by choice, they will unfreeze the eggs and start the IVF cycle,” De Prezzo explained. The single mother option is a path already paved, with JPMorgan executive (and CEO succession candidate) Marianne Lake </news/marianne-lake> choosing to become a single mother at the age of 42, albeit with a surrogate. It’s not unheard of.
Options like that are not well-known, and neither is the cost of the egg freezing procedure. “There is a lot of misinformation about cost,” De Prezzo said. “People don’t know – if you’re under 34 [years old], don’t have serious medical issues, and your weight is in a certain band, you can actually freeze your eggs for free if you donate half of them. There are lots of options for women that don’t have a big budget, especially if they are younger.” That particular offer <https://www.londoneggbank.com/egg-freezing-and-sharing/> only applies to UK-based professionals.
The cost issue might not even be an issue at all for some professionals – most big banks offer packages to support family planning initiatives. At Morgan Stanley <https://www.morganstanley.com/people-opportunities/perks-benefits-working-at-morgan-stanley>, for example, the bank offers a $75k stipend for “family building” – to assist with the cost of adoption, surrogacy and fertility treatments including both IVF and egg- and sperm-freezing with up to 12 months of storage included.
Morgan Stanley isn’t the only bank offering such a service. Citi has a similar program <https://www.citibenefits.com/Work-or-Life/Growing-Your-Family> but only covering up to $24k. JPMorgan also offers <https://jpmcbenefits.aetna.com/application/files/3417/2738/2283/Family_building_JPMC.pdf#:~:text=Effective%20July%201%2C%202024%2C%20you,Your%20health> up to $35k for “eligible fertility medical expenses.” There are a lot of options for professional women to freeze their eggs, with most programs global.
Most women, however, are not aware of their options. And they do not have support, both physical and emotional, which De Prezzo can offer. “I believe it is important. We provide a nutritional workbook as well, to prepare your body before, during, and after the freezing,” she said.
De Prezzo is based in Malaga, and usually points women in the direction of Spain or Czechia for the best-budgeted egg freezing clinics. She said she even meets all the women who come to Malaga for a treatment. “I love that part of it. That is where our real connection starts. It’s very satisfying for me.”

item.title: Morning Coffee: Hedge fund superstar does due diligence on his new boss. People are getting protective of their clients
item.description:
It might be a cliché, but it’s worth repeating because it’s very true – finance is a people business. For every hedge fund, bank or brokerage that collapses because of operational failures or financial losses, there are a dozen that fall apart simply because relationships between key people broke down. So if you’re in the position of someone like Gustav Rydbeck <https://www.businessinsider.com/how-verition-scaled-long-short-equity-culture-multistrategy-hedge-fund-2026-5>, who was recruited from Balyasny last year to build a long-short equities business for Verition Capital, the most important thing to know is not the risk management framework or the AuM. It’s what kind of people you’re getting involved with.
Click here to join the bubble by eFinancialCareers, our new anonymous community. <https://bubble.efinancialcareers.com/login?_gl=1*ut8n6u*_gcl_au*MTg4MDY2NTExNi4xNzYxNTU3NDIz*_ga*ODczNzI2NTA0LjE3NTM2OTk2ODI.*_ga_NZFQV4MXPM*czE3NjM2NDM0MDYkbzI1NiRnMSR0MTc2MzY0MzQzOSRqMjckbDAkaDMyMjc4OTUzMw..>✍️
So, Rydbeck quietly and subtly reached out to friends in the industry who had worked for Nick Maounis and Josh Goldstein, Verition’s founders. He claims that nobody had a bad word to say about them, even people who had been made redundant.
This is, to say the least, rare in the world of multistrategy funds, where risk management policies are strict and opportunities to feel hard done by are plentiful. Verition claims to want to “build an environment where people can develop long-term careers”, and disdains the “pod shop” image, but so does every other pod shop. Unless Gustav Rydbeck’s friends are unusually lucky or tactful, they must have been walking the talk better than the rest of the sector.
And it seems that Rydbeck is trying to preserve this collegiate and people-centred atmosphere. It’s noticeable that in the list of high-profile hires he has made to Verition’s equities pod so far, traders and portfolio managers are less prominent that people with titles like “head of analyst development” and “head of long-short portfolio manager engagement”.
The trouble with this approach is that it costs money. When you’re trying to grow a business, there is always a temptation to prioritise hires who are going to contribute directly to either asset gathering or portfolio returns. Persuading investors that their pass-through expenses are well spent on people whose job is to provide the social and human infrastructure isn’t always easy. That’s one of the reasons for the well-known “valley of death” phenomenon which seems to have stricken hedge funds as diverse as Balyasny, Schonfeld and Brevan Howard, where you reach a certain level of AuM and headcount, and then see performance and outflows go into crisis as the business becomes too large to run itself.
Verition now has $14bn under management and 500 employees, so it is arguably through this stage, even if it’s not quite at the scale of Citadel, Millennium and the real multistrat giants. And according to its internal statistics, 90% of its profits over the last ten years have been generated by teams who are still there. In the hedge fund world, it seems that it’s nice to be profitable, but it’s profitable to be nice.
Elsewhere, a lawsuit in the UK <https://www.ft.com/content/c7f80db3-87f6-49e8-af4c-39998b873fee> has brought into focus one of the most controversial questions in the wealth management industry - who owns the client relationship?
When times are good, it’s something that everyone has a bit of a vested interest in keeping ambiguous. Firms obviously want to think that clients have a relationship with the company, rather than with any employee. On the other hand, most people find it quite weird to think of having a relationship with an LLC, PLC or Inc. And if you want client relationships, you have to recruit the advisors who bring them in, which means that you can’t afford to appear too grabby when it comes to people’s personal franchises.
In tougher times, though, companies would ideally like to cut costs, but not lose the revenue. And so questions of “whose account is it anyway?” start having to be given clear and definite answers. Which is a fertile ground for disagreement, discontent and litigation.
This is likely to become a more serious problem. Increasing use of AI is likely to increase the importance of personal relationships compared to other parts of the banking value chain. And in many cases, it’s arguable that sell-side bankers have at least as much of a personal claim on corporate clients as wealth managers do for high net worth individuals. It’s not impossible to see a world in which ownership of key relationships becomes an important negotiating point in hiring.
Meanwhile…
Christopher Willcox’s wholesale division at Nomura has ambitions. While the whole firm is increasing its earnings target by 50%, the wholesale division wants to get its RoE into double digits, boost equities revenues by 20-30% and global advisory by 50%, while expanding in EMEA, credit and commercial real estate. (Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-05-28/nomura-raises-annual-profit-target-50-after-record-year?utm_medium=email&utm_source=author_alert&utm_term=260528&utm_campaign=author_20195502>)
Hopefully none of the bankers on the SpaceX deal were naïve enough to think that they would get through the whole transaction without receiving an email titled something like “Elon’s recent post”. He’s said something controversial about an important revenue transaction during the quiet period, apparently. (FT <https://www.ft.com/content/7df0906c-6d9b-4440-b94e-65af0a785f7b>)
SocGen has hired Anna Repetto from Barclays to be the new head of European equities syndicate and corporate broking. (Financial News <https://www.fnlondon.com/articles/societe-generale-taps-barclays-repetto-to-lead-european-ecm-syndicate-94ce6640>)
JPMorgan apparently meant it when they said that they did not accept the accuracy of the FINRA arbitration panel in the “$642.50 deli plate” case brought by Brent Ryan Bodner. They are filing an appeal against the $4m decision which went against them, and providing more details of the case, which seems to involve a lot of people who are family members, clients, and/or employees at the same time. (WSJ <https://www.wsj.com/finance/banking/the-salami-incident-that-has-jpmorgan-chase-on-the-hook-for-4-25-million-bcfe73f5?mod=mhp>)
Apollo CEO Marc Rowan has lost a lawsuit which appears to, in part, relate to the septic tank for a lobster restaurant. (FT <https://www.ft.com/content/176ae252-a8e2-46ff-b419-ecb51c12cf8e>)
The husband of a London banker wants us all to feel sorry for how guilty he feels about his extramarital affair. (Telegraph <https://www.telegraph.co.uk/health-fitness/wellbeing/sex/assume-extramarital-affair-tragedy-life/?WT.mc_id=tmgoff_pso-twitter_core_pro_broad_carousel_content_father_of_three&rm_id=The+Telegraph_Conversion_Core%2FMixed_May+2026&twclid=21e3cl112nh0pk43mn4w4s4qhd>)
Just in time for investment bank internship season, New York City has relaxed zoning laws to make it easier to bars and restaurants to allow dancing. This could make for some entertaining Instagram-related content over the summer. (NY Post <https://nypost.com/2026/05/27/business/new-york-state-liquor-authority-poised-to-make-it-easier-for-bars-restaurants-to-allow-dancing-the-dance-police-is-over/>)

item.title: Citi made another senior JPMorgan hire, but not in its investment bank
item.description:
As we mentioned earlier this week </news/is-citi-preparing-for-another-hot-summer-of-jp-morgan-hires>, the flow of talent between JPMorgan and Citi </news/citi-hire-from-jpmorgan>may soon pick up in either direction, due in no small part to Citi's investment banking head Vis Raghavan </news/citi-paid-vis-raghavan-25m-to-hire-from-jp-morgan-jp-morgan-paid-doug-petno-23m-to-hire-from-citi> hoping to bring in trusted colleagues from his former employer. The latest person to make the switch, however, will be working in Andy Sieg </news/andy-sieg-women>'s wealth division.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Francis Mulvey has joined Citi Wealth's investment solutions team as its Americas head of fixed income. He reports to Emily Wasco, head of fixed income across Citi Wealth. He spent the last three years at JPMorgan as a hybrid capital trader and officially joined Citi on May 11th.
Prior to JPMorgan (his second stint at the bank), Mulvey got around. He was an executive director at broker-dealer StoneX, a portfolio manager at hedge fund Exoduspoint, and was previously head of US credit trading at SocGen.
Andy Sieg said at Citi's investor day earlier this month that, under his rule, the headcount at Citi Wealth has shrunk roughly 20%. It's looking to hire now though; Sieg said Citi Wealth is hiring ~500 specialist hires, including 100 private bankers. The focus is Asia which is the team's "fastest growing" and "most productive" area.
