item.title: Morning Coffee: 31-year-old ex-Morgan Stanley analyst earns $12k a day doing latest fashionable job. Why senior bankers are disinclined to retire
item.description:
If you're an analyst </news/banks-weird-hierarchies-analysts-associates-vps-mds>in a bank and you have a feeling there are more zeitgeisty jobs you could be doing instead, pay careful attention to 31-year-old Dave Wang. Dave graduated from Harvard University and spent two years working in M&A </news/get-a-job-in-ma> for Morgan Stanley in New York City. Then he went fashionably off-piste and has remained there ever since.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
First it was crypto. After leaving Morgan Stanley, where he says he was the lead analyst on the bank's $13bn acquisition of E-Trade, Wang became the Miami-based head of crypto investments for SoftBank's Latin American fund. Then he set up his own digital asset fund, which he tells Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/features/2026-05-25/the-ai-trainers-charging-25-000-a-day-to-push-wall-street-s-agentic-shift> he sold after generating fine returns. Now, it's AI.
Wang and his friend - a former associate in the fintech group at Goldman Sachs, with whom he also worked at SoftBank, have founded a company that teaches bankers and hedge fund </news/how-to-get-a-hedge-fund-job> employees how to use large language models. They are reportedly earning $25k a day between them.
Training people how to use AI is the skill of the moment. As we reported </news/citadel-securities-and-jane-street-need-ai-educators-and-they-are-not-alone>last month, Jane Street has been advertising a $300k job for a "machine learning educator" to run classes for its people. The role is an evolution of the prompt engineering jobs </news/prompt-engineering-jobs-in-finance-an-overhyped-niche>of yore: firms now need people to teach the art of the prompt, not just to write them.
Wang and his friend Felipe Sinisterra are prompt masters. They have prompts for making Claude assign numerical values to management statements to facilitate sentiment analysis. They have prompts for analysing videos of founders' pitches so that inadvertent pauses, winces, or arm folding can be ascribed hidden meaning.
Wang and Sinisterra aren't the only ex-bankers riding the AI train. Taron Arshakian from </news/the-ex-deutsche-bank-associate-offering-junior-bankers-an-ai-side-hustle> Deutsche Bank is developing AI benchmarks. Nicholas Lin </news/junior-bankers-ai-jobs>, also from Morgan Stanley, is automating junior banking jobs out of existence at Anthropic. In every cycle, there are alternatives and money to be made elsewhere. AI is it in 2026.
Separately, if your managing director </news/what-is-an-md-investment-bank> seems unwilling to retire, it might be because he or she is working hard to provide $337k each to members of his/her extended family.
The Financial Times <https://www.ft.com/content/a1025dca-de56-4715-b8a8-a3dc31e87d6c?syn-25a6b1a6=1> notes that it's becoming increasingly imperative to support not only children, but grandchildren. The wife of one London banker in his 50s says they're constantly receiving "wearing" texts saying their adult son needs something for his burgeoning family. Another (already retired) banker says he's given £250k each ($337k) to his grandchildren. “With the cost of property and few jobs that offer security for life, I can’t see how many children will get on unless they have the support of parents and grandparents,” he says. If you can't afford that, keep toiling.
Meanwhile...
Individual London bankers bought the British government's 2061 bond as a long term bet. It has not worked out well for them and some are selling £50k holdings to exit the trade. (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-05-22/london-traders-are-stuck-with-an-ultra-long-bond-bet-gone-bad>
Jobs that are immune to AI in financial services: Senior fund managers, financial advisers building long-term client relationships and risk managers exercising judgment. Jobs that are not immune to AI in financial services: Junior analysts doing data processing and model building. (The Times) <https://www.thetimes.com/life-style/parenting/article/ai-proof-careers-advice-ffdrfljdq?utm_source=twitter&utm_medium=paid-social&utm_campaign=FY26_times_twitter_5_uk_performance_conversions_bau-sales&utm_content=pro_single_video_editorial_parenting_may_daughter-wants-career-ai-image-2&twclid=21tz2mzj5ww9ikf0r212dkzh2j>
Elon Musk has been awarded 1.3bn shares with the ultimate vesting conditions. They could be worth $750bn, but only if he establishes a human colony on Mars with 1 million inhabitants. (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-05-22/musk-could-earn-760-billion-from-spacex-mars-shot-pay-packages?cmpid=eveus&utm_campaign=eveus&utm_medium=email&utm_source=newsletter&utm_term=260522&utm_content=4780>
Quantum computers could hack bitcoin. (FT) <https://www.ft.com/content/99c1c1e7-1a1c-479c-9fc8-e21aea5c3f0e?syn-25a6b1a6=1>
You can earn $260k working on a prediction markets desk at a trading firm or hedge fund. (Financial News) <https://www.fnlondon.com/articles/prediction-market-traders-eye-up-to-260-000-salaries-at-hedge-funds-65ad08ae>
How to further yourself as a banking intern. </news/internship-investment-bank> "Overcommunicate by saying "Will do”, “sounds good”, or “On it” constantly so we know the message has been recognized." Make sure you respond to emails promptly. (X) <https://x.com/HighyieldHarry/status/2058991029399810102?s=20>
Trump seems to be forcing green card applicants to apply in their home countries. "So everyone on a O1 or H1B visa would have to stop working legally in the US, go back to their country and wait for years of backlog?...This includes top scientists in our universities, founders of billion dollar companies." (Business Insider) <https://www.businessinsider.com/experts-react-donald-trump-new-immigration-policy-green-card-applications-2026-5?utm_source=Iterable&utm_medium=email&utm_campaign=%5BClone%5D%20Business%20Insider%20Today%20%E2%80%94%20May%2025%202026 >
JPMorgan has reserved the right to pursue its own claim against Chirayu Rana </news/chirayu-rana-jpmorgan>. (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-05-22/jpmorgan-denies-ex-vp-s-viral-harassment-claims-in-court-filing>
Laura Hajdini's </news/lorna-hajdini-jpmorgan> colleagues say she is a good person: “She came in person to some of our team-building events, even though she would need to stay late to complete her own work after.” (The Times) <https://www.thetimes.com/us/news-today/article/jpmorgan-sexual-abuse-chirayu-rana-lorna-hajdini-wh2gc930q>
Recruiters really like to find someone with high achievements but a low social class background because this is a signifier of high competence. (Wiley) < https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/pdf/10.1111/1468-4446.70125 >
Leigh-Ann Russell, chief information officer and global head of engineering at BNY created a "digital twin" of herself with AI. It recently told her: "You don't get your energy from sleep. You get your energy from creating space for yourself. Don't set your alarm late. Go for a run." This was great. (Business Insider) <https://www.businessinsider.com/bny-executive-uses-ai-digital-twin-as-coach-2026-5?utm_source=Iterable&utm_medium=email&utm_campaign=%5BClone%5D%20Business%20Insider%20Today%20%E2%80%94%20May%2025%202026 >
Intellectual giftedness is a neurodivergent condition. It comes with low socio-emotional regulation, heightened sensitivity, neuroticism and potential depression. (Science Direct) <https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0001691826007560>
A key characteristic of Western civilization is the odd combination of ennui and overwork. Who can now be sure what makes something valuable, bar an arbitrary designation of financial worth? The uncertainty transforms the practical advantages of money into an uncontained worship of money. Overwork is a result. (MarkVernon) <https://markvernon942268.substack.com/p/carl-jungs-penultimate-concerns?utm_source=substack&utm_medium=email&utm_campaign=email-restack-comment&action=restack-comment&r=qrn8&triedRedirect=true>

item.title: The Coffee Run: Inside the battle of the banks for SpaceX’s $1.5tn IPO
item.description:
We've launched a new weekly video series <https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PLqJc_c5JT66B3Cp_26xsN8Xsv2EtvtEQQ> to accompany Morning Coffee, in which we look at one story in more depth to give you the full picture.
The initial public offering of SpaceX, Elon Musk's aerospace and AI company, is set to be one of the biggest of all time. Banks are clamoring to get involved and 22 of them have... two are involved more than the rest.
Those two banks are Goldman Sachs and Morgan Stanley. The former, somewhat unexpectedly, received the top billing for SpaceX's IPO. The industry expected Morgan Stanley to lead, due to Elon Musk's relationship with banking chairman Michael Grimes.
How did Goldman pull it off.? And is there more than meets the eye? Find out by watching the video below.

item.title: Pay for private equity secondaries professionals was mighty after a strong 2025
item.description:
If you grew up wanting to work in private equity </news/how-to-get-a-job-in-private-equity> (for some reason), you probably didn’t grow up wanting to work in secondaries </news/private-capital-advisory-jobs-investment-banking>, of all things. But if you’ve found yourself in that situation, you’re probably making a pretty penny out of it.
Click here to join the bubble by eFinancialCareers, our new anonymous community. <https://bubble.efinancialcareers.com/login?_gl=1*ut8n6u*_gcl_au*MTg4MDY2NTExNi4xNzYxNTU3NDIz*_ga*ODczNzI2NTA0LjE3NTM2OTk2ODI.*_ga_NZFQV4MXPM*czE3NjM2NDM0MDYkbzI1NiRnMSR0MTc2MzY0MzQzOSRqMjckbDAkaDMyMjc4OTUzMw..>✍️
Data from recruitment consultancy Jensen Partners shows that the average professional working in secondaries earned $793k for their work in 2025 in normal (salary and bonus combined) compensation, compared to the average alternative asset management professional who earned $800k.
Pay was better at lower ranks than higher ones. Secondaries VPs earned $510k in median compensation in 2025, compared to $410k for VPs in private credit and $450k for alternative asset management VPs in general.
Pay was not quite so generous higher up the ladder. Secondaries department heads earned “just” $1.08m on average in 2025, compared to $1.2m for partners in alternative asset management in general, and $1.43m for partners in private credit specifically.
One place where secondaries pay was a bit more on par with the market was in its carried interest </news/what-is-carried-interest>. This is a type of compensation granting professionals working on a deal a slice of that deal’s profits. Secondaries professionals, on average, received $875k in carried interest on average for their work in 2025, compared to the private credit average of $600k.
Things might change in 2026. In an April report, JPMorgan noted <https://www.jpmorgan.com/insights/markets-and-economy/markets/private-market-secondaries> that the secondaries market hit $224bn in 2025, which was a record year, and was up 41% on 2024. The bank called it a “booming” market.
As such, secondaries professionals are in high demand these days. A slew of moves have happened since the start of the year, including JPMorgan’s hiring of Will Boyle, as global head of secondary advisory from Morgan Stanley, as Bloomberg reported <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-05-04/jpmorgan-hires-morgan-stanley-s-boyle-to-lead-pe-secondaries> earlier this month. One of the biggest teams in the space is boutique bank Evercore's, which is headed up by ex-UBS banker Nigel Dawn. Bloomberg noted <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/features/2025-10-27/private-equity-market-revitalized-by-200-billion-secondaries-boom> in October last year that Dawn's 150-person team generated about 13% of Evercore's advisory revenue, and they were paid a collective $150m.
Johnson Associates, the compensation consultancy, estimated after Q1 that secondaries bonuses would be up by 5 to 10% at the end of the year, relative to 2025.

item.title: UBS is unleashing people with the most desirable skills into the market. Or not
item.description:
An era is coming to an end at UBS. When the Swiss bank announced its first quarter results <https://www.ubs.com/content/dam/assets/cc/investor-relations/quarterlies/2026/1q26/full-report-ubs-group-consolidated-1q26.pdf>in April, it revealed that the assets remaining in its non-core division had been reduced to $20.bn. That sounds like a lot, but in 2023 the unit had $84bn of assets </news/the-credit-suisse-traders-who-are-cruising-until-2027> to get rid of. Much has been achieved.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Accordingly, the people who've been selling off the assets in the non-core unit are moving on. Dan Chantler, a managing director (MD) who was running the linear trading team, announced last week that he's leaving UBS after nearly 25 years. Chantler said he was proud of managing a global trading team that had been "instrumental in the greatest banking integration of our time." Around $77bn of the initial assets in the non-core unit were the result of UBS's forced <https://www.ft.com/content/3080d368-d5aa-4125-a210-714e37087017?syn-25a6b1a6=1>merger with Credit Suisse in June 2023. Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2024-01-18/ubs-fails-to-find-single-buyer-for-credit-suisse-distressed-debt-portfolio> reported that they initially included 30 trading positions, including Credit Suisse's illiquid distressed debt investments.
Chantler isn't the first person to leave the non-core unit. David Leung, head of the unit in Singapore left last October and is now at Huatai as head of fixed income trading for the region. Louise Kitchen </news/2021/06/louise-kitchen-deutsche-bank>, who joined UBS in 2022 as head of the non-core unit and who formerly ran Deutsche Bank's similar operation, left last October.
UBS declined to comment for this article, but the departures from its non core team appear intentional. The wind down is expected to be complete by the end of this year. At that point, people will in the unit have nothing to do.
People who don't leave UBS are finding other roles internally. Former Credit Suisse trader, Shane O’Cuinn </news/2022/01/christian-meissner-credit-suisse> was running the non-core unit for a while, but is now busy running UBS's leaky </news/nomura-rates-traders-ubs> macro team instead.
Some UBS insiders say working in the non-core unit has been akin to a finishing school and is the perfect preparation of a career clearing up the mess created by crisis situations. "Call it professional dismantling," says one. " The people who do it well are, quietly, among the most sought-after operators in wholesale banking."
In this version of events, running down a non-core book makes you the person every bank needs to hire. "Understanding every position in a legacy book, every counterparty relationship, every funding structure and hedge chain, does not make you a specialist in endings. It makes you a specialist in complexity," says one insider. It makes you someone, "who has seen the full architecture of a mature franchise, knows what works and what fails, and can construct something durable without repeating the mistakes embedded in the book they just unwound."
Not everyone is so sure, though. A senior macro trader who left UBS suggests the non-core team didn't do much. "Those guys clipped coupons," he says. "They were given a budget of money they were allowed to lose while they got rid of risk to other banks. It was a cost centre and not a profit centre and people there were always looking to get out."
However, others in the unit agree that time in the non-core unit endowed its people with some special powers. Chantler didn't respond to multiple requests to comment for this article but a former member of his team said the UBS non-core unit met its KPIs a year ahead of the deadline set by by the bank. "All budgets were based on the economic value of each transaction and we only would execute based on metrics that made the exit of the position better long term for the firm," he said. "We were a highly experienced team that had to balance effective risk management while ensuring portfolio optimisation at all times."
Working in non-core gives you "quite a skillset" says this fan. "Often we would be managing large gamma risk moves while also structuring economic and capital efficient packages to sell."
Writing on LinkedIn, Chantler said he has "plenty of ambition and drive left." Banks that need the special skillset might want to get in touch.

item.title: Singapore macro people are having a moment: "50% upticks to change jobs"
item.description:
If you work in Singapore and you also work on a macro (rates or FX desk), this is a fine time. There are people who would like to hire you.
One junior macro trader in Singapore informs us that his phone has been ringing with headhunters. How many headhunters? "Quite a number," he says. "Poaching is going full throttle."
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Singapore's macro hiring market has already been active this year. The activity has focused on Nomura </news/nomura-s-singapore-macro-hiring>, which has recently hired Yagnish Rathi from Verition and Ryan Fong from ANZ to accompany senior ANZ traders Anshul Sidher and Herr Ling Yeo, who've arrived after a period of gardening leave. There have been exits too: Jeanna Lee </news/jeanna-lee> went from Nomura to BlueCrest.
Beyond Nomura, Jain Global hired Barclays' head of APAC rates trading Jiujiu Xiong in February. Balyasny made four macro hires, including Ron Choi </news/balyasny-pay>last year. Dymon Asia hired Zhibin Zeng from Millennium.
One Singapore based headhunter says macro hiring has now "gone bonkers" and that bids are north of 40%-50%, with some VPs getting buybacks 70% higher than their current compensation. Teams are very lean, he says. "A single exit cuts deep and the buyside is draining the sellside talent pool."
More movement is expected. Nomura traders are waiting for bonuses to be paid next week. When they are, some are likely to move on. One, Masaki Hamada, already resigned before bonuses were announced in April and has yet to turn up elsewhere. There are suggestions of disgruntlement among some remaining Nomura traders amidst suggestions that the recent bonus round </news/nomura-bonuses> favoured recent hires who came on guarantees and made less money available for others.
Nomura declined to comment on Hamada's exit. He didn't respond to a request to comment.

item.title: Morning Coffee: The billionaire hedge fund manager whose wife disagreed with self-enrichment. The redemption of Morgan Stanley's tech team
item.description:
We have written about Chris Hohn </news/chris-hohn>before. When we wrote about him last year, he was saying, "The most important thing is consciousness and love," and, "Whether you go the easy way or the hard way through suffering, you eventually come to learn that the spiritual world is not just real, but it's the whole thing."
Six months later, 59-year-old Hohn has been saying similar things to the Financial Times <https://www.ft.com/content/521d1861-82c5-4194-9358-f3dd53d122b5?syn-25a6b1a6=1>. The Financial Times suggests that Hohn might be Britain's Warren Buffett. Hohn is not only the UK's most successful hedge fund manager, but he runs the fifth most profitable hedge fund </news/how-to-get-a-hedge-fund-job>of all time.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Hohn manages $77bn of assets under management using only himself and six or so 'elite analysts.' He holds 15 positions in companies with strong competitive advantages. He likes hard assets and infrastructure. Specifically: “Railroads, toll roads, airports, cell phone towers.”
Hohn is very rich and is worth nearly $14bn. In the words of a former employee speaking to the FT, he can be "completely insatiable", single-minded, and "bruising." He doesn't mind offending people and he is direct. This is par for the course among hedge fund moguls, but as he approaches his seventh decade, Hohn is also increasingly a proselytiser for the non-material world.
“Close your eyes, and in the darkness you can know God and God is consciousness,” he tells the FT. Consider the difference between “someone who is alive and someone who is dead”, he instructs the journalist. Then he hangs up the phone.
Hohn's women seem to have influenced him. His first wife disagreed with self-enrichment and was reportedly 'put off' by his aspirations to make a fortune. She was won over when Hohn said he wanted to use his fortune to alleviate suffering, and received £337m when their marriage ended 12 years ago. Hohn's second wife asked him to read up on consciousness and has fuelled Hohn's spirituality. Hohn is certain he knew her in another existence.
Hohn's relationships have also brought him pain. He tells the FT that his divorce was a 'dark night of the soul,' and reflects that: “Evolution occurs out of suffering.”
Hohn has donated £1.4bn ($1.9bn) to charitable causes. He said last year that he's just a custodian of his wealth: "It's money that isn't mine. It's to be given away in service to humanity." When he invests, Hohn says it's on the basis of both analysis and intuition, and that intuition is "the intelligence of the soul." This year, his Children's Investment Fund was down 4% through to the end of April, but Hohn points to times past when it fell 50% before tripling in value. If this happens, he will give more money away. Hon says his intuition tells him: "my purpose here [is] to help people." It helps that his wives say this too.
Separately, when we observed </news/space-x-ipo-goldman-sachs-morgan-stanley> earlier this week that Michael Grimes at Morgan Stanley seemed to have been overlooked in the SpaceX IPO and that Kim Posnett at Goldman Sachs seemed to be the star attraction, we were not in possession of all the facts.
Firstly, it seems that Goldman's lead position on the IPO wasn't just Posnett's doing. Secondly, it seems that Morgan Stanley's role might be bigger than it seems.
Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-05-20/goldman-ceo-slides-into-musk-s-dms-during-bid-to-lead-spacex-ipo>reported on Wednesday that Goldman Sachs </news/kathy-ruemmler-goldman-sachs> CEO David Solomon helped persuade Musk to choose Goldman by 'sliding' into his DMs on X. It is not clear what Solomon said there. If Musk had paid proper attention he might have noticed that Solomon doesn't really use his X account <https://x.com/DavidSolomon/with_replies> in normal times and has never posted anything. He does, however, follow Musk.
Nor was Goldman's success just Solomon's doing. The Financial Times <https://www.ft.com/content/10cecc4e-dbce-477d-9f10-068581c6e650?syn-25a6b1a6=1> reports that David Ludwig, Dan Dees </news/dan-dees-goldman-sachs> and Susie Scher were also instrumental in Goldman's glory. Several of the Goldman team were reportedly seen in SpaceX's offices on a daily basis during the run up to the IPO announcement.
Grimes, however, was reportedly not seen in SpaceX's offices. Maybe Grimes was busy elsewhere. Although Goldman achieved the very prestigious "lead left" role on the SpaceX IPO, the FT notes that Morgan Stanley has the “stabilisation” mandate, which means it will support SpaceX’s shares during volatility immediately after the IPO. This could be more lucrative, someone tells the FT. Someone else tells the FT that Goldman was only listed before Morgan Stanley because it comes first in the alphabet. No hard feelings.
Meanwhile...
OpenAI has been working with Goldman Sachs and Morgan Stanley on an IPO worth over $1 trillion. (FT) <https://www.ft.com/content/028a169f-cd1c-438b-b50d-df4af6297318?syn-25a6b1a6=1>
Wally Cheng, head of global technology M&A at Morgan Stanley is feeling excited. He says “I think the deal activity is going to span the full spectrum — private and public.” (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-05-20/morgan-stanley-dealmaker-sees-full-spectrum-of-m-a-ahead-in-ai>
Another Citi banker left. Ed Wehle joined in 2023 and has left for Jefferies, where he will be as global head of technology services investment banking. (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-05-21/jefferies-hires-citi-s-ed-wehle-for-global-tech-services-banking>
Apollo and KKR have been trading in and out of private credit loans. (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-05-21/private-credit-s-unthinkable-becomes-reality-as-trading-revs-up?srnd=phx-finance>
Quant hedge fund QRT's flagship fund manages around $10bn of AUM and has generated annualized returns of around 30% since its inception in 2018. Headcount there has grown from 600 in early 2023 to 1,100 by mid 2024, 1,400 by March 2025 and 2,000 now. (RupakGhose) <https://rupakghose.substack.com/p/the-rise-of-qrt>
Brevan Howard was up 3.7% through to May 15th. This was partly thanks to its star trader Fash Golchin and to its strategist Abhijit Chakrabortti who have been placing increasingly large bets on equity markets. (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-05-21/stocks-help-brevan-macro-hedge-fund-offset-rates-trading-losses?srnd=phx-finance>
Investec is expanding into the UK's private banking market. Maybe it will need some private bankers. (FT) <https://www.ft.com/content/f8706bd9-3d31-4147-b374-7794ba15c55b?syn-25a6b1a6=1>
"AI isn’t coming for builders or sellers, but it is coming for measurers. Tireless, independent, efficient and available." It is also coming for middle managers and for marketing (which is full of measurers). (WSJ) <https://www.wsj.com/opinion/how-i-choose-which-cloudflare-employees-to-replace-with-ai-40a197e5?mod=hp_opin_pos_1>
AI is replacing executive assistants and recruiters. (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/features/2026-05-21/mckinsey-pwc-and-ey-lay-off-executive-assistants-as-ai-accelerates?srnd=phx-finance>
Reid Hoffman has got digital twin, Reid AI, which he uses for public appearances and media interviews. The system—trained on 22 years of Hoffman’s books, speeches, podcasts and articles—has delivered more than 75 addresses and presentations since its 2024 launch. (A human helped prepare them, though.) (WSJ) <https://www.wsj.com/tech/ai/ai-agents-work-executives-a38400e1?mod=hp_featst_pos5>
How to behave when you buy your child a house. Set a spending limit of $700,000 and insist that they live no more than two miles away. (WSJ) <https://www.wsj.com/personal-finance/these-parents-are-buying-homes-for-their-kidswith-strings-attached-aa3da6d2?mod=e2tw>
Photo: By Foreign, Commonwealth & Development Office <https://www.flickr.com/photos/10246637@N04/53343461377/, CC BY 2.0, https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=172634226>

item.title: Hegde fund Balyasny’s latest Hong Kong PM is famously nice
item.description:
Working at a hedge fund </news/how-to-get-a-hedge-fund-job> can be lucrative. If you’re a portfolio manager </news/2023/09/portfolio-manager-job-hedge-fund>, it can be lucrative due to your performance. If you’re working for a portfolio manager (PM), it can be lucrative depending on your PM. And some have better reputations than others.
Click here to join the bubble by eFinancialCareers, our new anonymous community. <https://bubble.efinancialcareers.com/login?_gl=1*ut8n6u*_gcl_au*MTg4MDY2NTExNi4xNzYxNTU3NDIz*_ga*ODczNzI2NTA0LjE3NTM2OTk2ODI.*_ga_NZFQV4MXPM*czE3NjM2NDM0MDYkbzI1NiRnMSR0MTc2MzY0MzQzOSRqMjckbDAkaDMyMjc4OTUzMw..>✍️
Alex Chau joined Balyasny last month as a PM in Hong Kong, covering Asia-Pacific and European financials. He joined the firm from Millennium </news/how-to-get-a-job-at-hedge-fund-millennium>, where he ran a market neutral Asian financials pod as a senior portfolio manager.
Chau’s new analysts at Balyasny will probably be quite happy to see him come. One of his former analysts, Nishant Shah, retired last year </news/hong-kong-hedge-fund-analyst-declares-retirement-aged-35-after-practicing-fire> at the ago of 35 and credited “lots of luck and generous bosses” to his ability to do so. In his retirement announcement, Shah called Chau “one of the sharpest minds, fairest supervisors and humblest personalities I’ve ever had the pleasure of knowing.” High praise indeed.
He is currently working as a CIO for Indian investment firm J Curve Capital, but he did note that he planned to work “some philanthropic passion projects & entrepreneurial journeys” after retiring, so it doesn’t seem to be out of necessity.

item.title: The ex-Deutsche Bank associate offering junior bankers AI side hustles
item.description:
It's not so long ago that Anthropic was reportedly <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2025-10-21/openai-looks-to-replace-the-drudgery-of-junior-bankers-workload> hiring junior bankers </news/junior-bankers-ai-jobs>and paying them $150 an hour to write prompts. Seven months later, Anthropic has launched an array agents <https://www.anthropic.com/news/finance-agents>for financial services. The agents can build pitch decks and models, review earnings and conduct market research. Now Jamie Dimon is suggesting he might need fewer bankers </news/lower-value-human-capital> in the future.
Sitting in a junior banking job in the circumstances might feel like being a frog in hot water. If you are experiencing the angst, a former Deutsche Bank associate </news/banks-weird-hierarchies-analysts-associates-vps-mds>has a proposal that might allay your anxiety.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Taron Arshakian spent nearly three years at Deutsche Bank in New York. He worked in the German bank's investment banking </news/what-is-an-investment-bank> coverage team "at the intersection between capital markets </news/capital-markets-jobs-ecm-and-dcm> and M&A </news/get-a-job-in-ma>." Then he left. Arshakian's FINRA registration shows him quitting in April 2026. "The work never ends, you cannot just relax," he says of his time in banking. "I felt like I didn't want to do that any more."
Arshakian is already doing something different. In February, he founded Proxion AI. Among the things Proxion offers are AI side hustles to people still working in banking.
"I work with AI labs to refine their models," says Arshakian. "Something like Claude can build you the full model from beginning to end. That's good enough if you're a senior in a university, but for models to be used in a professional context they still need refining with human judgement."
Arshakian sells AI labs this human judgement. One month after leaving Deutsche Bank, he says he's signed up 200+ junior bankers to each work for around 15 hours a week refining AI models. They do this alongside their banking jobs and they earn $75-$300 an hour. Arshakian himself is possibly earning even more - he says AI labs will pay $1k-3k each for model validator referrals.
It doesn't sound like the sort of thing that banks would approve of, but Arshakian insists it's fine. "It depends upon regulations and compliance, but it's a contractor job," he says. "You just tell compliance 'I have a corporation' and you open an LLC. You don't have to explicitly say what it does. I pay you via the LLC."
He says there's huge demand. "All my guys work in the industry and have full time jobs. They take companies and real financials and build real models. They're writing the rubrics for the AI models of the future."
Given that AI companies will pay thousands for the right people to train their models, Arshakian seems to be onto something. But he has bigger aspirations than acting as an employment agency arranging hustles for former colleagues. His intention is to develop a set of AI benchmarks that will set standards for whether models are any good. "You can have models for everything from M&A transactions to credit underwriting, IPOs, debt issuance and equity issuance and they will all need benchmarks," he says. "In the future, the benchmarks will be like the ratings agencies."
Arshakian has built four benchmarks already. He has no regrets about leaving Deutsche Bank. "It's difficult to leave. You think you have a secure full time job, but that's in your imagination - they could fire you next month."
He points out that he's not asking people to leave banking anyway. "I'm saying, 'Keep your job and take a part time job with me.'" It will be good for your resume, he adds. "You will know how AI works and how teaching the models works. That will make you more employable."

item.title: JPMorgan is hiring quantum computing PhDs on $260k salaries again. It's less interested in VR
item.description:
JPMorgan is widely recognized as the industry leader for quantum computing research </news/quantum-computing-jobs-banking> in financial services, but its quantum division faced a significant roadblock this time last year. Marco Pistoia and Charles Lim </news/jp-morgan-s-quantum-computing-team-lost-its-leaders-and-there-is-a-mystery>, the bank's two most senior quantum specialists, left in confusing and seemingly unanticipated circumstances. Life has gone on without them, however, as JPMorgan has begun hiring quantum computing researchers again.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
In the past month, JPMorgan has posted job listings for an associate, senior associate and VP for quantum computing in New York. The senior associate role, which pays a salary of up to $190k, is focused specifically on quantum error correction, a process that makes data from quantum computing algorithms more reliable and accurate. The VP role, which pays a salary of up to $260k, doesn't have a set area of focus, but you'll be expected to lead research projects on "algorithms, error correction, compilation, simulation, or financial applications." Applicants are expected to have a relevant PhD and a strong academic publication record. Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/features/2026-04-26/wall-street-s-quantum-computing-divide-goldman-retreats-jpmorgan-invests>reported last month that JPMorgan has more than 50 people </news/quantum-computing-jobs-banking-jpmorgan> in the team already.
The quantum roles sit within the bank's Global Technology Applied Research (GTAR) division. It's led by Rob Otter, who most recently ran quantum computing at State Street, and previously worked in JPMorgan's blockchain arm Kinexys (then known as Onyx). He was appointed to the role, formerly held by Pistoia, last July. Ruslan Shaydulin, an executive director in the quantum team, took Pistoia's secondary title: head of quantum computing.
GTAR has published multiple quantum research papers since Otter joined. In November, it published a report on its most significant breakthrough, certified randomness <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2511.03686>, in partnership with startup Quantinuum. This was first announced last March, <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2025-03-26/jpmorgan-says-quantum-experiment-generated-truly-random-numbers> before news of Pistoia's departure; he has not been credited as an author while Otter has. Both men were credited on a paper published this February, focused on using quantum algorithms to price derivatives. <https://arxiv.org/pdf/2602.03725>
Not all banks are sold on quantum. Bloomberg reported that Goldman Sachs' quantum team, </news/it-s-not-just-jp-morgan-goldman-sachs-and-others-lost-quantum-talent-too>once an industry leader, has "evaporated" amid cost cuts as a meaningful breakthrough was seen as too far away. Indeed, Otter suggested to Bloomberg that useful quantum algorithms are still years away.
GTAR also includes other frontier technologies like immersive technology (AR/VR). That division also lost its leader, Blair McIntyre, </news/jp-morgan-hired-an-ai-scientist-from-meta-s-reality-labs-after-its-vr-head-quietly-left> who returned to academia with Northeastern University. However, it's understood that the bank is not looking to find a replacement for this role and is focusing its research on other areas. The bank previously used the technology to provide immersive training simulations </news/2023/12/jpmorgan-virtual-reality>; this might have been useful in the COVID era but as banks return to the office, times seem to have changed.

item.title: Jamie Dimon says "old jobs;" Bill Winters says "lower value human capital." How rude
item.description:
What kind of job do you do in an investment bank? If it is an "old job," bad luck. If you are also a person of "lower value human capital," tough times. Both phrases sound a bit rude, but only Bill Winters at Standard Chartered </news/standard-chartered-job-cuts-ai> has been beasted for saying the latter. Winters has since apologised. <https://www.wsj.com/finance/banking/ceo-walks-back-comment-about-replacing-lower-value-human-capital-with-ai-15bdfc5c>
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Jamie Dimon at JPMorgan isn't apologising for talking about "old jobs." Precisely the opposite. Talking to Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-05-21/dimon-says-jpmorgan-will-hire-more-ai-people-fewer-bankers>, Dimon said Winters had been "inartful" in referring to "lower value human capital." It will be "old jobs" that disappear, added Dimon. He didn't say which these are though.
Some are already known, though. If you work in a "monitoring" job at Standard Chartered, you may have already discovered that your job is old and that you are low value: 88% of monitoring jobs </news/standard-chartered-job-cuts-ai> at the bank have been replaced by AI.
Other jobs that might fall into the category of "old" and occupied by "low value" persons are outlined (albeit not in these terms) in Goldman Sachs' recent shareholder letter. <https://www.goldmansachs.com/investor-relations/financials/current/annual-reports/2025/letter-to-shareholders.pdf>The bank firm is working on "six workstreams" that are susceptible to AI automation. These are: client onboarding and KYC; vendor management; regulatory reporting; lending; enterprise risk management; and sales enablement. If these are your habitats, bad luck.
Needless to say, all banks are busily exploring the kinds of 'operational efficiencies' that might eliminate tens of thousands of jobs and enable them to hit their costs of capital and reduce their cost income ratios. Many of the roles susceptible to these investigations are situated in what Morgan Stanley CEO Ted Pick calls "core infrastructure." - "Classic operational flow" and "surveilling" are subsets that lend themselves to "efficiency," says Pick. Surveilling and monitoring sound like the same thing.
It's not just surveilling and onboarding etc. though. Pick says Morgan Stanley also has "a client agent inside of the electronic trading platform" that can answer technical questions. Jamie Dimon seems to be suggesting today he might hire fewer bankers as AI advances. AI has also been threatening junior jobs in private equity firms since at least 2021: </news/2021/09/artificial-intelligence-private-equity>why employ a young ex-banker to filter deals when an impartial LLM can do it for you.
In the circumstances, many jobs look old. Many people look "low value." Everyone's a marginally additive anachronism. Relationships are the new gold. And knowing how to plumb </jobs-United_Kingdom-Durham-Maintenance_Plumber.id24238048> a sink.
Follow me on X <https://x.com/MadameButcher>. Follow me on LinkedIn. <https://www.linkedin.com/in/sarahbutcherjournalist/>

item.title: What is an investment bank? What does it do?
item.description:
It’s difficult to pin down what an investment bank does. For some people, it’s just offering M&A </news/what-are-ma-jobs> advice. For others, it’s also involvement in capital markets </news/capital-markets-jobs-ecm-and-dcm> activities. Some people include sales & trading </news/how-do-you-get-a-job-in-sales-and-trading-in-a-bank>. And most member of the public imagine rich old (white) men sitting in a room and planning the world economy. Reality, as ever, is more complicated.
Click here to join the bubble by eFinancialCareers, our new anonymous community. <https://bubble.efinancialcareers.com/login?_gl=1*ut8n6u*_gcl_au*MTg4MDY2NTExNi4xNzYxNTU3NDIz*_ga*ODczNzI2NTA0LjE3NTM2OTk2ODI.*_ga_NZFQV4MXPM*czE3NjM2NDM0MDYkbzI1NiRnMSR0MTc2MzY0MzQzOSRqMjckbDAkaDMyMjc4OTUzMw..>✍️
Take Goldman Sachs </news/how-to-get-a-job-goldman-sachs>, for example. For many people, Goldman is the investment bank par excellence. But Goldman Sachs doesn’t just investment bank </jobs/investment-banking> – it also asset manages </jobs/asset-management>, wealth manages </jobs/wealth-management>, and operates a small retail bank </jobs/retail-banking?> named after its founder, Marcus Goldman.
Asset and wealth management, as well as retail banking, are definitely not investment banking. But pretty much every major investment bank does do them. So what exactly is an investment bank exactly? What does it do?
What does an investment bank do at its core?
Investment banking entails two fundamental things.
Firstly, banks help their clients to raise funds by issuing securities such as equities or bonds. These clients can include both companies and governments.
Secondly, they help their corporate clients buy or sell parts of their businesses, or maybe merge with other corporations. The former is known as capital markets </jobs/capital-markets> or underwriting </jobs/underwriting>, while the latter is known as Mergers and Acquisitions </jobs/M&A>, or M&A for short. Capital markets involve Equity Capital Markets </jobs/equity-capital-markets> (known as ECM) and Debt Capital Markets </jobs/debt-capital-markets> (DCM) and relate to issuing stocks and bonds, respectively.
Similar to most DCM issuances is loan syndication. This is a lending style that involves multiple banks, usually for a higher-risk loan product that requires a higher risk profile (such as funding private equity </news/how-to-get-a-job-in-private-equity> activity). The syndication of the loan spreads risk across multiple lenders.
The core of all of these activities is valuation. An investment bank values bonds and stocks so they can be sold for fair values, and an investment bank values corporations to ensure that when a merger happens, a fair value is paid – or extracted.
What does sales & trading involve?
The other main activity that as an investment bank does is sales & trading </news/how-do-you-get-a-job-in-sales-and-trading-in-a-bank>. This is part of an investment bank, but it is not investment banking. Sales and trading is also known as the markets business of a bank.
When a bank helps a client raise money by issuing bonds or shares through its capital markets team, it guarantees the prices of these products by selling them to the market. This requires salespeople and it requires traders. Salespeople and traders sell and trade the financial products the bank has helped create, as well as those which have been created previously. They also sell and trade derivative products based on the underlying securities, as well as syndicated loans.
In an M&A deal, the markets team might construct derivatives that help reduce the client's exposure to changing foreign exchange (FX) rates. In debt capital markets deals, where new tradable debt is being issued, the markets team might help organize a group of investors (known as a syndicate) to assist in underwriting the debt being issued in the event that it's not all sold. This spreads the risk of the deal.
Banks also trade securities that they didn't create. This is called the secondaries market - basically, second-hand securities. Securities can be fixed income, currencies, and commodities (FICC) products or equities products, or derivatives of either. Potential revenue is huge - according to market intelligence provider Tricumen, FICC trading revenue alone was $106bn globally in 2025, and equities trading revenue was another $77bn, dwarfing investment banking fee pools, which only added up to around $68bn that same year.
Much of that trading is electronic, with banks acting as market makers. When a client (such as a hedge fund </news/how-to-get-a-hedge-fund-job>) comes to the bank to set up a position, the bank finds a buyer (in a liquid enough market) and sells it. Banks take a small percentage of the price, known as a haircut, and that is their profit. Vast amounts of capital is required to make markets, with banks leaning on their huge balance sheets to make their market making possible.
& </news/research-job-investment-bank>' </news/finance/how-to-get-a-job-at-jane-street> </news/credit-trading> </news/credit-trading>"" <https://www.ft.com/content/4f20fbb9-a10f-4a08-9a13-efa1b55dd38a?syn-25a6b1a6=1>"" <https://www.institutionalinvestor.com/article/2eaad054qel0icuwrgcu8/corner-office/goldman-expands-private-credit-ambitions-with-major-overhaul>""&& </news/standard-chartered-job-cuts-ai> </news/private-credit>'' <https://www.citigroup.com/global/news/press-release/2024/citi-and-apollo-announce-25-billion-private-credit-direct-lending-program> <https://www.businesswire.com/news/home/20260518530387/en/Citi-and-HPS-Investment-Partners-a-part-of-BlackRock-Announce-%E2%82%AC15-Billion-Private-Capital-Program-to-Expand-Direct-Lending-in-EMEA> <https://www.aima.org/article/press-release-strong-growth-sees-private-credit-market-reach-us-3-5-trillion.html> </news/2023/01/boutique-investment-banks>& </news/how-to-get-a-job-in-asset-management> </news/centerview-pay-in-london-up-20-for-partners-down-40-for-the-rest> </news/working-hours-banks> <https://www.jpmorganchase.com/ir/investor-day>'''''& </news/what-are-ma-jobs>'' </news/capital-markets-jobs-ecm-and-dcm>'&''''&&&''" </news/goldman-sachs-capital-solutions-group>" </news/how-to-get-a-job-in-private-equity> </news/private-credit>''& </about/editor-tips>

item.title: Ex-traders and quants at Elon Musk's XAI are on $300k+ salaries… they're about to be much richer
item.description:
SpaceXAI, formerly known as XAI, owner of X, formerly known as Twitter, is an interesting place to work. It recently merged with SpaceX, and has forged a shocking alliance <https://techcrunch.com/2026/05/20/anthropic-will-pay-xai-1-25-billion-per-month-for-compute/> with rival AI lab </news/the-difference-between-working-as-a-quant-in-finance-and-as-a-researcher-in-an-ai-lab>Anthropic. Its researchers and members of technical staff (MTCs) could become very rich when SpaceX has its initial public offering in June </news/space-x-ipo-goldman-sachs-morgan-stanley>; some of them have come from financial services.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Elon Musk </news/elon-musk-is-hiring-hft-quants-and-fintech-engineers-at-ai-his-new-ai-startup> tweeted earlier today <https://x.com/elonmusk/status/2057327547411570907?s=20>that SpaceXAI wants "world-class engineers/physicists" and is considering people with "zero prior experience in AI." Last month, we reported that XAI hired Daniel Pfeifer from Dutch trading firm Optiver and Depei Li from Jane Street </news/how-to-get-a-job-at-jane-street> spinoff Five Rings. Other recent hires from the quant space are Aarav Patel, a former Citadel Securities quant who joined last October and Rachit Surana, a former Radix Trading quant. Both work on pretraining SpaceXAI's models.
Finance alums are still a relative niche at SpaceXAI however. LinkedIn data suggests that just ~6% of the firm's members of technical staff have previously worked at a major hedge fund </news/how-to-get-a-hedge-fund-job> or trading firm </news/high-frequency-trading-hiring-and-pay>. Google, Microsoft and Meta appear to be the top funnels into the company currently.
Being an MTC can pay very well. SpaceX's open listings in California post a salary range of $180k to $440k, but data from the H1B salary database suggests that the average pay for XAI MTCs was $311k in 2025. On Levels.fyi, the average total compensation for XAI engineers since the start of 2025 is $601k, suggesting up to half of compensation for the average MTC is in stock. This could be much more (or less) valuable after SpaceX goes public.
It's not all about MTCs. SpaceX has 222 open listings on its website, most of which are either AI tutor roles, or on-site roles in its Memphis and Southaven datacentres. It's also growing a team of 'finance experts' and has hired multiple ex-investment bank trading staff for it. The most senior is Ole Kristian Birkeland, a former BNP Paribas MD and its New York-based head of Latin American credit trading. He left BNP in 2017 and was semi-retired until joining SpaceXAI in December. Other hires include Jawad Khan, a Bank of America trading strategist, and Morgan Stanley high yield credit trader Lucas Bonnike. They joined in February and March, respectively. An open listing for a finance specialist says these specialists work remotely for up to $100 an hour to train and refine XAI's models "with a focus on investment analysis." It's a similar arrangement to OpenAI's 'Project Mercury,' </news/open-ai-junior-bankers> which enlisted investment bankers on up to $150 hour to help it create financial models.

item.title: Inside Citadel Securities' big new Hong Kong hiring
item.description:
After the Financial Times <https://www.ft.com/content/70ab1705-8c9c-4dce-b9cb-1e517cdd8349?syn-25a6b1a6=1> suggested last week that Citadel Securities had moved a few quantitative researchers away from Hong Kong, it might be supposed that the electronic trading firm </news/difference-between-electronic-trading-firms>isn't hiring much in Hong Kong. That supposition would be wrong. Citadel Securities strongly disputes the FT's version of events. It's committed to Hong Kong and it's hiring there. It's hiring a lot.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-05-21/citadel-securities-added-60-people-in-asia-half-in-hong-kong> reports today that Citadel Securities has recruited 30 people in Hong Kong already this year, and that the firm intends to continue adding researchers, engineers and traders as the year goes on.
Hong Kong is Citadel Securities' main APAC office. While the firm has hired 30 people in Hong Kong so far in 2026, Citadel Securities has only hired 'more than 10' people each in Singapore and Australia. Vikesh Kotecha, Citadel Securities' head of APAC (hired from Millennium in late 2024 </news/vikesh-kotecha>) is enthusiastic about Hong Kong. "Hong Kong has one of the strongest and most diverse talent pools in the world, and we are focused on hiring the best researchers, engineers and traders as we continue to grow our business and serve our clients across APAC," he says.
Who does Citadel Securities' Hong Kong hiring look like? Kotecha may be from Millennium but other recent recruits have come from banks. Richar Smerin, the firm's new head of structured products, arrived this week from Morgan Stanley, where he spent the past 17 years. Smerin is building Citadel Securities' new global structured products business out of the Hong Kong office. More jobs in his area seem likely.
Other recent recruits include Redha Achour, the former head of investment grade credit trading for APAC at RBC. We reported </news/redha-achour-citadel-securities> that Achour was joining Citadel Securities last year. He arrived in April '25. In September 2025, Savio Joseph, the former head of APAC flow derivatives and solutions sales at UBS, also arrived at Citadel Securities as co-head of APAC sales.
Bloomberg notes that Citadel has been expanding its high touch block trading business in Asia. The firm currently has 13 open vacancies in Hong Kong. Notably, it also has four vacancies in Guguram, a satellite town of Delhi, where the firm has been hiring since at least August last year.

item.title: Morning Coffee: Morgan Stanley bankers might need to carry multiple phones. HSBC boss gives a masterclass in tact
item.description:
A few years ago, there was a minor fashion among some bankers for having multiple mobile phones. As well as your normal work and personal phones, you might have a “batphone” – a special number that you only gave out to top clients, so that they could reach you when the ordinary phone was set to voicemail. Since everyone likes to feel special, the batphone quickly became available to all clients, leading some trend setters to get a “double batphone”, the equivalent of a nightclub’s secret VVIP lounge. If you then decided to extend the principle to a “family batphone”, and wanted to occasionally purchase a “burner” for disreputable or illicit conversations, you could be carrying as many as five or six Nokias and Blackberries. It was a great period for people who repaired suit pockets.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Now, it seems like this trend might be coming back. The staff of Morgan Stanley <https://www.ft.com/content/7674f883-1f58-4601-a291-d94d25ea8b20?syn-25a6b1a6=1> in Hong Kong have been given a new phone and iPad each, to be used when they go to the mainland.
This isn’t the first such policy; Deloitte and KPMG <https://www.ft.com/content/eace699c-ce75-485b-ac14-e6c10375fdcc?syn-25a6b1a6=1> have been telling employees to use burners and leave their main devices behind when going to China for a while now. It’s not completely clear what the concern is; cybersecurity people tend not to talk about threats in very detailed terms. But it seems that there is some combination of hacking, viruses and potential exposure of client data which is increasingly seen as a risk.
So, when Morgan Stanley bankers visit Chinese clients, they are going to leave their main business phones in Hong Kong. But when they return, they are still going to need to be contactable by those clients. And they’re quite likely to need to send files back and forth, which will need to be kept separate from the main IT system.
Which means that unless there’s a very clever technological solution which allows there to be completely separate data environments on one device (which is possible, but difficult), the bankers will have to carry their China batphone around with them when they’re in Hong Kong, as well as their normal Hong Kong/international phone.
And it might not stop there. Some companies have begun to worry about phone checks at the US border <https://www.forbes.com/sites/mariagraciasantillanalinares/2025/04/17/after-border-incidents-employers-worryand-even-issue-burner-phones-for-us-travel/>, after all. The EU has quite strict data protection laws. It’s not impossible to imagine a future where the world gets significantly fragmented, and where bankers with clients in multiple jurisdictions need to carry separate physical devices for all of them.
Which would obviously mean that having lots of phones would become a status symbol once more. Perhaps the hottest fashion trend for 2027 will be a utility belt <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Batman%27s_utility_belt>.
Elsewhere, while Bill Winters of Standard Chartered is engaged on a somewhat predictable apology tour <https://www.wsj.com/finance/banking/ceo-walks-back-comment-about-replacing-lower-value-human-capital-with-ai-15bdfc5c> for his unfortunate comments about “low value human capital”, HSBC CEO Georges Elhedery <https://www.reuters.com/legal/legalindustry/dont-fight-ai-hsbc-ceo-tells-staff-banks-begin-job-cuts-2026-05-20/> is showing how it’s done. Although his message was similar in content – “We all know generative AI will destroy certain jobs and will create new jobs”, the tone couldn’t have been more different. Rather than stigmatising the people who are likely to be replaced, he said that if HSBC’s staff were “not fighting us, not disenfranchised, not anxious, overwhelmed, and resisting the change”, then they could become “more productive versions of themselves”.
It almost sounds nice, until you remember that Elhedery is at least as much of an enthusiast for new technology as Winters, and if anything has more of a reputation for cost cutting. And people with the most valuable human capital are least likely to be fooled.
The problem in banking is always that the best people have the most options, and they are easily spooked. For every hundred people that you want to get rid of, you have to be ready for another twenty or so to leave even though you would really rather have kept them. The art of managing cost reductions is always to minimise this extra attritional loss, because it’s the sort of thing that really damages franchises. However tactful you are, sometimes it’s best to just leave a big job killer like AI as the unmentioned elephant in the room.
Meanwhile …
European bankers with “low value human capital” might have less to worry about than colleagues elsewhere in the world. The regulators are beginning to warn against “excessive automation”, and that processes like credit underwriting need to be checked by humans all the way through, not just at the end. (Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-05-20/bank-ceos-ai-obsession-collides-with-red-lines-from-regulators?embedded-checkout=true>)
One of the dangers when you start to use influence gained by succeeding in the financial world to promote political views is that you make enemies. A group of teaching unions, whose pensions are investors in Apollo funds, are raising governance issues with respect to Marc Rowan’s intervention in lobbying for the resignation of university presidents. (FT <https://www.ft.com/content/249d8d43-4a75-4c5e-b368-f0cc9c0e1869?syn-25a6b1a6=1>)
Teresa Sweeney, the COO of Blackstone’s data science team, has gone to Elliott Management, to help them build out a single data repository for the whole firm to train an AI on. (Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-05-20/elliott-ramps-up-its-ai-efforts-with-key-hire-from-blackstone?taid=6a0daf202ec63a000167a718&utm_campaign=trueanthem&utm_content=business&utm_medium=social&utm_source=twitter>)
A while ago, a few enterprising quants realised that crypto exchanges offered easy pickings for algorithmic traders. Now it seems that the same thing is happening on Polymarket and Kalshi; if you’re not one of the sharks, you’re probably chum. (WSJ <https://www.wsj.com/finance/investing/polymarket-kalshi-betting-profits-prediction-markets-eb23ac11?mod=hp_listb_pos1>)
Apparently, one of the interventions which helped Kim Posnett and her team at Goldman to get top-left position on the SpaceX IPO (although Morgan Stanley are saying they are equal co-leads and it’s just alphabetical) was for David Solomon to revive the X account he used to use to promote DJ clips, and slide into Elon Musk’s DMs. (Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-05-20/goldman-ceo-slides-into-musk-s-dms-during-bid-to-lead-spacex-ipo?embedded-checkout=true>)
Harvard faculty have voted for the proposal to stop grade inflation. In a short while, the howls of the world’s most “never got a B” people will be audible as far away as Wall Street. (WSJ <https://www.wsj.com/us-news/education/harvard-grade-inflation-a-cap-a17d5d69?mod=hp_lead_pos10>)
OpenAI is now planning to IPO soon. Despite Sam Altman’s feud with Elon Musk, the lead bankers are apparently Goldman Sachs and Morgan Stanley, same as SpaceX. (WSJ <https://www.wsj.com/tech/ai/openai-ipo-filing-date-0ec95af5>)

item.title: A Citi TMT banking MD who spent 17 years at the bank in London is leaving
item.description:
Robert Farrington, a managing director in Citi's London technology, media and telecommunications (TMT) team is understood to be leaving the bank.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Citi declined to comment and Farrington didn't respond to a request to comment. Multiple sources said he has announced his resignation.
Farringdon is thought to have a new role elsewhere. Deutsche Bank has been suggested. Deutsche Bank declined to comment. Various other Citi bankers have appeared at Deutsche Bank, which appears to be getting the gang together under Alison Harding Jones and Anthony Parsons. </news/deutsche-bank-s-ex-citi-bankers>Not everyone at Deutsche Bank appears happy with this.
Farrington spent nearly 18 years at Citi after joining as a graduate trainee in 2008. He is not the only senior TMT banker to leave Citi's London office this year. Davide Russo </news/davide-russo> left in March and has yet to resurface elsewhere.
Speaking to Financial News <https://www.fnlondon.com/amp/articles/citigroups-uk-bosses-ramp-up-ambitions-after-overhaul-c22f75b8> Tiina Lee, head of Citi's business in the UK, said Citi is using money it's saved from its transformation to invest in new bankers. As we were first to report, Citi has also hired Stuart Ord, </news/stuart-ord-citi> Deutsche Bank's head of M&A and Klaus Hessberger </news/klaus-hessberger-lazard-citi>, Lazard's head of European financial sponsor coverage.
Citi's investment bank is run by Vis Raghavan who formerly worked for JPMorgan. Raghavan has hired many new people, some of them also from JPMorgan. Speaking at Citi's investor day </news/citi-investor-day-2026>earlier this month, Raghavan revealed that Citi needs 100 new MD bankers if it wants to have as many as it's 75th centile competitor, and said he plans to increase the bank's banking headcount by another 15%.
Follow me on X <https://x.com/MadameButcher>. Follow me on LinkedIn. <https://www.linkedin.com/in/sarahbutcherjournalist/>

item.title: Goldman Sachs' "incredibly charismatic" woman beat Morgan Stanley's allegedly "dorky" man
item.description:
Kim Posnett, <https://www.goldmansachs.com/our-firm/our-people-and-leadership/leadership/management-committee/kim-posnett> co-head of investment banking at Goldman Sachs </news/how-to-get-a-job-goldman-sachs>, has a reputation for being "incredibly charismatic." </news/2023/05/kim-posnett-goldman-sachs>Now it seems that Posnett is so charismatic that she has come between Elon Musk and the only banker he found tolerable.
Posnett, who was previously Goldman's head of technology, media and telecommunications banking, is running the Goldman Sachs team which will reportedly <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-05-19/goldman-sachs-is-said-to-lead-spacex-s-mega-ipo-bank-lineup?taid=6a0cea505bba4e0001a52444&utm_campaign=trueanthem&utm_content=business&utm_medium=social&utm_source=twitter>lead the line-up for SpaceX's coming IPO. In doing so, she has displaced Morgan Stanley from the top spot.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Morgan Stanley's team is being led by Michael Grimes, its chair of investment banking. Grimes left Morgan Stanley to work for the Trump administration in 2025, before going back to the bank in February 2026. <https://www.ft.com/content/7d960fcc-c80f-4c12-8b8e-1fd41fc7a444?syn-25a6b1a6=1>During his previous three decades there, Grimes ran Morgan Stanley's technology banking business and became famous for working on huge technology IPOs like Facebook, Airbnb and Uber.
Morgan Stanley really likes Grimes. At the time of his 2025 departure, Grimes was on a $10.5m salary </news/michael-grimes-morgan-stanley> with $56m of deferred stock bonuses accumulated over the previous three years. Speaking to the Financial Times <https://www.ft.com/content/7d960fcc-c80f-4c12-8b8e-1fd41fc7a444?syn-25a6b1a6=1> upon his return, Morgan Stanley said Grimes would "continue to manage and build relationships with many of our most important global corporate, venture, private equity and sovereign clients.”
Those clients include Elon Musk, who founded SpaceX in 2002 and who presumably had a key role in selecting Posnett's team to lead the imminent SpaceX IPO. Musk and Grimes have history: they worked together on Musk's $44bn acquisition of Twitter. In February, the Wall Street Journal <https://www.wsj.com/tech/elon-musks-go-to-banker-is-back-in-action-for-the-spacex-ipo-1dac530a>described Grimes as one of the only bankers Musk could tolerate. When Grimes returned to Morgan Stanley three months ago, it was therefore widely expected that the key role on the SpaceX IPO would be his.
It's not. Business Insider <https://www.businessinsider.com/spacex-ipo-banks-wall-street-fees-goldman-sachs-2026-5>reports that Morgan Stanley is still working as a lead bank on the SpaceX IPO, just not as the lead. Instead, it is reportedly the stabilization agent, tasked with supporting the stock price after the IPO.
It's not clear why Goldman Sachs won. Grimes, who has a background in computer engineering, seems a bit like a tech entrepreneur. He's "dorky" </news/michael-grimes-banker> according to a friend <https://www.wsj.com/finance/michael-grimes-trump-investment-accelerator-e9f185ef?mod=e2tw> who spoke to the WSJ. That same friend added, “He doesn’t talk like a banker. He isn’t slick.” Grimes is immersed in new technologies. During his brief time with the government, Grimes was said </news/michael-grimes>to upset people with interview questions like, "What’s four to the fourth power?."
Posnett seems polished by comparison. She was briefly a Hollywood actress and has said <https://fortune.com/ranking/40-under-40/2016/kim-posnett/>this experience taught her how to tell a story. Posnett is popular with Goldman Sachs' CEO David Solomon. Posnett was also popular with Goldman Sachs' former head of investment banking Greg Lemkau. It was Lemkau who ascribed the incredible charisma to her.
Somewhere along the line, Musk may have been persuaded by this incredible charisma too. Either that, or he just wanted a change.
SpaceX intends to raise $75bn; underwriting fees on the deal could be $4bn or more <https://www.marketwatch.com/story/wall-street-is-set-to-reap-a-fee-bonanza-from-the-spacex-ipo-that-could-reach-1-billion-13f6c906?regwall=eafs>.
Follow me on X <https://x.com/MadameButcher>. Follow me on LinkedIn. <https://www.linkedin.com/in/sarahbutcherjournalist/>

item.title: Which coding languages pay the most for banking technology jobs
item.description:
Working in technology at an investment bank </news/how-to-get-a-technology-job-bank> can earn you a lot of money, but pay is not distributed evenly. Depending on what coding language </news/programming-languages-for-a-career-in-finance> you specialise in, you could find yourself earning a lot more money than your peers.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
We've looked technology job listings for top US investment banks in New York, where salary disclosures are mandated by law. After analyzing over 500 datapoints for four of the most popular languages </news/programming-languages-jobs-banks-finance> in finance, we found that C++ </news/python-solves-the-same-problem-as-c-70-times-slower> engineers tend to earn the most, but Python </news/python-libraries-for-finance> has the most variation in pay.
C++ outperformed the other languages on average for both the upper and lower bounds of salary ranges. High-level C++ engineers </news/low-latency-c> can expect to earn $233k on average, and can earn up to $325k if you're truly exceptional. High-level Python professionals earn just over $210k on average. The highest available salary we found, however, was a JPMorgan managing director </news/2019/07/pay-managing-directors-tech> (MD) working as a treasury quant in its consumer and community bank. This gave a salary range of $300k to $500k and was the only MD listing we found. The next highest salary we found in Python was still higher than the other languages: $350k.
Python also had the lowest average salary at the lower bound. There are multiple reasons for this; there are significantly more listings available for Python than C++ and JavaScript, and the jobs tended to skew towards junior professionals. There are many more roles outside of pure engineering that use Python for data analysis </news/python-libraries-for-finance>; the lowest Python salary we found was for a junior structurer at Citi, but other banks had various data analysis and risk roles that brought down Python's average.
Salary, of course, is only one aspect of compensation. Banks pay hefty bonuses (on occasion </news/technology-bonuses-banks>) to technologists; our 2026 salary and bonus report found that, last year, the average bonus for banking technologists was 37% of salary. For quants, </news/quant-jobs-finance> who are more likely to use Python, bonuses were 66%.
Have a confidential story, tip, or comment you’d like to share? Contact: WhatsApp: http://wa.me/442079977910 <http://wa.me/442079977910> (+44 20 7997 7910), Telegram: @AlexMcMurray, Signal: @AlexMcMurrayEFC.88 Click here to fill in our anonymous form </about/editor-tips>, or email editortips@efinancialcareers.com.
Bear with us if you leave a comment at the bottom of this article: comments are moderated intermittently by human beings. Sometimes these humans might be asleep, or away from their desks, so it may take a while for your comment to appear. You must take sole responsibility for comments you post on this site. We will take reasonable steps to weed out anything that we consider to be offensive or inappropriate.

item.title: Ex-banking and consulting employees were fired from Meta today
item.description:
Meta, the tech company formerly known as Facebook, has had a brutal day. 8,000 employees, 10% of its global workforce, was or will be fired today. Among those put to the axe were former employees of a plethora of financial services firms.
Click here to join the bubble by eFinancialCareers, our new anonymous community. <https://bubble.efinancialcareers.com/login?_gl=1*ut8n6u*_gcl_au*MTg4MDY2NTExNi4xNzYxNTU3NDIz*_ga*ODczNzI2NTA0LjE3NTM2OTk2ODI.*_ga_NZFQV4MXPM*czE3NjM2NDM0MDYkbzI1NiRnMSR0MTc2MzY0MzQzOSRqMjckbDAkaDMyMjc4OTUzMw..>✍️
Former employees of Citi, Goldman Sachs, and Barclays who'd moved to Meta have been writing tearful and heartfelt departure messages on social media. Many of them reported themselves as top performers at Meta. They now report themselves as #OpenToWork.
More than any other single financial services firm (that we saw), there were a lot of ex-Deloitte employees among the pile of discarded Metamates. A “Metamate” is what Meta’s employees call each other, apparently.
One of them who left Deloitte for Meta was a senior consultant for Deloitte and was booted from Meta after less than a year working there – on his birthday. He noted that, in performance reports, he regularly exceeded expectations.
On professional careers forum Blind, reception to the layoffs were more mixed. “Meta employees have the highest egos… On average Meta employees sh*t on every other company facing layoffs en masse,” one Amazon employee said. “I feel like the average Meta employee needs this chaos and this humbling exercise.”
One Meta employee, while trying to defend themselves and their culture in another thread on the platform, said “a little sympathy would be nice,” as “it could be anyone in tech next.”

item.title: Lorna Hajdini sues Chirayu Rana, says her life has become "a daily living nightmare"
item.description:
Lorna Hajdini, </news/lorna-hajdini-jpmorgan-support> the JPMorgan leveraged finance executive director accused of exploiting male vice president Chirayu Rana </news/chirayu-rana-jpmorgan> and of making him her sex slave, is countersuing Rana.
In a court filing yesterday, Hajdini's lawyers repeatedly denied all Rana's accusations and laid out her claim of 'defamation, defamation per se, intentional infliction of emotional distress, prima facie tort, and intentional interference with business relations.'
Hajdini's lawyers didn't respond to a request to comment for this article. Her full filing can be found here. <https://cdn.filestackcontent.com/vYdcfBOfTGqCzTP8lS2f>
Rana's lawyer didn't respond to a request to comment for this article. His earlier filing detailing his complaints against Hajdini and JPMorgan can be found here. <https://cdn.filestackcontent.com/LKY1ufUPTV2Jwvx5qLSK>
JPMorgan also did not respond to a request to comment for this article. A spokesperson for the bank told the New York Post: “We fully support Lorna and her right to defend herself and protect her reputation. As we have said from the outset, we don’t believe the allegations against her or the firm have merit.”
Hajdini's new filing states that her life has become "a daily, living nightmare from which she has been unable to escape." Although she is still working for JPMorgan, the filing says Hajdini, a "hard worker" who performs at an "exemplary level" is finding it harder to do her job. She is unable to take public transport and must pay for "private and/or shared-ride transportation to avoid media and other public scrutiny."
The new filing states that Hajdini "has sought and received mental-health treatment to manage the severe emotional distress" caused by Rana's claims. It says there have been threats to her personal safety and that both Hajdini and members of her family "have been mocked, ridiculed, and harassed around the clock." Hadjini has been "the ongoing focal point of countless jokes, memes, and AI generated images and videos of a persistently vile, degrading, and sexual nature."
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Hajdini's filing adds that her "beloved" work with an organisation representing underprivileged students has also come to an end.
In the circumstances, Hajdini says Rana must compensate her for "compensatory and punitive damages, as well as the full costs of this proceeding, including attorney’s fees," while also paying special damages for mental healthcare to manage her "emotional fallout."
Hajdini's filing accuses Rana of being a 'sexual predator' who maliciously targeted her with the intention of both destroying her reputation and extracting millions of dollars from JPMorgan and herself. It also alleges that Rana made a similar complaint at a previous employer and says that he fabricated the death of his father to get time off work.
Speaking to the Wall Street Journal <https://www.wsj.com/business/jpmorgan-banker-chirayu-rana-sexual-assault-lorna-hajdini-179b67b3>earlier this month, Rana's attorney Daniel Kaiser said: “We look forward to the truth and facts coming to light through court proceedings. Whether my client’s civil rights were violated will be determined in a court of law.” Kaiser told the WSJ that although Rana's father hadn't died, a “dad-like figure” who helped raise him had instead passed away.
Rana's original claim states that Hadjini "leveraged her seniority" to compel him to perform lewd acts. Among other things, he alleges that Hadjii told him suck her toes in return for facilitating his promotion and bonus. Hajdini denies this. It says she was at no time Rana's supervisor and neither prepared his performance reviews, determined his bonus, nor decided his promotions.
JPMorgan said previously that it fully investigated the allegations and found no wrongdoing. In her filing, Hajdini says she sat for 'lengthy' interviews and provided evidence to prove that Rana's accusations were untrue.
Even if Hajdini succeeds in her complaint against Rana, it is not clear whether Rana will be in a position to compensate her. The WSJ reported previously that Rana was earning over $500k </news/chirayu-rana-jpmorgan-1m> a year at JPMorgan. It also reported that he initially asked the bank for $22m in damages </news/chirayu-rana-jpmorgan-court>. This has not been confirmed by Rana's attorney.

item.title: Goldman Sachs’ newest trader in New York didn't seem to work for two years
item.description:
Career breaks aren’t for everyone </news/2019/09/no-jobs-left-in-risk-banking>. Sometimes you come back and get nothing. Sometimes you come back and work an even better employer than before.
Click here to join the bubble by eFinancialCareers, our new anonymous community. <https://bubble.efinancialcareers.com/login?_gl=1*ut8n6u*_gcl_au*MTg4MDY2NTExNi4xNzYxNTU3NDIz*_ga*ODczNzI2NTA0LjE3NTM2OTk2ODI.*_ga_NZFQV4MXPM*czE3NjM2NDM0MDYkbzI1NiRnMSR0MTc2MzY0MzQzOSRqMjckbDAkaDMyMjc4OTUzMw..>✍️
Towny Swiggett joined Goldman Sachs this month as a managing director (MD). He spent the last two years out of the industry, and was previously at hedge fund </news/how-to-get-a-hedge-fund-job> Whitehaven Asset Management as a partner and senior investment professional. He traded municipal bonds at Barclays before that.
Whitehaven’s flagship fund is an actively managed fund in the $3.7tn US municipal bond market. It appears he will still be involved in municipal bonds at Goldman, too. About a year and a half after Swiggett left the fund, it announced that it was returning cash to investors to become a family office, Bloomberg reported <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2025-12-18/muni-hedge-fund-whitehaven-to-return-cash-become-family-office>.
Municipal bonds, also known as munis, are a pretty good place to be in 2026. A report from Goldman Sachs Asset Management said <https://am.gs.com/en-us/advisors/insights/article/2026/key-themes-munis-2026-outlook> that market volatility will persist, driven by “the selection of a new Federal Reserve Chair, the ongoing impact of evolving economic data, and increasing policy unpredictability.” Issuances will increase, too. Both of those things are good for traders.
Muni trading has not been a great career for everyone, however. Citi exited the business back in 2023. "It became quite difficult to profitably trade both munis and secondary credit," said head of markets Andy Morton at its 2026 investor day <https://www.citigroup.com/rcs/citigpa/storage/public/investor-day/citi-ID26-markets-presentation-transcript.pdf>. Since then, the firm's intermediation revenue has fallen significantly behind its financing revenue. Maybe munis are doing better now.
