item.title: A Google cloud director left after 20 years to join Goldman Sachs
item.description:
Nowadays, AI labs </news/the-difference-between-working-as-a-quant-in-finance-and-as-a-researcher-in-an-ai-lab> like OpenAI </news/openai-culture> are the most attractive destination for technologists but, in the early 2000s, tech giants like Google </news/2023/01/google-layoffs-goldman-sachs> ruled the roost. Jordyn Buchanan joined as an engineering manager back in 2006 and stayed there for two decades; he's finally moving on, but he's opting for a move into finance.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Buchanan, now in his early 50s, announced via LinkedIn that he's joining Goldman Sachs as a managing director. </news/what-is-an-md-investment-bank> It's not clear what his exact role is, but he worked most recently on Google's cloud transformation. He says he was involved in creating policies for internal consumption of its primary compute services (Google Compute Engine and Google Kubernetes engine), as well as its AI enterprise agent </news/agentic-ai-generative-use-cases-finance> platform.
Rather than working in California, Buchanan spent his Google tenure in New York. He first joined in the city to build out its site reliability team, but went on to work in its storage infrastructure team, then its domains team.
All this probably sounds good to Goldman, which is building out its AI powered 'operating system' known as OneGS 3.0 </news/goldman-sachs-cutting-2025>. The bank is looking for areas to streamline with AI, and has already cut a small number of jobs as part of this.
Buchanan is one of many big tech alums to switch to Goldman Sachs. The firm's chief information officer, Marco Argenti, joined from AWS </news/2020/01/marco-argenti-goldman-sachs> in 2019 and has been one of the bank's biggest AI proponents. </news/english-as-a-coding-language-llm>

item.title: “A senior banker assaulted me. This is the advice I give other young women”
item.description:
You don’t think it will happen to you but as recent cases <https://www.ft.com/content/edbe449d-fbb0-4682-8f56-557bd065c76e?syn-25a6b1a6=1>have made clear, it might. There are still men who feel they can act with impunity in financial services and there are still young women who suffer.
A few years into my career, I was assaulted by a senior man on my team. I will not go into details, but it left me traumatised. Despite this, I have gone on to have a good career.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
My advice to young women in banking who experience anything similar is to get out immediately. At the very least go on sick leave. If you’re in the UK, claim constructive dismissal.
The longer you stay in the firm alongside the perpetrator, the greater the disadvantage you will be at. As the victim, you will not be thinking clearly, and you are in an extremely vulnerable position. Your employer will despatch HR to interview you, but HR are effectively the employment law function of the firm.
When you enter an interview with HR, they will typically record everything you say, and if you are not thinking clearly they will use it against you. They are not impartial but are effectively there to protect the firm. If the man you are up against is a key revenue generator, they are there to protect him. There is an asymmetry of experience and of information: HR will have dealt with this kind of thing before and will know what the man is saying. You are the victim, have no experience, and do not know what HR are being told by the perpetrator.
HR will usually buy time. They will tell you they are conducting an investigation. Then they will come back to you and say they have found nothing. During this time, your working life will have been impossible, and your mental health will have deteriorated. HR will wear you down. Then they will fire you and ask you to sign an NDA which waives your right to sue.
Going up against this is tough and expensive and is why many women don’t bother. If you think you want to fight, don’t stick around to be drained. I fought. I found a new job elsewhere and I am still in the industry now. My career has been affected – I earn less and have been promoted less and have spent a fortune on lawyers. But it’s been worth it. Women need to stand up.
Joanna Griffiths is a pseudonym

item.title: Morning Coffee: Citi hired the man with JPMorgan’s most interesting job in London. Bank CEO’s controversial comments
item.description:
Chuka Umunna <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-05-19/citi-hires-former-shadow-business-secretary-umunna-for-uk-team?utm_medium=email&utm_source=author_alert&utm_term=260519&utm_campaign=author_18657817&embedded-checkout=true> didn’t have a particularly auspicious start in investment banking. </news/what-is-an-investment-bank> He was a British politician who looked like a rising star in the 2010s, but fell out with Jeremy Corbyn, then launched a party of his own which quickly imploded and then gave up politics just in time to miss the Labour Party’s revival. But Chuka is making up for lost time. After giving up on politics, he joined JPMorgan five years ago. Now he’s gone to Citi, joining Stuart Ord </news/stuart-ord-citi>, Joe Seifert and all the other big bankers being brought in as Tiina Lee <https://www.fnlondon.com/articles/citigroups-uk-bosses-ramp-up-ambitions-after-overhaul-c22f75b8?mod=homepage> and James Fleming inflate the bank’s UK franchise. Alongside his new job (and presumably a significant increment in pay), Chuka is likely to be doing some deals.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Umunna’s early title at JP Morgan was “Global Head of Corporate Governance and Sustainable Solutions”. This sounds quite nice, but to people familiar with the industry it’s got that “oddly non-specific” feel to it which is often associated with former politicians and other appointments which bring prestige to the franchise rather than directly generating revenue.
That all changed in February 2026, when Umunna became UK Head of JPMorgan’s Security and Resiliency Initiative <https://www.fnlondon.com/articles/jpmorgan-rolls-out-1-5tn-defence-financing-push-to-europe-f41cb759>. This is JPMorgan’s worthy and exciting project to finance industries relevant to national security. Globally, the bank is making $10bn of direct venture capital investments with the aim of facilitating $1.5trn of total financing in industries including defence, aerospace, supply chains and nano materials. Most of the money is likely to be invested in the USA rather than the UK, but that’s a lot of deals. JPMorgan’s illustrious advisory council <https://www.jpmorgan.com/about-us/security-and-resiliency > for the security and resilience mission includes the likes of Jeff Bezos.
Umunna has given this all up for Vis Raghavan’s </news/vis-raghavan-pay-citi>Citi. Presumably he will be an MD, with revenue-generating responsibilities to match.
More broadly, Umunna’s career trajectory tells us a lot about the development of ESG as a field. When ESG meant 'Environment, Social and Governance', the main use of a former politician in was to help smooth over relationships with people who might get angry at you, while advising on the financing of renewable energy subsidies.
Nowadays, though, ESG is coming to mean “Energy, Security and Guns.” Global governments are rethinking defence spending. Someone with a good contact book and familiarity with the workings of government is now not just a nice “third MD in the room” to help meetings go more smoothly; they’re potentially able steer the path to someone with check-writing authority. Umunna appears to have mastered the art of being in the right place at the right time.
Elsewhere, people are getting cross <https://www.bloomberg.com/opinion/articles/2026-05-19/ai-job-cuts-even-top-bankers-must-listen-to-their-inner-human> about Standard Chartered CEO Bill Winters, who referred to some of his staff as “low value human capital <https://www.thetimes.com/business/companies-markets/article/bank-cuts-7800-roles-to-swap-lower-value-human-capital-with-ai-ffg9xghrs>”. It’s an unlovely phrase, admittedly. But in banking, it’s often better to get a blunt truth than a polite lie. In context, Winters was referring to as many as 7,800 people in back-office and corporate centre roles, who are likely to be replaced by AI.
There’s not really a nice way to put that; the problem isn’t that the language is disrespectful, it’s that the news is bad. Even if Winters had strategized for hours with his comms team and come up with a phrase like “front-line AI trailblazers”, people would still be upset. There’s a phenomenon called the “euphemism treadmill <https://stevenpinker.com/files/pinker/files/1994_04_03_newyorktimes.pdf>”, whereby something which starts as a polite way of referring to a nasty reality inevitably gets tainted by association with the thing that it describes.
So, although “people” is almost always better than “human capital”, and it’s never a good look to refer to one’s fellow human beings as “surplus”, “low value” or similar, we shouldn’t really spend too much time blaming Bill. In many ways, criticising his words is a coping strategy to try and ignore the importance of what he said.
Meanwhile …
Half of Deutsche Bank’s businesses earn less than their cost of capital. Christian Sewing is not saying which ones at this point. And given that banking is a cyclical business it’s probably more important to know how well the successful half are doing. The intention is to reallocate capital until 70% of it is allocated to businesses beating their benchmark. (Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-05-19/deutsche-bank-says-just-half-of-business-earned-cost-of-capital?utm_medium=email&utm_source=author_alert&utm_term=260519&utm_campaign=author_23851719>)
Lucy Baldwin has gone from head of global head of research at Citi to global head of equities at Rothschild. That’s a move down in size of firm, but a move up in terms of responsibilities; given that equity research is a notoriously challenged business line, it probably makes sense in career terms. (TheTrade <https://www.thetradenews.com/citi-global-head-of-research-to-lead-equities-at-rothschild-co/>)
After a few not so great years, Deloitte is raising its bonus pool by 14% and lots of accountants will be getting promotions. (FT <https://www.ft.com/content/b4c70f82-d721-43e2-ad83-a86a3f609b0d>)
Darin Oduyoye, who is responsible for putting together the JP Morgan wealth management summer book recommendations, says that “family office clients are especially interested in books on generational transition”, presumably now that “Succession” and “Game of Thrones” have come to an end. Other recommendations from JPM advisors seem to consist of the usual mix of dad-friendly history, airport business biography and oddly technical finance. (CNBC <https://www.cnbc.com/2026/05/18/jpmorgan-summer-reading-list.html>)
If you’re looking for an alternative career to make six figures in New York, the hotel housekeeper’s union have agreed a new set of contracts that will take their members there. (NYT <https://www.nytimes.com/2026/05/18/nyregion/nyc-hotel-housekeepers-pay.html>)
Or if you want something more glamorous, Mark Zuckerberg is looking for a “beach water person” (seems to mean a lifeguard) for his place in Hawaii. (WIRED <https://www.wired.com/story/mark-zuckerberg-priscilla-chan-lifeguard-beach-water-person/?utm_brand=wired&utm_social-type=owned&utm_source=twitter&utm_medium=social&utm_campaign=aud-dev>)

item.title: “Ignore the rankings that say big US banks are the best places to work”
item.description:
Another ranking </news/top-ten-best-employers-finance>is out, this time from LinkedIn <https://www.linkedin.com/pulse/linkedin-top-companies-2026-10-best-employers-grow-your-pu29f/?trackingId=caHSPNamQTWKnL1X5cgSLQ%3D%3D>, saying that big US banks are some of the best places to work for.
Take it from me: they are not.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
I have worked for the organisations at the top of the list and while there are undoubtedly some brilliant people there, these banks are not good places to work. They destroyed my career. </news/banking-career-destroyed-md>
I was a top producer. I had no compliance </news/how-to-get-a-job-in-compliance> or conduct issues, no blow-ups with anyone. My career was going well until a cabal of managing directors at the bank I worked for manipulated my numbers so that I looked bad. Then they got rid of me.
I joined another US bank. It was no better. It was full of low quality people managing decrepit systems and had a mouse infestation on a trading floor that smelled like a toilet.
You will not learn this kind of thing from reading ranking articles, which always present organisations in a good light.
Large banks are not meritocratic. They are about politics, alignment and managerial incentives. Succeeding in one is not just about delivering outcomes – it’s about navigating these systems. And just because a bank is strong in one business area, don’t assume that it will be strong in your area. There are always huge nuances.
Never be deceived by prestige. Having a logo on your resume will help up to a point, but not if you’re spewed out by a system that's being gamed by the kinds of corporate players that thrive in these places.
Take it from me. You're better off joining a smaller name firm that doesn't attract narcissists.
Fabien Grandis is a pseudonym

item.title: "Python solves the same problem as C++... 70 times slower"
item.description:
C++ and Python </news/python-or-c-hudson-river-trading-explains-which-languages-are-needed-for-each-job> are incredibly different programming languages, </news/programming-languages-for-a-career-in-finance> but they're more alike than you might think. Bjarne Stroustrup, who created C++ </news/finance/c-coding-language-problems>, said on a recent podcast <https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=U46fJ2bJ-co> that Python serves the same "fundamental needs" that he was serving when he created his language.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Speaking to ex-Meta </news/meta-s-ex-morgan-stanley-cfo-was-one-its-least-efficient-sql-users> engineer Ryan Peterman, Stroustrup said he wanted something that had "high and low-level facilities in the same language" when building C++. Python is an innately high-level language, but has an abundance of libraries </news/python-libraries-for-finance>which provide low-level performance. Stroustrup says that C++ is "the reason [Python] is viable," since those libraries are mostly written in either C++ or C.
Python may be boosted by C++ but it can rarely compete in terms of speed. Stroustrup said that, even when boosted by C++, Python "runs something like 70 times slower." Older studies have suggested that Python is up to 57 times slower </news/2023/06/which-programming-language-uses-the-most-energy> than C++ for basic programs. Mojo, </news/ai-people-don-t-like-c-jane-street-has-its-traders-use-this-lanugage-instead> a superset of Python, is trying to close that gap, but it's yet to fully take off.
Python is better for experimentation. Stroustrup said this is "because you don't have to know enough about the language. You don't have to know about type systems." He said that C++ is a language for computer scientists </news/financial-services-technology-jobs-no-degree>, while Python serves a wider breadth of people, from web developers to physicists.
In quant finance and electronic trading, where some of the most lucrative engineering jobs </news/low-latency-c>on earth can be found, there's a notion that Python is like a toy programming language. This can be a good thing; Jane Street has become enamoured with Python in a machine learning context, as it allows their traders to iterate through ideas quickly, then offload the best ones to low-level technologists to build that strategy out.
Which language should you learn? Perhaps both; Stroustrup said "it's not good for you to be monoglot," since you get stuck in the trends and ideas of that language's community. He said you should "broaden your mind with ideas and techniques," since engineers need to build things.

item.title: Goldman Sachs' head of US fixed income sales is retiring after 12 years as a partner
item.description:
It's nearly midway through the year, and partners are retiring from Goldman Sachs. First there was James Houghton, a macro salesman and member of Goldman's APAC operating committee. Now there's Matt Verrochi, a onetime cross-asset salesman, who was Goldman's head of Americas fixed income sales until sometime this week.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Goldman Sachs isn't commenting but sources at the firm say that Verrochi has retired. He became a managing director at the firm in 2010 and a partner in 2012. As is the case with partners who leave, Verrochi will remain affiliated to Goldman in an advisory role.
It is not clear who will replace Verrochi. He began his career at Goldman Sachs in 2004 and ran the San Francisco trading floor <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2015-07-23/goldman-sachs-s-verrochi-heads-west-to-replace-recktenwald>from 2015 before being promoted to co-head Americas fixed income sales in New York in 2021.
Verrochi, to whom we have not spoken, appears to be genuinely retiring. If he wants another job, he could always try Citadel Securities, where some retired Goldman partners </news/goldman-sachs-citadel-securities>seem to be having a second wind.

item.title: Citi and Deutsche Bank hired new rates traders in Paris
item.description:
JPMorgan may have decided </news/jpmorgan-paris-traders>that it doesn't want so many rates traders in Paris, but there are still plenty of these traders in the French capital. Citi and Deutsche Bank have each hired one.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Neither bank is commenting and nor are the traders themselves, but we understand that both Victor Caen, a EUR inflation trader at BNP Paribas and Richard Bert, a EUR inflation trader at Morgan Stanley have got new jobs.
Caen is understood to be joining Citi. Bert is understood to be joining Deutsche Bank.
The moves come as inflation is a new and rising concern in the wake of war in the Middle East. Caen and Bert are part a rising generation of inflation traders, having begun their careers in 2012 and 2017 respectively.
Caen has had more time to rise than Bert. He looks like a replacement for Douglas Cypel, </news/citi-s-top-inflation-trader-is-joining-brevan-howard-goldman-s-is-a-man-of-mystery> who disappeared to Brevan Howard in February.

item.title: Morgan Stanley hired a new tech banker in California. It's too late for what really matters
item.description:
Morgan Stanley’s Menlo Park office is a pretty cool place. The weather is great, the city is a lovely place to raise a family, and technology banking is booming. Luckily, Morgan Stanley is even adding to the fun with some new hires.
Click here to join the bubble by eFinancialCareers, our new anonymous community. <https://bubble.efinancialcareers.com/login?_gl=1*ut8n6u*_gcl_au*MTg4MDY2NTExNi4xNzYxNTU3NDIz*_ga*ODczNzI2NTA0LjE3NTM2OTk2ODI.*_ga_NZFQV4MXPM*czE3NjM2NDM0MDYkbzI1NiRnMSR0MTc2MzY0MzQzOSRqMjckbDAkaDMyMjc4OTUzMw..>✍️
Software banker Aaron Pine joined Morgan Stanley as a managing director (MD) in its Menlo Park office. He joined the firm from Citi, where he worked for 18 years in San Francisco, and was made an MD in 2020 </news/2020/12/citi-managing-directors-2020>.
Weather aside, Morgan Stanley’s Menlo Park office might be the most exciting place to be in the industry this year. It’s likely to the be the office from which the bank will oversee the public listing of Elon Musk’s SpaceX </news/bankers-spacex-ipo>, which will be the largest IPO in history.
Aside from its unique size, the SpaceX IPO will also be uniquely complicated. Aside from being a defence contractor and rocket manufacturer, SpaceX also runs a telecommunications platform (Starlink) and has a multi-centi-billion-dollar AI firm bolted onto it in xAI, which operates Grok. It also owns X, formerly known as Twitter, because why not. There are 21 banks in the syndicate.
Morgan Stanley has been Elon Musk’s go-to investment bank for years. And in February, the firm re-hired Michael Grimes </news/michael-grimes-morgan-stanley> in Menlo Park as chairman of investment banking; Grimes has been called a “Musk whisperer <https://www.ft.com/content/21be3dde-2fcb-41ff-a6d6-d71fede4e8b8?syn-25a6b1a6=1>” for his connection to the SpaceX CEO. But it might not be enough: Polymarket has Goldman Sachs <https://polymarket.com/event/lead-bank-in-spacexs-ipo-836> pegged to be the lead bank on the SpaceX IPO, after months of Morgan Stanley being in the lead.

item.title: Standard Chartered already cut 88% of one team, but these jobs look safe
item.description:
AI is coming for 20,000 jobs at HSBC </news/hsbc-ai-job-cuts>and now it is also coming for around 8,000 at Standard Chartered. At an investor event <https://www.sc.com/en/investors/events-and-presentations/2026-investor-event/> in Asia, Standard Chartered declared that it intends to cut 15% of jobs in its corporate functions by 2030, aided by automation. The Financial Times <https://www.ft.com/content/ff678495-2a99-4a70-a340-31c0f8094b4c?syn-25a6b1a6=1> notes that this means 8,000 people.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
While corporate function jobs at Standard Chartered are due to disappear in the next four years, there are pockets where a more complete kind of decimation happened already. In its 'transformation presentation <https://www.sc.com/en/uploads/sites/66/content/docs/standard-chartered-may-2026-investor-event-transformation-presentation.pdf>' yesterday, Standard Chartered declared that AI has already enabled an "88% reduction in monitoring manpower" via a "central observability platform." Almost everyone who was monitoring its processes has therefore gone.
It's not all bad news. People in corporate functions are in jeopardy and people in monitoring roles have been done away with, but some people at Standard Chartered have a promising future.
These people are in Standard Chartered's global markets business and interface with financial institutions clients like banks, broker dealers, fintech firms, hedge funds </news/how-to-get-a-hedge-fund-job> and asset managers. Much as Citi </news/citi-investor-day-2026> has decided that it wants to increase its market share with hedge funds, Standard Chartered has decided that it wants to increase its market share with all these financial institutions.
Yesterday's CIB presentation <https://www.sc.com/en/uploads/sites/66/content/docs/standard-chartered-may-2026-investor-event-cib-presentation.pdf> explains why. Financial institutions clients generate higher returns on risk weighted assets than corporate clients, says Standard Chartered. While corporate clients generated returns of 6% last year, financial institutions clients generated returns of 8%. This matters in a world where Standard Chartered wants to achieve a return on tangible equity of 18% across the bank by 2030. If you work in prime broking, </news/prime-broking-banks-2026> your job is probably safe.
Meanwhile, the 8,000 job cuts in the corporate functions look like the natural consequence of an evolutionary process. Standard Chartered explained <https://www.sc.com/en/uploads/sites/66/content/docs/standard-chartered-may-2026-investor-event-transformation-presentation.pdf> how it first created a "horizontal shared platform" and moved its back office into "centralized hubs" (AKA "global capability centres"). It's now ready to build an "agile operating model" with fewer people, 20% higher productivity per person and a cost to income ratio of 57% instead of 63%.
Other banks have also followed this process. Other banks are likely to do the same thing. As the flood waters rise, prime broking relationships are the high ground.
Follow me on X <https://x.com/MadameButcher>. Follow me on LinkedIn. <https://www.linkedin.com/in/sarahbutcherjournalist/>

item.title: Hedge fund Balyasny hired a JPMorgan trading MD in Hong Kong
item.description:
Balyasny </news/where-do-portfolio-managers-work-balyasny> has been looking to grow in Asia after an excellent 2025. Revenues in the continent were up 82% <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-01-16/balyasny-asia-revenue-jumps-82-to-record-during-expansion-drive>year-on-year, and headcount across Hong Kong, Singapore and Tokyo has risen to more than 250. In 2026, it's attracting some big names to join the party.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Among them is Ronny Dharma, who joins the hedge fund </news/how-to-get-a-hedge-fund-job>'s Hong Kong office after nine and a half years with JPMorgan. He made MD in 2021, and was latterly JPMorgan's APAC head of volatility trading. Before JPMorgan, Dharma spent a decade on the equity derivatives desk at Merrill Lynch, starting as a strat </news/strats-jobs-investment-banks> before becoming a trader.
Dharma's not the only PM to arrive in Asia. Last month, we reported </news/balyasny-hired-a-brevan-howard-pm-in-singapore-as-it-looks-to-grow-in-asia> that Trung Nguyen joined Balyasny from Brevan Howard in Singapore.
Balyasny is growing its office footprint both in and outside of Asia. Alongside tripling its Tokyo office space, it is also opening a new office in Abu Dhabi, </news/balyasny-abu-dhabi> led by ExodusPoint alum Yash Agarwal.
JPMorgan and Balyasny declined to comment.

item.title: Will Jane Street's most lucrative Asia trading roles stay in Hong Kong?
item.description:
Jane Street, </news/how-to-get-a-job-at-jane-street> the trading firm </news/high-frequency-trading-hiring-and-pay> that paid staff an average of $2.7m </news/jane-street-pay-2026> last year, employs more than 600 people across its Asian offices. Soon there will be more; Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-05-17/jane-street-doubles-capacity-in-singapore-as-expansion-rolls-on> reported today that the firm is doubling its office footprint in Singapore to a capacity of 250 people. What does that mean for Jane Street's lucrative trading roles? </news/jane-street-s-traders-on-1-4m-have-fighter-pilot-eyes-high-pain-thresholds>
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Jane Street's Singapore office currently appears to be an engineering hub. There are few traders with public profiles on LinkedIn, and its most senior recent hires are all engineers. David Bridson, for example, joined as an engineer last June </news/jane-street-hired-an-engineer-from-google-s-rebellious-ai-team-and-moved-him-to-singapore> from Google Deepmind. Samir Kuthala, a Goldman Sachs executive director, joined this February.
Jane Street's Singapore job listings are similarly tech focused. There are currently 16 job openings in Singapore, almost entirely in either engineering or cybersecurity, with no available trading roles. This includes two gradate roles in software and linux engineering. In Hong Kong, by contrast, there are 17 openings in its trading, research and machine learning division, eight of which are graduate or internship </news/this-is-what-it-takes-to-get-a-64k-jane-street-internship> roles.
Although working in tech at Jane Street pays very well, that $2.7m average appears to skew heavily to trading roles. Levels.fyi data suggests that Jane Street software engineers were paid an average of $386.6k in 2025.
Bloomberg reported that the new Singapore office will house trading staff, as well as tech and infrastructure specialists. It's not clear whether it plans to increase its proportion of trading staff in the city, though. Jane Street did not respond to a request for comment.
Jane Street did not respond to a request for comment.

item.title: Morning Coffee: Hedge fund analyst's attempt to sue boss for $134m went badly. The management lore of Brian Moynihan at Bank of America
item.description:
The life of a hedge fund analyst is not what people imagine. While the imagination might suggest exciting and consequential investment analyses, protein-based diets and fast cars, this is not always so. As an analyst, your analysis and ideas may be ignored, your working days may be long and your pay is often decided entirely by your portfolio manager, who may decide to give the money to himself. It can be a terrible job. </news/analyst-hedge-fund>
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
For the past few years, one London hedge fund professional, who began as a junior hedge fund analyst and progressed to becoming the 'right hand man' of a top portfolio manager (PM), has been locked in a battle with his former boss. Having worked 12-18 hours a day </news/junior-hedge-fund-analyst-working-hours> for a PM who was the son of a friend of his mother </news/hedge-fund-davide-leone-jacopo-moretti>, Jacopo Moretti was fired after reportedly losing $86m <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2025-05-15/hedge-fund-founder-davide-leone-accused-of-bullying-homophobia-in-uk-suit>and being off sick for a year. Moretti then claimed that he had been unfairly dismissed and that his boss had been "coercive, controlling and abusive." He attempted to sue this boss in a British court for £100m ($134m).
Moretti may now regret that endeavour. Last October, a court decided that Moretti was entitled to no more than $2k in compensation </news/junior-hedge-fund-analyst-working-hours> for procedural irregularities in his dismissal and charged him $40k in translation costs. Last week, another court decided that he also has to pay £1.6 million ($2.1 million) to the cover the legal costs of his former boss after bringing such a vexatious case with no reasonable chance of success. Much of the case was based upon secret recordings of the boss, whom Moretti said had been using anti-Semitic language. The judge, however, ruled that Moretti "“was at least in part motivated by desire to cause maximum embarrassment and potential reputational damage..."
Moretti didn't respond to a request to comment for this article. He told Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-05-15/hedge-fund-founder-leone-wins-payout-in-ex-employee-s-discrimination-case>: “The tribunal upheld my unfair dismissal claim, while most of the remaining claims were dismissed. Obviously, I am disappointed with the outcome.”
Moretti's case might be taken as a warning for hedge fund analysts with grievances. Aside from the very long hours and his boss's possible volatility, Moretti's life in employment may not have been entirely bad. He was reportedly paid <https://www.hedgeweek.com/former-dl-and-partners-exec-alleges-unfair-dismissal-and-abuse/>$2m a year and now has a 1974 Porsche 911, a £1.7m flat in London and shareholdings in 13 private companies. The tribunal said Moretti has been living in Monaco.
Moretti has nonetheless reportedly <https://www.telegraph.co.uk/business/2026/05/15/worker-falsely-accused-boss-anti-semitism-must-pay/> argued that he will not be able to pay the £1.6m. The court ruling reportedly stated that his lawyers are no longer acting for him after he was unable to pay their bills.
Separately, Brian Moynihan, the well worn CEO of Bank of America, has been reflecting upon his long tenure and what it takes to run a big bank.
Moynihan has been CEO of BofA since 2010 and seems to have little intention of moving on. Speaking to the WSJ <https://www.wsj.com/video/series/wsj-leadership-institute-leaders/brian-moynihan-playbook-for-leading-bank-of-america/D0949CE5-0E23-482E-A593-4ABAF342BB25>, he says relaxes by reading spy novels and is currently reading a great book about cod.
If you want to manage a large bank like BofA, Moynihan says it's all about process. If you're trying to make decisions about everything "you're dead."
You also need to remain "fresh and relevant." Sometimes there are issues. Sometimes you hire people who don't work out. Moynihan suggested BofA isn't doing much hiring anyway. - He noted that the bank had 285,000 people in 2010, which rose to a peak of 305,000 people and then fell back to 211,000 people. Headcount will be even lower in the future, said Moynihan. BofA has to hire 1,000 people a year to cover attrition but it's also 14,000 people internally to optimize those it already has.
Moynihan's rival is Jamie Dimon at JPMorgan. Moynihan praised Dimon but he also said "all things come to pass" when asked about JPM's superior stock performance and didn't elaborate upon whether this was a joke.
Meanwhile...
It's a fine time to be a UK M&A banker but not a UK ECM banker. The value of M&A targeting UK companies is up 250% this year at $150bn. This is the highest it's been since 2015. However, only two IPOs have been priced this year in the UK. (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-05-18/london-m-a-advisers-see-best-year-in-10-years-despite-political-flux?accessToken=eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9.eyJzb3VyY2UiOiJTdWJzY3JpYmVyR2lmdGVkQXJ0aWNsZSIsImlhdCI6MTc3OTA4NTkyOCwiZXhwIjoxNzc5NjkwNzI4LCJhcnRpY2xlSWQiOiJURU82U0tLSkg2VjYwMCIsImJjb25uZWN0SWQiOiIxQTVBMjlFRUM4OUY0NTYwQTdCNzEyM0U3OTkzQTMwRSJ9.ZgSBxPQ8kwAqExotaSUsHB7zriOx6sRbT6MYqJW1lMM>
Citi is feeling excited about expanding in the UK. It wants to be number three and is currently fifth and is spending some of the money freed up from its 'transformation' to achieve this. “We are investing in investment banking talent with private equity M&A and exits in mind - there will be more hires.” It's also moving into a new office with a beacon at the top for parties. (FN) <https://www.fnlondon.com/articles/citigroups-uk-bosses-ramp-up-ambitions-after-overhaul-c22f75b8>
Maybe Michael Grimes will not win the SpaceX IPO for Morgan Stanley. (X) <https://x.com/PolymarketMoney/status/2056417025438671251>
One moment ClearStreet was going for an IPO. </news/clear-street-s-350-engineers-may-not-be-so-rich-after-all>Now it's got a new CEO and is cutting 52 jobs, mostly in engineering. (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-05-18/clear-street-ceo-exits-firm-cuts-jobs-after-scrapped-ipo-plans?utm_medium=email&utm_source=author_alert&utm_term=260518&utm_campaign=author_19908303>
Crypto exchange Kraken cut 150 people after eliminating some AI technology that was efficient. (Bloomberg) <https://150 workers after implementing AI technology that improved efficiency, >
State Street will still open a new operating centre in Al Ain, Abu Dhabi. And Partners Capital opened its first Abu Dhabi office on Monday. (FT) <https://www.ft.com/content/d4dd2412-57f9-4973-81e1-c637e148f8d6?syn-25a6b1a6=1>
The UK is relaxing its ringfencing rules. Banks will be given “an allowance” worth up to 10 per cent of their ringfenced assets adjusted for risk and will be allowed to conduct more hedging activity through derivatives inside the entities as long as they support the "real economy." (FT) <https://www.ft.com/content/81f0698c-07d7-4d9d-9f01-0e7d0d7e0461?syn-25a6b1a6=1>
UBS trades at about 1.5 times book value — a standard valuation metric for banks — compared with 2.9 times for Morgan Stanley. (FT) <https://www.ft.com/content/19e68223-1b35-41e7-b1f2-2633f9d618db?syn-25a6b1a6=1>
Morgan Stanley hired Sebastian Bladt from JPMorgan in Germany. (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-05-18/morgan-stanley-said-to-poach-jpmorgan-s-bladt-in-german-m-a-push>
How it is when you retire from banking: "My diary resembles a productive life.... I recently spent an hour on a video call that began as an invitation to join an advisory board and somehow morphed into a pitch for me to invest in a pre-revenue start-up and help raise money for it..." (FT) <https://www.ft.com/content/dc095f2b-727a-4ee9-b8a9-75166123382f>
Iris Sun principal investor of the global flagship fund at 500 Global in Palo Alto, tries to observe and nurture founders’ soft skills by hanging out with them. She recently invited one 21-year-old founder she was about to invest in on a 4 a.m. hike to see “whether he properly showed up.” (WSJ) <https://www.wsj.com/lifestyle/careers/slow-ventures-tech-etiquette-class-20feced9?mod=hp_featst_pos5>
The pandemic was a good time for completing a Goldman Sachs internship while pregnant. "I interned as a summer associate through my first trimester, turning my Zoom camera off, throwing up, and turning it back on." (Business Insider) <https://www.businessinsider.com/goldman-sachs-wall-street-entrepreneur-dance-studio-millennial-2026-5>

item.title: Pay for hedge fund portfolio managers vs. long-only PMs in London
item.description:
If you’re a stock picker in London, you might feel like you’ve been squeezed recently. Hiring has been slow, teams are being rebuilt more than expanded, and juniors have fewer career opportunities than they, or you, can remember.
Click here to join the bubble by eFinancialCareers, our new anonymous community. <https://bubble.efinancialcareers.com/login?_gl=1*ut8n6u*_gcl_au*MTg4MDY2NTExNi4xNzYxNTU3NDIz*_ga*ODczNzI2NTA0LjE3NTM2OTk2ODI.*_ga_NZFQV4MXPM*czE3NjM2NDM0MDYkbzI1NiRnMSR0MTc2MzY0MzQzOSRqMjckbDAkaDMyMjc4OTUzMw..>✍️
Data from recruitment consultancy Octavius Finance suggests that your fears are correct - but there is still money to be made, if you know where to find it. A market update from the firm concerning pay for long-only equities professionals suggest that their pay can reach anywhere up to £600k ($800k) or more.
At this highest level, compensation is tied to the profit and loss (PnL) that professionals generate. Below it, total pay for long-only equity professionals is between £75k ($100k) and £300k ($400k), depending on seniority. “Compensation varies meaningfully by platform, fund performance, and individual contribution,” Octavius Finance said.
The most lucrative seats in long-only equity management are for European generalist and global generalist roles, Octavius Finance said. However, these seats have declined in number significantly in recent years, in part due to the increased popularity of passive investment strategies, which are increasingly squeezing the pie that stock pickers eat from. A PwC study from a few years ago suggested <https://www.pwc.com/us/en/industries/financial-services/library/mutual-fund-outlook.html> that the percentage of US equities being passively managed would rise from 44% in 2022 to 58% by 2030.
Equities professionals are aware of this, of course. There was “increased interest in switching sector coverage or moving into long/short equity strategies, alongside broader concerns around the continued growth of passive investing,” Octavius Finance said.
Pay for hedge fund </news/how-to-get-a-hedge-fund-job> portfolio managers (PMs) had the same scope. According to Octavius Finance in a market update slightly earlier in the year <“This%20has%20led%20to%20increased%20interest%20in%20switching%20sector%20coverage%20or%20moving%20into%20long/short%20equity%20strategies,%20alongside%20broader%20concerns%20around%20the%20continued%20growth%20of%20passive%20investing,”%20the%20firm%20said.>, a hedge fund professional with 10+ years of experience could expect to earn between £150k and £200k in fixed compensation, with a bonus of between 150% and 200% of base. The numbers suggest that this means compensation of up to £600k ($800k) in total compensation.
The numbers are higher at the very top of the tree, however. Hedge fund PMs are paid from their generated PnL. When we last checked </news/pay-at-hedge-fund-citadel-in-london-500k-to-21mm>, a large multi-strategy fund like Citadel paid its members (UK legal jargon for partners in a partnership) an average of $21m (£16m) each.
And, at Rokos Capital Management <https://efinancialcareers.com/news/rokos-pay>, one lucky member received £477m ($642m). But this was probably founder and CIO Chris Rokos himself. That’s how good the pay gets at the very top of the tree.

item.title: The ex-Citadel and Goldman Sachs guy offering graduates an alternative route to hedge fund jobs
item.description:
When Mark Pacitti left the UK's Newcastle University, he took a fairly formulaic route into finance. Pacitti went to Goldman Sachs </news/how-to-get-a-job-goldman-sachs> and then he went to hedge fund </news/how-to-get-a-hedge-fund-job>Citadel. </news/2022/10/how-to-get-a-job-at-citadel>Now he's doing something different.
Pacitti runs Woozle Research, a primary research platform which helps hedge funds gain expert insights into sectors they're investing in. Woozle has been in existence for a decade and now employs around 30 people split between Glasgow and London. Woozle is hiring at least 10 more people, says Pacitti. Graduates with hedge fund aspirations may have reason to apply.
"A lot of people come to us with the goal of wanting to work on the sellside or the buyside," says Pacitti (referring to banks and funds). "They take the CFA exams, which we pay for and then they often join a sellside seat as an equities analyst or go on to work for a fund."
Pacitti's people are not analysing stocks or investing capital. They are talking to people.
"Hedge funds will give us a 15 minute brief and tell us which themes they are looking at and which experts they are seeking insights from," says Pacitti. "We have a team that designs a questionnaire, interviews experts, delivers the transcripts and provides the survey data directly on the platform." For example, he says, the might speak to senior procurement people about how they're using AI software. Other places do this too (GLG Insights), but Pacitti says Woozle is cheaper.
Despite being cheaper, Pacitti says his people are paid anything from £38k to £55k, plus a bonus of between 10% and 40% based on a profit sharing plan. "They don’t need niche industry knowledge," he elaborates. "But they do need to understand business and economics."
They also need to able to persuade people to talk to them. Woozle's interviewees are paid around £200 for an hour, but they still need encouragement to talk about things. "You need to be a bit outgoing and able to make people open up on the phone," Pacitti says. This is where hedge fund analysts can be lacking - they are are good with numbers, but less adept at chatting, Pacitti suggests.
The ability to elicit facts from human beings is not one of AI's strengths, so Pacitti's people may end up safer than Citadel's PhDs. </news/paternity-leave-trading>Pacitti says "a lot" of people apply for his jobs, but presumably his acceptance rate is above the circa 0.5% at Citadel in recent years.
LinkedIn confirms that some of Pacitti's previous employees have converted their time with him into jobs elsewhere. One is now a fundamental research analyst at Point72. Another is in leveraged finance at Jefferies. One is an equity researcher at Redburn/Rothschild. It's a training program of sorts. "We train them up. It works for us," declares Pacitti.

item.title: HSBC hired a new cloud CTO from Citi as AI layoffs loom
item.description:
Cloud infrastructure isn't the hot topic that it was at the turn of the decade, </news/2021/01/cloud-computing-jobs-banking>but it's only becoming more important as banks adopt AI. HSBC, which signed an agreement with AI lab </news/the-difference-between-working-as-a-quant-in-finance-and-as-a-researcher-in-an-ai-lab> Mistral in December, <https://www.mobileworldlive.com/ai-cloud/hsbc-boosts-ai-capabilities-with-mistral-pact/> just hired a new MD from Citi </news/citi-tech-transformation-is-progressing-which-could-be-bad-news-for-citi-devs>to lead its cloud engineering teams.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Paul McMahon joined HSBC in London as its CTO head of cloud engineering. He spent two and a half years at Citi, where he was head of cloud for architecture, engineering, product management and scaled adoption. He was also previously a cloud MD at Credit Suisse.
McMahon has an unusual background. According to his profile, he has dyslexia, which meant that he "left school with little academic qualification." He spent his early career as a contract technologist at Barclays, then Cazenove. He then spent an extended period of time away from finance, which included a stint as chief cloud architect at BP.
It's not clear whether McMahon's role is newly created. HSBC has several cloud leaders already, including Mark Bryant, head of engineering for digital and cloud services.
Cloud engineers in finance told us earlier this year </news/cloud-engineer-job-security-finance>that they've been getting a lot less attention from recruiters over the past few years, but the AI race is making them more valuable. This is good news for cloud staff, but less so for up to 20,000 other HSBC employees; in March </news/hsbc-ai-job-cuts>, the bank announced plans to cut that many roles within the next three to five years using AI.

item.title: Totally-not-a-hedge fund BlueCrest added a PM to its Singapore team
item.description:
BlueCrest isn’t a hedge fund </news/how-to-get-a-hedge-fund-job>. It’s a family office, technically, for founder Mike Platt </news/bluecrest-mike-platt>. Anyway, said family office that isn’t a hedge fund hired a brand-new portfolio manager.
Click here to join the bubble by eFinancialCareers, our new anonymous community. <https://bubble.efinancialcareers.com/login?_gl=1*ut8n6u*_gcl_au*MTg4MDY2NTExNi4xNzYxNTU3NDIz*_ga*ODczNzI2NTA0LjE3NTM2OTk2ODI.*_ga_NZFQV4MXPM*czE3NjM2NDM0MDYkbzI1NiRnMSR0MTc2MzY0MzQzOSRqMjckbDAkaDMyMjc4OTUzMw..>✍️
Jeanna Lee joined BlueCrest Capital Management as a portfolio manager, based in Singapore. She joined the firm from Nomura, where she spent 8 years across its Seoul and Singapore offices, and was most recently a managing director for the Japanese bank. Lee spent 14 years at Deutsche Bank across its Singapore and Seoul offices.
Portfolio managers are known to prefer working at BlueCrest above other hedge funds as it offers 30% of generated profits as pay, versus the standard 20% seen elsewhere.
It isn’t clear how much BlueCrest pays in Singapore specifically, but it’s definitely a chunky sum. The firm’s 46 partners in London split $1.3m between themselves </news/bluecrest-london-pay>, according to the latest publicly available accounts. It’s likely that Singaporean equivalents will earn in a similar ballpark.

item.title: "The tax-free sovereign wealth fund jobs might involve more than you bargained for"
item.description:
The expat finance bargain in certain states that shall not be mentioned has long been one of the cleanest trades in global banking for the better part of two decades. Tax-free pay. Sun. A British school for the children. A flat with a balcony you actually eat dinner on for six whole months of the year. People in London talk about it the way they used to talk about Hong Kong, before Hong Kong became complicated.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Some London bankers took the trade. Gavin, not his real name, for reasons that will become clear, was one of them. He left a perfectly fine job in the City of London and moved his wife and two children to the Gulf last year. His base salary runs to US$25k a month. Tax free. There is a brand new Range Rover. Maldives for Christmas. The children’s school has a uniform, a crest and British teachers.
By every metric that Gavin's London friends use to track these things, be it Instagram, schools, cars or compensation, Gavin has won.
Gavin is also strongly considering leaving.
The job, it turns out, is not the job he was hired for. Gavin works for a sovereign wealth fund. The work, in theory, is deal origination, portfolio monitoring, fund commitments. The work, in practice, is waiting. Decisions are made in a room he is not in. His role is to be available, to nod, to produce a memo when asked, and to accept, gracefully, that the memo will sit unread until either God, or someone two levels above his boss, decides it should be read. Neither tends to be in a hurry. His boss sometimes says he is demotivated.
Gavin is in a position of indignity. Given his tax free status, Gavin's indignity is comfortable if not tolerable. He has not produced a single meaningful piece of work since joining.
Gavin's daughter is about to turn 12. She asked her father recently why some of her friends wear black dresses outside the house.
Gavin and his wife live, the local media says, in one of the safest cities in the world. This is correct. Occasionally, there are minor infractions but they feel manageable.
Then, the war started. Gavin told his son it was just fireworks. The dog was scared. On the way to the office the following morning, Gavin saw a piece of metal on the side of a roundabout. He took a picture. In the office, colleagues strongly suggested that he deleted it. One spoke about jail. Gavin was not entirely sure his friend was joking.
The fund’s strategy shifted. More local. Deal origination became deal participation in a small set of badly run national champions, on terms no London committee would clear. Gavin's expertise became surplus to requirement.
Meanwhile, Gavin's boss, who did not move from London, has mentioned in a one to one that the talent pipeline from Lebanon, Jordan, Egypt and India has never been deeper. He has also hinted that Gavin should not complain and is free to leave.
Should Gavin leave? London is not what it used to be. Singapore is crowded. New York is far away and his wife is not keen.
Either way, the school year ends in June. His daughter will certainly have to make new friends when the next academic year begins.

item.title: Morning Coffee: Deutsche Bank chief economist's paternity leave interrupted by UK chaos. Ken Griffin's new AI warning
item.description:
As has been helpfully pointed out </news/working-hours-banks-2026> to us, traders work less hard than bankers. That is, unless there are market events, in which case people interfacing with trading floors work as hard as they must. This applies irrespective of major life events.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Accordingly, the Times <https://www.thetimes.com/business/companies-markets/article/uk-bond-market-diary-gilt-trader-ttnxs8tjd>reports that people interfacing with UK gilt markets </news/london-s-top-gilts-traders> have had their plans and routines upended in the past week. It's not clear whether anyone has had to cancel their exercise regimes </news/trader-stressed-war>, but at least one person has had to follow the markets despite becoming a new father only two weeks ago.
That person is Sanjay Raja, a Deutsche Bank managing director (MD) and the German bank's chief UK economist. Two months ago, Raja noted that markets were pricing in the probability of a March rate cut. Since then, things have changed. Raja's paternity leave </news/paternity-leave-london-finance>has unfortunately coincided with a leadership challenge to the UK prime minister, amidst an ongoing oil shock and repricing of government debt <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-05-17/us-and-iran-far-from-hormuz-deal-as-drone-hits-uae-power-plant?srnd=homepage-uk>globally. It's not clear how long he was planning to take off and Raja has a fine director in the form Shreyas Gopal, but last week he was pulled back to his screens. "It feels like the bond markets have decided that there’s a strong probability we’ll have a new prime minister at some point this year so the uncertainty is when,” he informed the Times.
Raja is not the only UK market participant whose life has been permeated by the bond market volatility. April LaRusse at Insight Investments has not had a recent child, but she has been in the habit of sometimes working from home. Last week, the Times said LaRusse and her colleagues were in the office instead. They were thinking about the bad stuff. “We always think about what could go wrong. You’re never thinking about the good stuff,” La Russe said.
Although senior traders say that volumes are lower than they might otherwise be </news/keir-starmer-gilts-hedge-funds> in the gilt market after hedge funds were burned earlier in the year, Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-05-18/hedge-funds-ramp-up-bearish-sterling-bets-on-andy-burnham-risk?cmpid=051826_morningemea&utm_campaign=morningemea&utm_medium=email&utm_source=newsletter&utm_term=260518&utm_content=4578>notes that put options on sterling (weakening) against the dollar were at their highest level in two years last Thursday.
This week could be consequential. "If you’d started to see the pound get a beating, that’s a whole other level of concern," said Raja, lamenting the old days when the gilt market used to be "wonderfully predictable and quite calm."
That traditional calmness might be why one senior trader told us last week </news/london-s-top-gilts-traders> that gilt traders are not the most talented people. This week could prove him right, or wrong. It may also require LaRusse and others to be in the office. And Raja to devote part of his paternity leave to the terminal instead.
Separately, Ken Griffin at Citadel </news/2023/10/ken-griffin-citadel-113> has had a conversion that doesn't bode well for top jobs in financial services.
Speaking at the Stanford Leadership Forum, <https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Csjy_A3Kj9s&t=11s>Griffin said there has been a "step change in the productivity of the AI toolkit" which is now "profoundly more powerful than it was just nine months ago."
Griffin said Citadel has consequently unleashed "a much broader array of use cases for AI," and that it "has been really interesting watch, to be blunt, work that we would usually do with people with masters and PhDs in finance over the course of weeks or months being done by AI agents over the course of hours or days."
Griffin confessed to feeling a bit depressed by this. "These are like extraordinarily high skilled jobs being, I'm going to pick a word, automated by agentic AI," he reflected. "You could just see how this was going to have such a dramatic impact on society."
The implication is that Citadel will need fewer PhDs and Masters students in the future, and that those it has will be supercharged. Brett Caughran, <https://x.com/FundamentEdge> a former Citadel portfolio manager who now runs an analyst training firm, says <https://x.com/FundamentEdge/status/2056079527735124129?s=20> he's starting to hear senior investment professionals "think seriously about the incremental utility of junior talent."
"I may not need to hire a junior at all," is becoming a more common refrain, says Caughran. This is a mistake, he adds. Juniors need to be trained to become a "true AI-augmented investor, and at year three will drive dramatically positive ROI to the firm." Hopefully Griffin agrees.
Meanwhile...
"Just after 8am on Friday, 8 May 2015 the Credit Suisse Fixed Income trading floor in London cheered as Ed Balls lost his seat. The Conservatives had won a surprise majority in the general election. The ‘risk’ of a Labour led-coalition was eliminated. Sterling rallied. Gilt yields fell. A bond salesman came up to me waving a Union Jack..." The celebrations turned out to be very misconstrued. (HybridEconomics) <https://hybrideconomics.substack.com/p/who-governs>
Jane Street is doubling its capacity in Singapore. (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-05-17/jane-street-doubles-capacity-in-singapore-as-expansion-rolls-on>
Nik Storonsky at Revolut: “Nik was not the brightest in our group. He didn’t say too much either. But the one thing that stood out about him was that he had a tunnel view about what he wanted.” (The Times) <https://www.thetimes.com/sunday-times-rich-list/feature/article/is-nik-storonsky-revolut-ceo-the-steve-jobs-of-banking-zqz7fjt3q>
Crispin Odey invited former staff to participate in a “single question poll”. The poll asked whether respondents felt “the depiction of the company and the person of Crispin Odey in the FT article did not reflect your experience of working at Odey”. Respondents were offered only one answer: “Agree.” (Financial Times) <https://www.ft.com/content/edbe449d-fbb0-4682-8f56-557bd065c76e?syn-25a6b1a6=1>
Jassem Bu Ataba Al Zaabi is the man to know in Abu Dhabi but know one knows who he is. He is chairman of Abu Dhabi’s Department of Finance and coordinates the emirate's $2 trillion of sovereign wealth fund investments. He appears to have a dry sense of humour. (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-05-15/abu-dhabi-s-money-man-steps-into-global-dealmaking-spotlight>
BP is dismantling its pipeline gas trading team now that LNG is the focus. (Reuters) < https://www.reuters.com/business/energy/bp-cut-pipeline-gas-trading-team-lng-focus-grows-sources-say-2026-05-15/>
A technologist whose wife had twins stopped coming into the office because his commute took two hours each way on the train. A tribunal found that this was not permissible. (The Times) <https://www.thetimes.com/uk/law/article/it-managers-wfh-sex-discrimination-claim-thrown-out-t2d86pd0r>
Jonathan Alpert will join Citi in September as global head of insurance from Bank of America. Ryan Willingham is joining as an MD covering specialty finance. He's from BofA too. (AsianBankingandFinance) <https://asianbankingandfinance.net/news/citi-targets-apac-insurance-investments-new-senior-hires>
Inside the mind of Chris Hohn </news/chris-hohn>, the hedge fund manager who gives his money away.“I’m not doing this for publicity, but to see if I can encourage others to give. Our life is a gift and that means our money is a gift and we’re meant to share it.” (The Times) <https://www.thetimes.com/sunday-times-rich-list/feature/article/chris-hohn-sunday-times-giving-list-2026-nsprcb96x>
SpaceX wants an investor relations manager. This person needs to be a social media expert. (Net Interest) <https://www.netinterest.co/p/the-future-of-ir?utm_source=substack&publication_id=43559&post_id=197872244&utm_medium=email&utm_content=share&utm_campaign=email-share&triggerShare=true&isFreemail=true&r=qrn8&triedRedirect=true>
It's not easy being a nepo baby. “They’re constantly being called ‘nepo baby’. They’re never able to own their own achievements. Every one of them has been told, ‘You only got into that university because your parents made a donation.'" (The Times) <https://www.thetimes.com/sunday-times-rich-list/feature/article/the-nepo-baby-whisperer-khb38dxv2>
Nurse practitioner, is a good-paying job that is also the fastest-growing field in healthcare. (WSJ) <https://www.wsj.com/health/healthcare/nurse-practitioner-is-now-the-hottest-job-in-healthcare-a98e0bc8?mod=e2tw>
